To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been made of trends in the level of prison population.
answer_text: Northern Ireland is no different to the rest of the UK, in that there has been no respite in growth of the prison population.  The table below shows the total NI prison population on 1 April between 2022 and 2026.YearTotal20221,55220231,82220241,88620251,96420262,079That is rapid, unprecedented growth and extremely challenging for the Prison Service to manage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been made of the potential impact of prison overcrowding on (i) prison safety; and (ii) rehabilitation outcomes.
answer_text: Evidence demonstrates that a high prison population generally results in an increase in tension.  Managing prisoners, in terms of encouraging positive behaviour, becomes more challenging – for example leading to increases in incidents and violence.  Every day Prison Service staff work incredibly hard to deliver safe, decent and secure custody for everyone working and living in prison.The key to the Prison Service’s success in recent years has been a focus on purposeful activity and rehabilitation.  Population pressures also place a strain on the personal development of prisoners, the provision of learning and skills, release preparation and sociable, creative and recreational activity.  All of this leads to a deterioration in release planning and preparation, which in turn has an impact on reoffending rates, which impacts on wider society.Despite the challenges, the Prison Service continues to prioritise rehabilitation by supporting and challenging prisoners to change and address their offending behaviour while in custody, as demonstrated by recent independent inspections of Hydebank Wood College and Women’s Prison and Magilligan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether any assessment has been made of trends in the cost of imprisonment.
answer_text: Northern Ireland is no different to the rest of the UK in that there has been no respite in the growth of the prison population. The Cost Per Prisoner Place (CPPP) has been increasing year on year as set out in the table below. YearCPPP2021/22£44,8682022/23£47,9272023/24£54,0242024/25£57,180Northern Ireland Prison Service costs have increased due to the need to re-open and provide staff for previously closed accommodation to house the increasing numbers of prisoners, in addition to pay and price increases.The trend in recent years suggests that further increases are likely, and the Prison Service is planning on that basis, within the funding it has available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any estimate that has been made of the level of the prison population, over the next five years.
answer_text: Given the wide range of factors which contribute to changes in the prison population it is not possible to provide reliable estimates.  However, the trend in recent years suggests that further population increases are highly likely, and the Prison Service is planning on that basis, within the funding it has available.If there are reductions in the population, they will need to be both substantial and sustained to offer any respite to the pressure currently faced by the Prison Service.My focus will remain on ensuring that the Service has the support and resources it requires.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) an update on the current cyber threat level facing schools, including (a) the types of cyber threats encountered; and (b) any significant incidents reported, within the last six months; (ii) any measures being implemented to address and mitigate these risks; and (iii) any consideration his Department has taken on whether schools have adequate resilience and contingency planning in place to respond to a major cyber incident.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) provides the ICT infrastructure for schools (known as ‘C2k’) and has advised that: Current cyber threat level (schools) – types of threats and incidents (last six months)In common with other public and private sector organisations, the EA continues to observe a sustained and evolving cyber threat environment affecting the education sector. The most prevalent activity targeting school users and services includes phishing, malicious email attachments/links, business email compromise attempts, and opportunistic scanning of internet-facing services. Wider sector reporting also highlights ransomware and data-extortion activity as a persistent risk for education organisations. There have been no other significant incidents in the last six months.(ii) Measures implemented to address and mitigate risksC2k maintains a layered set of technical and operational controls designed to reduce risk across centrally provided services. These include, email and web security filtering; anti-malware protections; security monitoring and alerting; protective configuration and patching processes for managed services; account security controls and network protections for centrally managed infrastructure. C2k also provides guidance and communications to schools to support safe use of services and to reinforce user awareness of common threats such as phishing.  (iii) Resilience and contingency planningC2k keep resilience and continuity arrangements under ongoing review, including backup and recovery capabilities for centrally managed services and documented incident management processes. C2k continues to work with relevant stakeholders to promote preparedness and to ensure that arrangements for responding to and recovering from major cyber incidents are tested and improved over time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment that has been made of the impact of the proposed changes to the qualifying period for Indefinite Leave to Remain on international nursing staff; and (ii) any steps his Department is taking to ensure the health service can retain the nursing workforce.
answer_text: The international recruits who arrive to work here across our HSC system provide an immensely valuable contribution to the delivery of health and social care services, let alone to our communities. Given the increasing demands being placed on the health service, even with locally commissioned professional training programmes, there will always be a need for international recruitment at all levels within health and social care supported by the UK immigration system. Since 2016 there have been a total of 1,906 nurses recruited into HSC of which a total of 1,717 (90%) still remain and are working in post across Northern Ireland’s HSC Trusts today. Social care and healthcare are integrated in Northern Ireland and a large proportion of social care is delivered by independent and voluntary sector organisations. Restricting international recruitment without a fully developed, long-term workplan would place unmanageable strain on already stretched services, exacerbating workforce shortages and comprising the safety, continuity and quality of care. I fully appreciate that the proposed reforms carry substantial consequences for HSC Trusts, both for existing internationally educated colleagues, and for future attraction and workforce planning. I have raised these concerns, and a wide range of others, in response to the Government’s Immigration White Paper, to the Migration Advisory Committee (MAC) and through The Executive Office. In addition, I have stressed our concerns in correspondence with the Home Office and directly in my meetings with Minister Seema Malhotra MP, Minister for Migration and Citizenship and Minister for Equalities. Immigration is a ‘reserved matter’ and whilst we will be able to raise our local issues through the MAC ultimately the UK Government will take decisions from a national perspective.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he is taking to involve young people in the co-production of local youth services.
answer_text: My department has engaged directly with young people as part of the review of Priorities for Youth and has established a young person’s reference group as part of the review structures.  The views of young people were also sought through specifically arranged focus groups across Northern Ireland.  Young people will continue to be involved in our work including the consultation on the emerging new youth work policy.The Education Authority Youth Service has advised it implements a variety of active measures to ensure young people are genuinely involved in shaping local youth services including:youth forums and advisory groups to reflect the diversity of local young people, including those from Section 75 groups.  Young people are assigned clear roles within these groups, with agreed terms of reference and direct connections to decision-makers, allowing their perspectives to shape service planning, design, and priorities.involvement in identifying needs, setting outcomes, shaping delivery models, and evaluating success. The most recent Regional Assessment of Need included surveys of over 5,500 children and young people and 140 focus groups, involving more than 1,500 participants.engagement through workshops, peer-led sessions, digital platforms, and creative activities, ensuring participation is accessible and engaging.their role as peer mentors, youth ambassadors, or cofacilitators who may help plan weekly youth club programmes, colead group discussions, or support outreach to peers who are less engaged.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he plans to take to improve (i) universal; (ii) targeted; and (iii) specialist, speech and language and communication support in schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority Local Impact Teams (LITs) include Language and Communication Specialists, who offer tailored support for schools as well as children and young people through a graduated, needs-based approach.(i) Universal supportAt a universal level, LITs support schools to embed the Graduated Response Framework through advice and guidance, resources and professional learning. This enables schools to meet most language and communication needs through high quality teaching and inclusive classroom strategies.(ii) Targeted supportWhere additional support is required, schools can request involvement from their LIT. This may include advice, consultation, modelling of strategies and time limited interventions, tailored to the needs of the child or group and focused on building school capacity.(iii) Specialist supportFor a small number of children with complex and persistent needs, a request can be made for Specialist Provision for Speech and Language, Speech and Language Classes (SLCs). This provision is delivered in partnership with Health and Social Care Trusts HSCT), who are responsible for speech and language therapy.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps he is taking to (i) improve self-sufficiency in food production; and (ii) create a framework that (a) makes resilience progress visible; (b) builds trust; and (c) supports strategic preparedness.
answer_text: Food cuts across many departments and while there is no food production framework and I have no plans to create one, there is the Executive approved NI Food Strategy Framework and its two-year action plan. Within mission two of the Food Action plan there is a theme and actions on food security and resilience. In addition, my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme’s (SAP) overall objective is to transition to a more sustainable farming sector by seeking to implement policies and strategies that benefit our climate and environment, while, very importantly, supporting our economically and socially significant agri-food sector.SAP will also strengthen sustainable productivity, ensuring the agri food sector remains competitive while maintaining high standards of animal health, public health, disease control and food security.The Farm Sustainability Payment Scheme within the SAP is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and help withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively, encouraging businesses to become more environmentally sustainable and efficient.My officials will continue to work across departments and with officials in DEFRA and the other Devolved Administrations in this important area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any recent discussions he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure, regarding ensuring children have access to practical road safety and cycle training.
answer_text: The safety of children travelling to school is of the utmost importance. I have engaged directly with the Minister for Infrastructure and the Education Authority (EA) on road safety matters and support the work being taken forward by the Department for Infrastructure to improve road safety for children and young people.Officials in my Department, together with the EA, are contributing to the Minister for Infrastructure’s Safer Journeys to School Programme, which is considering measures to reduce risks for children travelling to and from school.The Cycling Proficiency Scheme also sits within the remit of the Minister for Infrastructure, who announced funding for its delivery in schools in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether an Equality Impact Assessment was undertaken prior to the decision to cancel special school summer schemes.
answer_text: The decision to cancel summer schemes made by the Education Authority was based on the health and safety of children and young people.  The department is not aware of any Equality Impact Assessment being undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any assessment has been made of the potential impact of the proportion of fertiliser that is imported, on food security.
answer_text: All fertiliser used by the Northern Ireland agriculture sector is imported and therefore subject to world markets conditions which are outside the control of my Department.  Nevertheless, my Officials continue to monitor fertiliser markets and are in regular contact with DEFRA.  Any impact of potential restrictions in fertiliser supplies on food production is not expected to be immediate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any steps being taken to increase the availability of supported living accommodation.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any audit has been conducted to establish (i) the number of wards; and (ii) the number of bays, not in use that could be utilised for step-down or respite care, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of ambulances out of commission, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Responsibility for the ambulance fleet rests with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), which operates across five operational divisions broadly aligned to Health and Social Care Trust boundaries: Belfast, Northern, South Eastern, Southern and Western.At present, there are no ambulances out of commission within any NIAS division. All operational divisions have a full complement of serviceable ambulances available for deployment, with additional vehicles in use to meet increased demand.The number of ambulances currently available in each division is provided in table 1.Table 1 – Number of Ambulances available by DivisionBelfast Division42 ambulancesNorthern Division73 ambulances (including 9 additional vehicles)South Eastern Division45 ambulances (including 1 additional vehicles)Southern Division39 ambulances (including 2 additional vehicles)Western Division49 ambulances (including 2 additional vehicles)NIAS has an established fleet of 234 ambulances but is currently operating 248 vehicles, reflecting a temporary increase of 14 ambulances in response to sustained service demand.As of 1 April 2026, a total of five vehicles (three emergency ambulances and two patient care service vehicles) have been withdrawn from frontline use and are awaiting disposal. These vehicles are no longer operational and do not impact service delivery.NIAS operates a five-year vehicle replacement policy. However, due to increased demand and COVID-related supply chain pressures, a small number of vehicles beyond five years of age remain in use where they are safe and reliable to do so. All ambulances retained within the fleet are fully operational, and none are designated solely as spare vehicles, ensuring flexibility to manage maintenance, unplanned downtime and contingency requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support rural and other off-grid households with the cost of heating oil.
answer_text: My Department remains firmly committed to providing practical assistance to all households with heating costs. I am working with Executive colleagues to determine how the recently announced £17.2m allocation from the UK Government may be best utilised.Existing arrangements for support include awards from Discretionary Support which may include interest free loans to help with fuel costs and, in specific circumstances, non-repayable grants for essential living expenses.Further information on the Discretionary Support Scheme can be found here:  https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/discretionary-supportI encourage anyone facing difficulties such as being unable to afford  essential heating or basic living expenses, to contact my Department’s Make the Call service. Further information on how to contact the Make the Call service can be found here: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-service The NI Energy Advice Service (NIEAS), funded by my Department and delivered by the Housing Executive, continues to offer free and impartial advice including grant referrals, heating tips and oil buying savings. Information on how they can be contacted can be found here: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/housing-help/ni-energy-advice/ni-energy-advice-get-in-touch Applications can also be made to the Affordable Warmth Scheme through NIEAS, for more information on the scheme visit https://www.nihe.gov.uk/housing-help/affordable-warmth/affordable-warmth-scheme Longer term action is set out in the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy, with a new fund launching in 2027 to support low income and vulnerable households living in the least energy efficient homes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to support households that rely on heating oil for domestic heating, following recent increases in heating oil prices linked to the conflict in Iran.
answer_text: My Department remains firmly committed to providing practical assistance to those most at risk of fuel poverty. I am working with Executive colleagues to determine how the recently announced £17.2m allocation from the UK Government may be best utilised.Existing arrangements for support include awards from Discretionary Support which may include interest free loans to help with fuel costs and, in specific circumstances, non-repayable grants for essential living expenses. Further information on the Discretionary Support Scheme can be found here:  https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/discretionary-support I encourage anyone facing immediate risks to their health or wellbeing, such as being unable to afford essential heating or basic living expenses, to contact my Department’s Make the Call service. Further information on how to contact the Make the Call service can be found here: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/contacts/make-call-service The NI Energy Advice Service (NIEAS), funded by my Department and delivered by the Housing Executive, continues to offer free and impartial advice including grant referrals, heating tips and oil buying savings. Information on how they can be contacted can be found here: https://www.nihe.gov.uk/housing-help/ni-energy-advice/ni-energy-advice-get-in-touch Applications can also be made to the Affordable Warmth Scheme through NIEAS, for more information on the scheme visit https://www.nihe.gov.uk/housing-help/affordable-warmth/affordable-warmth-scheme Longer term action is set out in the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy, with a new fund launching in 2027 to support low income and vulnerable households living in the least energy efficient homes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any recent discussions they have had with the British Government on the rising price of home heating oil.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any recent discussions they have had with Executive colleagues in relation to the rising price of home heating oil in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) any estimate her Department has made of the increase in the average price of home heating oil, since the start of the conflict with Iran; and (ii) any assessment she has made of the impact of those increases on households that rely on heating oil.
answer_text: Consumer Council figures show that since the start of the conflict involving Iran, the average retail price of home heating oil in the North has more than doubled, with the cost of a typical 500-litre delivery increasing by over 100% between late February and early April.		I am very concerned about the impact of this spike in heating oil prices on households here, particularly on lower income and vulnerable households.		The Executive has agreed to provide an additional £19.2 million in addition to the £17 million announced by the Treasury to support consumers of home heating oil in the North who are in most need. Work is being taken forward to deliver this through the Department for Communities. I am meeting regularly with the Ministers from the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero, Michael Shanks and Martin McCluskey to press for urgent additional support. 		The Consumer Council provides invaluable information for consumers by monitoring heating oil prices and publishing weekly average prices. Consumers are encouraged to use its website to compare suppliers and find information on oil buying clubs and Fuel Stamp schemes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions they have had with the British Government on the potential merits of compensating people who were unable to pay mortgages, credit card bills and other household bills, due to delays in payments from the Civil Service Pensions Scheme.
answer_text: My Department only has direct responsibility for the Civil Service Pension Scheme (NI). The delays in payments from the Civil Service Pension Scheme are connected to the scheme in Britain. The Scheme Manager for this scheme is the Cabinet Office and the Scheme Administrator, Capita, is responsible for managing the Civil Service pension arrangements from 1 December 2025.As such, my Department has had no discussions on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any estimate he has made of the number of (i) adults; and (ii) children, participating in (a) GAA; (b) soccer (c) rugby union; (d) cricket; (e) tennis; (f) boxing; (g) athletics; (h) golf; (i) gymnastics; (j) netball; (k) basketball, in 2025, broken down by gender.
answer_text: My Department holds limited information on adult participation in sport, primarily obtained through the Continuous Household Survey, with the most recent results for 2024/25 published in November 2025. These results can be found at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/experience-sport-adults-northern-ireland-202425.However, Sport NI holds more detailed information relevant to your enquiry, and its Chief Executive has advised me that:“Data held by Sport NI on sports club membership is self-reported at financial year end by National Governing Bodies in receipt of investment awards. This therefore does not reflect all those participating across Northern Ireland in the named sports.Sport NI does not currently independently verify or validate this data and age-based data is not held. Table 1 shows the membership data by gender returned for 2024/25.Table 1: 2024/25 Membership data by genderSport Female Membership 2024/25Male Membership 2024/25Total Membership 2024/25Athletics4,2304,5958,825Basketball4151,2711,686Boxing1,8017,0498,850Cricket1,3636,2847,647Football8,58064,67173,251Gaelic Games78,306102,571180,877Golf9,52238,08947,611Gymnastics13,3051,95715,262Netball2,87732,880Rugby2,27410,85213,126Tennis3,2113,5236,734Total125,884240,865366,749”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any recent discussions she has had with industry representatives, reducing regulatory burdens on small businesses.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to support small and medium-sized businesses.
answer_text: My Department is taking forward a range of measures to support SMEs, recognising their vital role in driving productivity, innovation and employment. Support is also available from InterTradeIreland and Invest NI in areas such as innovation, mentoring, export growth and financial support. Councils also provide support through their Go Succeed service which provides support to start or grow a business.The Finance Minister recently made the legislation to extend the current Small Business Rate Relief Scheme for the forthcoming rating year. The scheme which costs around £22 million provides support to almost 30,000 businesses with discounts of between 50% and 20%. This extension provides vital continuity for thousands of entrepreneurs and SMEs across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the support available to small businesses in relation to unsolicited sales approaches from third party intermediaries.
answer_text: The Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS) recognises that small businesses may experience detriment as a result of misleading or intrusive sales practices in relation to unsolicited sales approaches from thirdparty intermediaries. TSS supports businesses, particularly small and micro-businesses, by offering practical advice and assistance to help them understand trading standards regulations. The Service also provides support to traders that have been subject to misleading practices by other businesses. Any allegations of misleading practices could give rise to a breach of the Business Protection from Misleading Marketing Regulations 2008 (BPRs), enforced by TSS in the North.  If businesses need advice they should contact Businessline on 0300 077 0323, or by emailing tssbusinessline@economy-ni.gov.uk. Businessline is the Trading Standards Service’s business support helpline.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she is taking to support electronic device repair businesses.
answer_text: Through Invest NI and IntertradeIreland, my Department provides a wide range of advice and support to help businesses start, grow, innovate, export and improve productivity. Electronic device repair businesses may also be eligible for support from Go Succeed, the council led business support service, which offers practical advice and assistance to entrepreneurs and small businesses at startup and early growth stages.Repair plays a key role in advancing the transition to a more circular economy. It extends the lifespan of products in use, reduces the need for new resources, helps minimise waste, encourages innovation in product design and helps to stimulate local employment opportunities.  The Circular Economy Strategy will help support repair businesses, by positioning electronic repair as a core circular process, alongside reuse, refurbishment and remanufacture.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she is taking to create cross-departmental (i) structures; and (ii) regulations that support small and medium-sized enterprises.
answer_text: My Department works on a cross-departmental basis to ensure that the needs of small and medium-sized enterprises are considered at an early stage of policy development. Officials provide advice and guidance to departments on better regulation tools, including Regulatory Impact Assessments and the Small and Micro Business Impact Test (SaMBIT), to help ensure that impacts on SMEs are identified and reflected appropriately. In addition, Invest NI provides a wide range of advice, guidance, innovation support and access to finance for businesses, and maintains the nibusinessinfo.co.uk website, which offers comprehensive guidance and resources to support SMEs across a range of regulatory and operational areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the steps she has taken to resolve the dispute with members of the Criminal Bar Association over Crown Court legal aid fees.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the proposed consultation to extend the protected characteristics in the Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc.) Bill.
answer_text: The Phase 2 Consultation to include outstanding recommendations from Judge Marinnan’s review is currently paused until after the Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc.) Bill has passed through the Assembly. The Consultation will be progressed in the next mandate and will seek views on the Department’s proposals in response to a number of recommendations. This will include the consideration of the addition of new protected characteristics of age and sex/gender. The Consultation may also seek the views of the public on provisions for a definition of sectarianism to accompany the introduction of a statutory sectarian aggravator.The Department is continuing to progress policy development for proposals for on the addition of further protected characteristics of age and sex / gender and on provisions for a sectarian aggravator in preparation for the Phase 2 Consultation. The Department’s priority is to ensure that the most impactful provisions to strengthen hate crime legislation will be delivered in this mandate and I remain committed to delivering the best outcomes for all victims of hate crime

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assessment she has made of the benefits of providing a safe footpath connection along the Drumenny Road, Ballinderry, from Gort Road to Mullan Road; and (ii) whether she will consider prioritising this project as a rural active travel scheme.
answer_text: Regrettably, when assessed against competing priorities, this proposal did not score highly enough for inclusion in the 2025/26 works programme. The scheme will, however, be reviewed and reassessed during the development of the Rural Active Travel Delivery Plan.I am also pleased to note that footway provision fronting the Derrychrin Primary School on the Drumenny Road was provided in 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the development of the legislative provisions to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.
answer_text: As outlined during the Second Stage debate of the Criminal Justice (Sentencing etc) Bill, my Department does not have the capacity or resources to develop legislative provisions to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photographs of road traffic collisions during this mandate.  However, if the Department for Infrastructure (which has lead policy responsibility for road safety matters), the Justice Committee, or an Assembly member wish to legislate on this matter during this mandate, my Department will actively support the development of any such proposals, in accordance with its responsibility to provide advice on the introduction of any new or amended offences and penalties within Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) when a funding decision for the TinyStart programme will be made; and (ii) any assessment that has been made of the impact on children and families of a reduction in funding for the programme.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026-27 have not yet been set by the Executive, therefore, I am not in a position to provide any commitments of funding for TinyLife beyond the current financial year. As a result, no formal assessments or discussions of the impacts on children and families of any potential reduction or withdrawal of funding for the TinyStart programme has been undertaken at this stage.Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 38810/22-27, in relation to the seven inspections to the PAR Biogas Site in Tullywiggan, Cookstown, where odour was detected, to detail (i) how foul was the odour; and (ii) any remedial measures put in place to eradicate this.
answer_text: Based on the standard 0-6 odour intensity scale, authorised officers recorded odour intensities of level 2 on two occasions, level 3 on four occasions, and level 4 on one occasion.The operator did install a carbon filter to process air within the pasteurisation shed and is currently seeking advice on further odour abatement technologies.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether an Odour Management Plan has been put in place for the PAR Biogas Site in Tullywiggan, Cookstown.
answer_text: The operator has submitted documentation in relation to odour management; however, this material remains under consideration by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, regarding the February 2025 Deloitte report on its investigation of alleged contract fraud within NI Water, whether Deloitte investigated any instances of invoice slicing.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my Department that, as part of the Deloitte investigation into alleged contract fraud within NI Water, the awarding of works to contractors was fully examined to ensure that it was in accordance with the relevant procedures, based on the value of the works. This did not identify any evidence of site remedial works being split or sliced to circumvent the required procedures for the award of C3 remedial repairs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, regarding the February 2025 Deloitte report on its investigation of alleged contract fraud within NI Water, to detail who was responsible for monitoring the awarding of contracts for remedial repairs at NI Water sites between October 2022 and July 2024.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my Department that the awarding of contracts for remedial repairs at NI Water sites between October 2022 and July 2024 was overseen and monitored by their Mechanical & Electrical Management and the Commercial team.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, regarding the February 2025 Deloitte report on its investigation of alleged contract fraud within NI Water, to detail the reason why a process map for the awarding of C3 remedial repair contracts by NI Water did not exist prior to November 2023.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has informed my Department that, prior to November 2023, NI Water awarded C3 remedial repair contracts in line with established general practice that had evolved over time, rather than through formally documented processes. This approach was informed by operational effectiveness, value for money, and resource availability. A formal process map aligned with the established process was subsequently developed in response to a contractor query, to provide greater clarity and transparency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, regarding the February 2025 Deloitte report on the NI Water Investigation of Contract Fraud Allegations, to detail the number of the 41cases involving fraud allegations in which Deloitte (i) conducted a site visit; and (ii) undertook detailed examination.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my Department that, as part of the Deloitte investigation into contract fraud allegations: (i)	No site visits were conducted due to the significant limitations this presented, specifically the passage of time (often up to four years) and the operational nature of the sites. As a result, various works would have taken place on the assets in the intervening period, making it difficult to definitely determine the state of the site at the time of the alleged incompletion of work. (ii)	The 41 cases were spread across a number of different allegation areas. Deloitte examined an appropriate and proportionate number in detail for each of the allegation areas. For the most serious allegation, work paid for but not completed, Deloitte examined four out of the relevant nine sites in detail. Five out of 18 relevant sites were examined in detail regarding allegations related to newly commissioned assets. The other 14 sites raised related to workmanship issues rather than allegations of fraud. The independent Technical Review carried out complementary to the Deloitte independent financial forensic fraud investigation reviewed all 41 sites in detail.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQO 3208/22-27, to detail the number of people diagnosed with oral cancer, in each year from 2015 to date.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR), based at Queen’s University Belfast and funded by the Public Health Agency (PHA), produces the annual Official Statistics on cancer incidence, prevalence, and survival in Northern Ireland, providing vital evidence to guide decisions about cancer service.  Publications by the NICR therefore constitute the authoritative source for oral cancer incidence figures.   The NICR main website is: www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/ The most recent validated data available from the NICR covers cancer diagnoses up to and including the year 2022. This is confirmed in the Oral Cancer Official Statistics Report (2025), which specifies the oral cancer incidence annually from 1993 to 2022.  This report can be accessed at:https://www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/file-store/official-statistics-1993-2022/Oral%20cancer%20report.pdf Further summary information is also available in the Factsheet and Datasheets at:https://www.qub.ac.uk/research-centres/nicr/cancer-information/cancer-data-by-tumour-type/head-and-neck-cancer/

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total cost of (i) maintenance; (ii) repair; and (iii) any capital and revenue projects, at Holywell Hospital, Antrim, in each of the last ten years.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the delivery of the Birch Hill Centre for Mental Health.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for an NHS dental appointment for new adult patients at dental practices, in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Dental practices are independent business and therefore manage their own workloads. No information is held centrally in relation to their current waiting times for HSC appointments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of dental practices accepting new NHS patients; and (ii) the number of dental practices that have stopped accepting new NHS patients, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Dental practices manage their own workloads. No information is held centrally in relation to whether they are currently accepting new health service patients or have previously ceased accepting them. The number of dental practices, including orthodontic and specialist practices, that carry out health service dentistry in each of the last five financial years by the Local Commissioning Group (Health Trust), is shown below in Table 1. Table 1: Number of Health Service Dental Practices by Local Commissioning Group (Health Trust) in each of the last five financial years.Financial YearBelfastNorthernSouth EasternSouthernWestern2020/21190876767572021/22189876667562022/23187876667562023/24188876668552024/25187846667552025/26285836566551 At 31st March of the financial year2 At 31st December of the financial year – most recently available published official statistics.Information on the number of dental practices by Local Commissioning Group, Local Government District and Assembly Area is routinely published on a quarterly basis in the tables 7.1-7.3 from the Accredited Official Statistics publication: FPS General Dental Services Statistics Quarterly Update, with the most recent  at the following link: https://bso.hscni.net/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/dental_quarterly_tables_q3_25-26_published.xlsx

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he will take to ensure patients do not have to seek private treatment due to the level of NHS dental services availability.
answer_text: Access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland at present and the Department is aware that some patients, particularly those not currently registered with a dentist, are finding it more difficult to obtain an appointment with a Health Service dentist. General Dental Practitioners are self-employed, independent contractors. Under General Dental Services Regulations, practitioners are free to choose the amount of time they dedicate to Health Service dentistry and to make their own decisions about where to establish practices. Neither myself nor my Department can intervene in this regard.My Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to the reform of General Dental Services. An action plan and programme of work toward this reform will be in place by April 2027. While that work is progressed, consideration is being given to other actions which could be taken to improve access to Health Service dental care for both registered and unregistered patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the financial sustainability of the NHS dental remuneration model; and (ii) to detail any recent discussions he has had with the British Dental Association regarding reform of the current NHS dental remuneration model.
answer_text: I fully recognise that the General Dental Services (GDS) in Northern Ireland is under significant pressure, and work is underway to identify options for reform. In December 2024, I commissioned a Cost-of-Service review for GDS. This review is due for completion by the end of March.A GDS Reform Advisory Group is being established to provide independent expert advice and ensure that reform aligns with Departmental priorities and addresses the needs of the patient. The outworkings of this group and the Cost-of-Service review will support the development of an Action Plan in line with the timeline set out in the HSC Three-Year Plan.I met with the British Dental Association (BDA) on 22 February during which GDS reform was discussed. The BDA has also been invited to participate in the GDS Reform Advisory Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the level of access to NHS dental services in rural constituencies, including Mid Ulster; and (ii) to detail any steps that are being taken to address regional disparities.
answer_text: The General Dental Services Annual Statistics for 2024/25 show that there are 63 dentists per 100,000 resident population in Mid Ulster, which is the same as the average for the whole of Northern Ireland, and 56% of people in Mid Ulster are registered with a dentist, which is higher than the Northern Ireland average of 54%.However, it is acknowledged that access to General Dental Services is challenging across Northern Ireland at present and I am aware that some patients, particularly those not currently registered with a dentist, are finding it more difficult to obtain an appointment with a Health Service dentist.Under current General Dental Services Regulations, dental practitioners operate as self-employed, independent contractors who determine both the amount of time they dedicate to delivering Health Service care, and the location from which they provide it.To help improve access to Health Service dentistry, an additional £7 million has been invested in General Dental Services in 2025/26. The investment has allowed for the continuation of the Enhanced Child Dental Examination Scheme and a 30% enhancement on fees paid to dentists for Health Service fillings, extractions and root canal treatments. £1.6 million has also been allocated to support dental practitioners who continue to provide Health Service dental care to their patients.The Dental Access Scheme also continues to be available to unregistered patients in urgent need of treatment. The addresses and contact details of participating practices can be found at the link below.https://online.hscni.net/our-work/dental-services/dental-access-scheme/ Longer term, the Department’s Health and Social Care NI three-year plan includes a commitment to the reform of General Dental Services. An action plan and programme of work toward this reform will be in place by April 2027. While that work is progressed consideration is being given to other actions which could be taken to improve access to Health Service dental care for both registered and unregistered patients.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, in relation to the appointment process for the Commissioner for Standards, whether the successful candidate declared, or was asked about, any interests from previous work during his time with the PSNI’s Anti-Corruption Unit and Professional Standards Department.
answer_text: The Office of Commissioner for Standards was established by the Assembly Members (Independent Financial Review and Standards) Act (Northern Ireland) 2011 (“the Act”) to investigate complaints of alleged breaches of the MLA Code of Conduct. The Functioning of Government (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 subsequently extended the Commissioner’s remit to include complaints relating to alleged breaches of the Ministerial Code of Conduct.Although appointments to the Office of Commissioner for Standards do not fall within the remit of the Commissioner for Public Appointments, in the interests of good practice the appointment competition was managed in accordance with the principles set out in the Code of Practice of the Commissioner for Public Appointments. During the recruitment and selection process, applicants were required to declare whether they have any actual or potential conflicts of interest at three different stages of the process, namely on the application form, at interview and as part of the pre-appointment checks. Guidance on Integrity and Conflicts of Interest was provided to candidates.At interview candidates were also asked whether they were aware of anything in their private or professional life that would, or have, the potential to undermine their ability to maintain public trust and confidence as the Commissioner for Standards or which could, or have the potential to, bring the Commissioner for Standards into disrepute. The successful candidate did not declare any conflicts of interest or highlight issues relating to their integrity at any stage of the recruitment and selection process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all payments made to date (i) for works carried out; (ii) as a loan or easements; (iii) for compensation; and (iv) for any other payments, under the NI Water Electrical Inspection, Testing Repair Contract, C1247, since 27 May 2025.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to his previous question, AQW 33880 22-27, which sets out the position in relation to this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any safeguards his Department has in place to protect whistleblowers; and (ii) who implements and oversees those safeguards.
answer_text: Section 4 and Annex 3 to the Civil Service Standards of Conduct policy, underpinned by the Public Interest Disclosure (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, and the Civil Service Raising a Concern Policy Framework, sets out the statutory and contractual protections to ensure that civil servants can raise concerns confidentially, safely and without fear of detriment or victimisation. Key safeguards included within the Standards of Conduct policy and Raising a Concern Policy Framework include a presumption that concerns are raised in good faith; provisions for confidentiality and, where requested, anonymity; access to multiple protected disclosure routes; assurance of support and nonretaliation; and arrangements to ensure concerns are handled transparently and that appropriate feedback is provided, subject to confidentiality constraints.Responsibility for implementing and overseeing safeguards for civil servants’ rests with the NICSHR Employee Relations Director, who acts as the servicewide Nominated Officer for whistleblowing matters. At departmental level, the Designated Officer is responsible for ensuring concerns are managed appropriately and in line with policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will answer to AQW 38483/22-27.
answer_text: I answered AQW 38483/22-27 on 25 March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline her Department's long-term plan for road maintenance that focuses on pothole prevention rather than temporary short-term fixes for filling potholes.
answer_text: On 2nd December 2025 I was pleased to launch the draft Road Maintenance Strategy which seeks to improve the overall condition of the road network and enhance safety and quality, as a means of improving resilience, by delivering a targeted programme of intelligent maintenance investment and a sustainable maintenance regime.The consultation has now ended and initial indications have shown a positive response.  Officials will now take some time to consider the responses in detail and I aim to finalise the Road Maintenance Strategy as soon as possible after publication of the consultation report. I have also committed to investing £1.3 million in the provision of a ground-breaking full digital survey of the North’s entire road network. This will provide a level of detail that will allow my officials to take data-driven, efficient decisions and interventions.The data provided by this new digital stocktake will assist in taking a targeted approach to routine maintenance activities such as patching to ensure the best utilisation of scarce resources. However, it is important to recognize that these measures on their own will not address the consequences of years of under investment and I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment is made to improve the quality of our road network and contribute to the delivery of the priorities set out in the agreed Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the number of people no longer registered with a dental practice.
answer_text: I am aware that access to General Dental Services is challenging at present and I understand the frustrations felt by patients.  I know that some patients are finding it difficult to obtain registration and appointment with a Health Service dentist.  This issue has been raised with my Department a number of times.The number of people no longer registered with a dentist in Northern Ireland has increased over the past 10 years. In the financial year 2015/16 36.7% of the population were not registered with a health service dentist.The number of people not registered with a dentist in 2024/25 was 45.6%.This decrease is primarily as a result of patient’s registrations lapsing because they had not attended their practice for over two years with a smaller number having their registration actively ended by the practice.  This reflects practices decreasing their health service commitment in order to increase their private commitment.General Dental Services will require a sustained effort to ensure stability over the longer-term. The Department’s ‘Health and Social Care NI -Three-year plan’ includes a commitment to develop an action plan and programme of work for the reform of General Dental Services by April 2027.While this work progresses, consideration is being given to other actions and initiatives that can be taken to improve access to health service dental care for both registered and unregistered patients. As part of this, I have commissioned a Cost-of-Service review which will help inform the future direction of General Dental Services. I anticipate that this review will be completed by the end of this financial year.Full information on dental registrations over the past ten years can be provided to the member.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any safeguards her Department has in place to safeguard whistleblowers; and (ii) who implements and oversees those safeguards.
answer_text: (i) The Department for Infrastructure has a comprehensive framework to safeguard those who raise concerns, as set out in its Raising a Concern Operational Guidance. Staff who raise genuine concerns are protected under the Public Interest Disclosure (Northern Ireland) Order 1998, which provides statutory protection against detriment when disclosures are made in the public interest. The Department also commits to maintaining confidentiality, preventing victimisation, and securely recording and managing concerns. For nonstaff, including members of the public, contractors and agency workers, concerns are treated with the same seriousness and in strict confidence, with clear reporting channels and assurances that information will be handled sensitively, even though statutory protections apply only to workers. (ii) Oversight of these safeguards is led by the Department’s Designated Officer who is responsible for triaging concerns, determining the appropriate response, maintaining the central record of cases, and reporting to the Audit and Risk Assurance Committee and the NICS-wide Designated Officers’ Forum.The full guidance is publicly available on the Department’s website: www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/raising-concern-operational-guidance

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support that will be provided to the farming community affected by the exceptional wet weather conditions.
answer_text: At this stage, a support package for the farming community affected by the exceptional wet weather conditions is not planned, however I am continuing to monitor the situation.The SAP’s Farm Sustainability Payment Scheme is designed to improve the resilience of farm businesses and help withstand ‘shocks’ that are beyond their ability to manage effectively, encouraging businesses to become more environmentally sustainable and efficient.A Crisis Framework is being developed as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) This framework will provide a pre-defined approach for government intervention in the event of a significant crisis impacting the agricultural sector in Northern Ireland.The SAP is a strong example of climate adaptation action being progressed by my Department, as we work with and support our agricultural sector to be resilient to the more frequent and severe impacts brought by climate change. As such, delivery of the SAP is a key action in the draft Northern Ireland’s third Climate Change Adaptation Programme (NICCAP3), which my Department has developed, with input from all Departments. As a cross-cutting Programme with more than 280 actions to build climate resilience, the NICCAP3 requires Executive agreement. I am currently engaging with my Executive colleagues to seek their agreement to finalise NICCAP3 and lay it in this Assembly as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support available for homeowners to help with the installation of solar panels.
answer_text: Financial support is not currently available for homeowners who wish to install solar panels. The Windsor Framework enables the extension to the North of the VAT relief for energy saving materials that was previously available only in Britain. This means that zero rates of VAT currently apply on installation of energy-saving materials such as solar panels.Feedback from the low carbon heat consultation in 2025 suggested that combining solar panels with a heat pump could reduce overall operating costs. This option is being considered as we take forward the development of the low carbon heat and energy efficiency support scheme in 2026. In the meantime, households may still find solar panels or solar with battery storage, a worthwhile investment offering long-term savings, even in the absence of grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of potholes or surface defects that have been repaired, in each of the last four years, broken down by constituency or local council area.
answer_text: My Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.My Department regularly publishes information in relation to reports of potholes which is made available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/pothole-enquiries and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.Surface defects information created from routine safety inspections is also available at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/surface-defects.  Surface defects are categorised using a range of options, which includes pothole, rutted, depressed, cracked, crazed uneven, edge deterioration etc.  A priority setting is based on the severity and risk to the public. Lower priority surface defects may be used for future planning purposes and therefore may not be instructed for repair. The data also includes ad-hoc defects created following a site investigation of a customer reporting pothole.Information available on the Open data portal relating to data '2025 to date' is refreshed each evening from the live work management system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total number of reported potholes or surface defects, in each of the last four years, broken down by constituency or local council area.
answer_text: My Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.My Department regularly publishes information in relation to reports of potholes which is made available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/pothole-enquiries and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.Surface defects information created from routine safety inspections is also available at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/@transportni/surface-defects.  Surface defects are categorised using a range of options, which includes pothole, rutted, depressed, cracked, crazed uneven, edge deterioration etc.  A priority setting is based on the severity and risk to the public. Lower priority surface defects may be used for future planning purposes and therefore may not be instructed for repair. The data also includes ad-hoc defects created following a site investigation of a customer reporting pothole.Information available on the Open data portal relating to data '2025 to date' is refreshed each evening from the live work management system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any support available for homeowners to help with the installation of electric vehicle charge points.
answer_text: A range of grants to support the installation of EV charge points are available. Further detail can be found online at: Electric vehicle chargepoint grants - GOV.UK.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any discussions he has had with Executive colleagues in relation to cross-departmental collaboration to prevent and end homelessness.
To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on an AI strategy.
answer_text: The Office of AI and Digital, under the leadership of Professor Helen McCarthy, is preparing a comprehensive strategy for the ethical adoption of AI in the public sector.This is a collaborative effort, drawing on expertise from across government, industry and academia. The scoping phase of this work is now complete and incorporated input from a diverse range of internal and external stakeholders to shape the key priorities of the strategy. In December 2025, a draft Strategy was circulated to all Departments for feedback and comment. The draft was also shared with Government AI Network, which brings together representatives from all nine Departments.Work is currently underway to consolidate this feedback to inform a revised draft in preparation for public consultation.  Taking an iterative approach will help ensure that the strategy is robust, inclusive, and future-proof.At the heart of the strategy is a firm commitment to the ethical and responsible use of AI, grounded in the principles of transparency, respect and fairness.By embedding these principles, we are reinforcing our commitment to inclusive governance and ensuring AI solutions are developed and used in ways that are morally responsible, socially beneficial, and aligned with human values.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when they will answer AQW 37863/22-27.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 37665/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of recent figures from the Simon Community showing that over 62,000 people are officially homeless across Northern Ireland, including over 1,600 people in Mid Ulster, to detail any steps he will take to enhance homelessness prevention efforts.
answer_text: Prevention of homelessness and the prioritisation of housing solutions for those most in need is a key objective within the Housing Supply Strategy. In addition to the funding allocated to the Housing Executive for community prevention activities, this year I ringfenced an additional £2.5 million for Strategic Homelessness Prevention. That funding is currently supporting 35 organisations to deliver 42 projects in local areas. I also allocated £3 million for the New Foundations Programme to support young people leaving care and prevent them entering homelessness. My Department will continue to work closely with partners across the Executive to explore opportunities for meaningful joint working and to ensure greater collaboration and engagement at a strategic level to prevent homelessness and improve outcomes for people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of recent figures from the Simon Community showing that over 62,000 people are officially homeless across Northern Ireland, including over 1,600 people in Mid Ulster, to detail any (i) discussions; or (ii) assessments, he has undertaken regarding the introduction of a statutory duty for the prevention of homelessness for the Housing Executive.
answer_text: I support improving protection and support for people at risk of homelessness and preventing it where possible. That is why I emphasised the need to shift to prevention in the Housing Supply Strategy and the Programme for Government.There is already considerable scope within the existing statutory framework to provide additional support and prevention measures, as evidenced by the amount of work already taking place, and my focus remains on improving things now.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of recent figures from the Simon Community showing that over 62,000 people are officially homeless across Northern Ireland, including over 1,600 people in Mid Ulster, to detail any discussions he has had with Executive colleagues regarding cross-departmental collaboration, to prevent and end homelessness.
answer_text: I regularly engage with Executive colleagues and stress the need for greater collaboration to prevent homelessness. Prevention and intervention to reduce housing stress and prioritise housing solutions for those most in need is a key objective within the Housing Supply Strategy I brought to the Executive. Along with the Minister for Health I have also launched the New Foundations project to help prevent young people leaving care entering homelessness.My Department will continue to work closely with partners across the Executive to explore opportunities for meaningful joint working and to ensure greater collaboration and engagement at a strategic level to prevent homelessness and improve outcomes for people.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail the amount paid to date for the hire of scaffolding on Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: Scaffolding was erected at the rear of Parliament Buildings and in Courtyards 1, 2 and 3 to prepare for the upcoming remedial works and to address health and safety concerns within the areas that require access.  The total paid for scaffolding hire for the Rear Parapet and Courtyards 1, 2 and 3 up to 31 December 2025 is £235,513.  The remedial works for these areas have now completed a tender process and will be awarded in February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any road safety measures, including provision of a roundabout, that can be taken at the junction of the Hillhead and Oldtown Roads, Castledawson.
answer_text: On 2 February 2026, my Department commenced work to resurface 3.7km of Hillhead Road, between Old Town Road junction and Harmony Hill.  This work is scheduled for completion on the 21 February, subject to favourable weather conditions.Officials will continue to monitor this location to determine if any other safety interventions are required. As the provision of a roundabout at this location would require significant investment, it will be considered as part of future works programmes subject to available funding and other competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions she has had with the Minister of Justice, regarding legislation to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.
answer_text: On 26th January, Minister Long and I facilitated a roundtable discussion with Road Victim Support Northern Ireland – Donegal, to consider appropriate measures to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.   I am pleased to say that a proposed approach to address the issue focussing on education, awareness raising and legislative options has been identified; the legislative strand of which Minister Long has agreed to lead on.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any steps she has taken towards introducing legislation to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.
answer_text: Following the Assembly debate on 8 December 2025, Minister Kimmins and I met with families and representatives campaigning for legislation to prevent the unauthorised recording and sharing of photos of road traffic collisions.  It was clear that the immense trauma that these families have faced in losing a loved one was further compounded by the circulation of distressing images on social media.I consider that the upcoming Victims and Witnesses Bill, which I plan to introduce to the Assembly in late Spring, may provide a suitable legislative vehicle for such provision.  Whilst my Department would not have the capacity to consult on these matters, I am content that my officials could support and work with the Committee to enable a Committee amendment, if Members were minded to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the steps she has taken to resolve the dispute with members of the Criminal Bar Association over Crown Court legal aid fees.

To ask the Minister of Finance, following the re-valuation of non-domestic properties, to detail any assessment he has made of the impact of increased business rates liability in 2026, on the viability of the hospitality sector.
answer_text: On 29 January 2026, I announced that I have stopped the Reval 2026 process.I refer the member to my statement on the matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when foster care allowances will be updated for the 2025-26 financial year; and (ii) when, and how, this information will be communicated to fostering families.
answer_text: The allowances paid to foster carers in Northern Ireland (NI) are for costs associated with day to day care; and are outlined within the annual published Fostering Model Scheme. The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis in respect of the decision for an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. The current published allowance rates are 2024-25 rates, and these include the annual uplift increase of 3% which was applied to foster care allowance rates for the 2024-25 financial year. The current allowances rates are published on the Department of Health website and are available to view at Foster care allowances Foster care allowances | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk). Consideration of an annual uplift to apply to allowance rates payable for 2025-26 is currently under review, and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplift to rates for 2025-26 will be backdated to April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail when there will be an increase to foster care allowances, to bring them into parity with those paid in England, Scotland and Wales, and closer to the level recommended by the Fostering Network.
answer_text: The allowances paid to foster carers in Northern Ireland (NI) are for costs associated with day to day care; and are outlined within the annual published Fostering Model Scheme. The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis in respect of the decision for an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. The current published allowance rates are 2024-25 rates, and these include the annual uplift increase of 3% which was applied to foster care allowance rates for the 2024-25 financial year. The current allowances rates are published on the Department of Health website and are available to view at Foster care allowances Foster care allowances | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk). Consideration of an annual uplift to apply to allowance rates payable for 2025-26 is currently under review, and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplift to rates for 2025-26 will be backdated to April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 38168/22-27, to detail (i) any engagement he has had with the Minister of Justice regarding the introduction of an aggravated offence for attacks on assistance dogs; and (ii) any actions he is taking to introduce an aggravated offence for attacks on assistance dogs.
answer_text: I recognise the role assistance dogs play in their owners’ lives and the lasting harm that can result from an attack.  I believe there is merit in making attacks on assistance dogs a specific, aggravated offence and my officials have explored the case for doing so.  This reform would require an amendment to primary legislation but unfortunately my capacity to enact legislation of this type has been constrained by this truncated mandate.Nevertheless, the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983, as amended, includes provisions which make it an offence for an individual’s dog to attack and injure an animal owned by another person.  An attack on an assistance dog would currently be dealt with under this offence.  I continue to focus on delivering the priorities outlined within my Animal Welfare Pathway.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
