question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any action he will take to mitigate the impact of the UK Government’s decision to impose a 64% cut to the Shared Prosperity Fund on the voluntary and community sector in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This poor outcome is the result of a succession of bad decisions by this, and the previous, British Government. The Shared Prosperity Fund failed to replace vital EU funds after Brexit. And now matters have been made even worse with this 64% reduction, due to the shift towards a mainly Capital profile. As well as my ongoing and substantial engagement with British Government Ministers on this issue, I brought the matter to the Executive on 22 January, after which the Executive jointly wrote to the Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government, Steve Reed, detailing the devastating impact this latest decision will have. With one voice, the Executive called on Mr Reed to work with his Ministerial colleagues to immediately rectify the profile of the Local Growth Fund here with a more favourable Resource allocation. The responsibly to fix this rests squarely with the British Government. I will continue to press Westminster to urgently change its approach and amend the profiling of the Local Growth Fund to one that better reflects need here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of (i) primary; and (ii) post-primary, school children who paid (a) full; or (b) subsidised, fees for instrument tuition provided by the Education Authority, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that it does not hold the information requested, for the years 2021 - 2026.Until the 2025-26 academic year, the administration of remission policies, in respect of reduced music tuition charges for Free School Meal Entitled (FSME) pupils, was largely undertaken at school level, meaning that EA does not hold the data centrally.From the commencement of the 2025-26 academic year, the EA has implemented a revised Remissions Policy, in respect of EA Music Service charges. This policy provides for 70% remission of all Music Service charges, in respect of FSME pupils. The collection of final data from schools, regarding numbers of FSME pupils availing of Music Service tuition in 2025-26, is not yet complete and as a result, it is not yet possible to provide final data on the number of pupils receiving remission due to FSME. Full data for 2025/26 will be available by the end of March 2026.In addition to the tuition provided within schools, EA Music Service also provides advanced or specialist tuition in Music Service Hubs, after school hours. This service is available to a smaller number of pupils.In the 2025/26 year:30 primary pupils receive instrumental tuition through EA Music Service Hubs. 10 of these pupils pay reduced charges, due to the EA Music Service Remissions Policy. 195 postprimary pupils receive instrumental tuition through EA Music Service Hubs. 28 of these pupils pay reduced charges, due to the EA Music Service Remissions Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she will take to protect young people online.
answer_text: As Justice Minister, I am committed to doing everything possible to keep our young people safe from harm and protect them from all forms of abuse and exploitation.  I am very aware that social media and the use of the internet in general is increasingly central to young people's lives and can have a very positive effect in terms of communication and learning opportunities, however, it also presents serious risks, particularly around grooming, exploitation and abuse.While telecommunications, including the making of associated legislation, regulation and oversight is a reserved matter under the remit of the UK Government, the responsibility to keep our children safe online is one we all share.  This shared commitment is reflected in the Northern Ireland Executive’s Online Safety Strategy and Action Plan which aims to empower children, young people, parents, carers and professionals to recognise, prevent and respond to online abuse.  My department plays an active role in this work with officials contributing to the cross-departmental implementation group responsible for driving forward the delivery of the strategy and ensuring its actions are effectively carried out.I am committed to progressing legislative reform where I can, to help protect our children and young people from harms associated with online behaviour caused by ever increasing advances in technology.  There is already a comprehensive and robust legislative framework in place in Northern Ireland to address a wide range of sexual offending related behaviours which apply both online and offline.  This includes new offences I brought forward in the last mandate through the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022.  However, I continue to look for opportunities where I can strengthen the law further.I am extending a range of child sexual abuse and exploitation protections of the Westminster Crime and Policing Bill to Northern Ireland, subject to the approval of the Assembly.  This includes enhancing the existing possession of a paedophile manual offence, strengthening the sex offender notification requirements and introducing a new offence to address the use of image generating tools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of the Lough Neagh Action Plan.
answer_text: Of the 37 actions within the Plan, 15 have been delivered and 22 are actively progressing. I remain committed to driving forward the delivery of the remaining actions at pace.Progress to date reflects coordinated efforts across key themes including scientific research and innovation, enhanced monitoring and enforcement, education and stakeholder engagement, regulatory reform, and strategic communications. The recovery of Lough Neagh will require sustained, long-term action.  Given the depth and complexity of the problem, substantial recovery will take many years, if not decades.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he will take to improve support for young adults with recognised learning difficulties and complex needs, in rural areas.
answer_text: My Department recognises the particular challenges faced by young adults with learning difficulties and complex needs in rural areas. Currently across all Health and Social Care Trusts there is no specific Programme of Care to support people with learning difficulties, presenting as distinct to individuals with a diagnosed learning disability. Across each Trust, adults with a diagnosed learning disability receive support and service provision from Learning Disability Services.Health and Social Care support for people with learning difficulties, is currently determined by their specific presenting needs. Where individuals have complex needs that relate to healthcare, physical disability or sensory needs, the Physical and Sensory Disability Services situated within the region’s Health and Social Care Trusts are positioned to assess and provide the individual with the relevant supports e.g. equipment, social care, signposting.  In a similar vein, individuals presenting with Mental Health Needs may access support from Community Mental Health Teams. Health and Social Care Trusts plan and deliver services in line with local population needs and Section 75 equality requirements, including consideration of rural impact, to ensure the most effective use of available resources. It is acknowledged that young adults with learning difficulties and complex needs can experience challenges in terms of continuity of care provision when entering Adult Services. While Trusts will often consider how individual needs may be best met under ‘Best Fit’ principles, it can be challenging to meet the ongoing social care needs of these individuals when they do not meet access thresholds for existing statutory support services such as Learning Disability, Mental Health or Physical & Sensory Disability Services.  Consideration is required across the Health and Social Care system to clarify existing care pathways and determine how these individual’s needs should be best met on a regionally consistent basis. Ensuring that young people in rural areas receive consistent and equitable health and social care support remains a priority for my Department.A key priority within the Autism Strategy is improving regional pathways of care. In support of this, my Department recently consulted on the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework. The Framework proposes an inclusive, needs-based approach to neurodiversity which does not require a formal diagnosis as a prerequisite for support. This model aims to reduce stigma, promote earlier intervention, and improve outcomes across health, education, and developmental domains. My Department is currently considering consultation responses and is working with colleagues in the Public Health Agency and Child Health to define the requirements that will underpin this pathway and inform future commissioning.To strengthen the focus on adult autism pathways, a dedicated Project Lead for Adult Autism was appointed in Febr

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Deloitte and Mott MacDonald were procured to investigate fraud within NI Water.
answer_text: NI Water has advised that it used existing NI Water contracts to procure Deloitte to investigate allegations of fraud and Mott MacDonald to carry out technical reviews. For any future queries relating to operational matters in NI Water, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 36710/22-27, (i) whether legal advice on the payment £108,786.78 by NI Water of contractor liabilities under TUPE, was sought by the Board of NI Water; and (ii) whether the decision to approve the £108,786.78 by the Board of NI Water, was made unanimously.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that (i) yes, legal advice was sought; and (ii) the Board was advised and approved this payment. The decision was unanimous and approved by all members in attendance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail a timeline for the completion of work on Maydown Bridge, Benburb, including a date for reopening.
answer_text: In the interest of public safety, my Department took the decision to close a short section of the Maydown Road in Benburb following the partial collapse of a retaining wall and section of carriageway during Storm Amy.Due to the collapse exposing and undermining several utility ducts and cables, repairs to the wall and carriageway could not begin until all services were safely decommissioned and diverted. I am pleased to confirm that works to repair the wall and carriageway started in November and are progressing on site as planned.  Subject to favorable weather conditions, the road is expected to reopen to traffic by mid-March.I fully appreciate the impact of the closure and apologise to the local community, including businesses in the area, for the inconvenience caused. Local diversions are in place, and I urge the travelling public to drive with due care and attention and allow extra time for their journeys.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35836/22-27, to detail (i) whether legislation to prohibit overtaking of school buses at bus stops will be introduced in this mandate; and (ii) a timetable for the introduction of this legislation
answer_text: I have established a Safer Journeys to School Programme within the Department to examine options to improve safety for school journeys, including legislative options. This work is evidence-led to ensure interventions are effective and proportionate.Officials are working at pace to explore a range of practical operational and behavioural interventions.  As part of this work, the Programme is examining the feasibility and potential impact of legislative measures, including a possible statutory prohibition on overtaking a stationary school bus. There is no confirmed timetable for legislation at this stage.The Department remains committed to improving road safety and will continue to engage with stakeholders to deliver the most effective measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he plans to take to improve services for the diagnosis of, and support for, adults with ADHD.
answer_text: The Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability in an extremely challenging financial environment.The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. The final ADHD needs assessment report was received by the Department at the end of November 2025.  The report is currently under consideration, with a view to identifying next steps and arranging publication thereafter. It is anticipated that the report will be published in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any work her Department has planned for further enhancement to the A6 road.
answer_text: The A6 Phase 2 Derry to Dungiven scheme is part of the A6 Executive Flagship project and will deliver 5.3km of new high-quality dual-carriageway from the existing terminus of the A6 dual-carriageway at Drumahoe to the A2 at Gransha, and an upgrade of the existing A2 dual-carriageway from Caw to Maydown.As you will be aware, part of the works for this scheme, between Caw and Drumahoe, will encroach on the illegal waste site at Mobuoy. I formally approved the department’s response to the recent public consultation on the Draft Remediation Strategy which closed on 2 October 2025  however, we must await finalisation of the DAERA strategy and the associated timeline for its implementation.  Once this is confirmed my officials can determine the most efficient delivery mechanism for the progression of the scheme.My Department is also considering the provision of a signalised junction at Dales Corner, Active Travel improvements between Drumahoe and Altnagelvin, the upgrading of the Variable Message Signage at the Foyle Bridge and the removal of the old Park and Ride site at Drumahoe, with expected delivery in 2026/27 alongside the routine monitoring of the road network and resurfacing where required.Longer term considerations include the construction of a grade separated junction at the A6 /A29 junction near Maghera.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) legal firms held on retention by NI Water; (ii) retention fees paid to those firms; and (iii) reason each firm has been retained by NI Water, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised that it currently retains 3 legal firms under contract, these being:-CMS Camerson McKenna Nabarro Olswang LLP;A & L Goodbody Northern Ireland LLP; andDAC Beachcroft LLP.NI Water has advised that it does not pay retention fees to any of these firms.The firms have been retained for the last 5 years under Contract C1046, which expires in March 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, regarding the introduction of an aggravated offence for attacks on assistance dogs, within the forthcoming Sentencing Bill.
answer_text: Legislation regarding the welfare of animals is the policy responsibility of Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) and the introduction of an offence of this nature would be the responsibility of that Department.  I have not had any engagement with the Minister for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, regarding the introduction of a new aggravated offence for attacks on assistance dogs.Executive approval for the content of my Sentencing Bill was granted in December 2024 and the introduction of an aggravated offence for attacks on assistance dogs was not considered for inclusion. However, my Department, in conjunction with DAERA, will be reviewing existing animal welfare offences and penalties as part of my Department’s Review of Sentencing Policy launched earlier this month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration she has given to the introduction of an aggravated offence for attacks on assistance dogs, within her forthcoming Sentencing Bill.
answer_text: I refer the Member to my response to AQW 38168 22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs) under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, carried out by his Department and its arm's-length bodies, of policies, strategies, and services affecting rural communities; and (ii) a breakdown of (a) the number of; and (b) which, strategies and services that were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) Over the past five years (2019-2024), my Department and its arm’s-length bodies have completed a total of 62 Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs).The following table presents an annual breakdown of the total number of RNIAs completed by DoF and its arm’s-length bodies.Year (1 April – 31 March)Number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments completed by DoF and its arm’s-length bodies2019/202062020/202172021/2022222022/2023122023/202415RNIAs completed by my Department and its arm’slength bodies are published annually by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) within the Rural Needs Annual Monitoring Reports. These reports are publicly available at:https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/publications/rural-needs-annual-monitoring-reports (ii) The Act does not require public authorities to assess enhancements as a consequence of the RNIA. Therefore, my Department does not hold information on the number of, and which, strategies and services were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.RNIAs do, however, form an integral part of the policy development process and help ensure that rural needs are considered across all relevant strategies and services. My Department meets its statutory obligations under:Section 1(1) of the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act), which places a statutory duty on public authorities listed in the Schedule to the Act to have due regard to rural needs when developing, adopting, implementing and revising policies, strategies and plans and when designing and delivering public services; andSection 3(1) of the Act, which places a duty on public authorities to monitor and report on activity under the Act and requires my Department to compile information on the exercise of its functions under section 1 of the Act and send that information to DAERA who collate and publish an annual report containing the information and lay this report before the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) the number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs) under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, carried out by their Department and its arm's-length bodies, of policies, strategies, and services affecting rural communities; and (ii) a breakdown of (a) the number of; and (b) which, strategies and services that were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs) under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, carried out by her Department and its arm's-length bodies, of policies, strategies, and services affecting rural communities; and (ii) a breakdown of (a) the number of; and (b) which, strategies and services that were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In accordance with the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, my Department has due regard to the social and economic needs of people in rural areas when developing, adopting, implementing or revising policies, strategies and plans, and when designing and delivering public services. The Department also fulfils its statutory duty to monitor and report under section 3(1) of the Act.	The Department for the Economy and its arm’slength bodies listed in the Schedule to the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 complete Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs) and submit an annual monitoring return to DAERA, which publishes these in its Rural Needs Annual Monitoring Reports.The total number of Departmental RNIA including ALB’s is 126.A breakdown of the total number of RNIAs from 2019-20 to 2023-24 are:Year 1 Apr – 31 Mar)Number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments2019/2020202020/2021362021/2022242022/2023292023/202417 The Act does not require public authorities to assess or report enhancements arising from RNIAs, nor is there a statutory basis for providing a breakdown of such enhancements. Accordingly, neither the Department nor DAERA compiles this information. The Department meets its obligations under sections 1(1) (due regard) and 3(1) (monitoring and reporting). Nonetheless, the RNIA process forms an integral part of policy development. Where rural needs are identified, they inform the design or refinement of policies, strategies and services. The Department will continue to comply fully with the Act and provide annual monitoring information to DAERA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs) under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, carried out by her Department and its arm's-length bodies, of policies, strategies, and services affecting rural communities; and (ii) a breakdown of (a) the number of; and (b) which, strategies and services that were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department complies with its statutory obligations under the Act - namely Section 1(1) and Section 3(1) of the Act to pay due regard to rural needs when developing, adopting, implementing or revising policies, strategies and plans, and designing and delivering public services and to monitor and report annually to the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. The Act does not require public authorities to assess enhancements of strategies and services.The table below outlines the Rural Needs Assessment screenings carried out on policies, strategies and delivery of public services over the last five years. As my Department’s arms length bodies were not included in the Rural Needs Act 2016 Schedule of Public Authorities they are not included in this table.  2020/212021/222022/232023/242024/25513172318

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs) under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, carried out by his Department and its arm's-length bodies, of policies, strategies, and services affecting rural communities; and (ii) a breakdown of (a) the number of; and (b) which, strategies and services that were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 undertaken by the Department of Education and its arm’s length bodies in each of the last five years is as follows:1 April 2020 to 31 March 2021171 April 2021 to 31 March 2022281 April 2022 to 31 March 2023351 April 2023 to 31 March 2024461 April 2024 to 31 March 202579The Department complies with its statutory obligations under the Act specifically the due regard to rural needs as outlined in Section 1(1) and with regard to Section 3(1) which requires public authorities to monitor and report. In doing so the Department ensures that the needs of people in rural areas are considered in developing robust policy and in the implementation of relevant strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs) under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, carried out by her Department and its arm's-length bodies, of policies, strategies, and services affecting rural communities; and (ii) a breakdown of (a) the number of; and (b) which, strategies and services that were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWER The Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is the only arm’s length body of the Department of Justice (DoJ) that is subject to the requirements of the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016.   Since April 2020, the number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments of policies, strategies, and services affecting rural communities that have been carried out by the DoJ and PSNI is as follows: YearDoJPSNI2019-209352020-2110252021-2210362022-2312382023-241631Further information on the Rural Needs Impact Assessments are included in annual reports published by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.  The reports include details of how there has been due regard to rural needs when developing, adopting, implementing or revising policies, strategies or plans.  There has been no formal assessment of which strategies or services have been enhanced as a consequence of the Rural Needs Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs) under the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016, carried out by his Department and its arm's-length bodies, of policies, strategies, and services affecting rural communities; and (ii) a breakdown of (a) the number of; and (b) which, strategies and services that were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) Over the past five years, my Department and its bodies have completed a total of 158 Rural Needs Impact Assessments (RNIAs). The following table presents an annual breakdown of the total number of RNIAs completed by DAERA. Year (1 Apr – 31 Mar)Number of Rural Needs Impact Assessments completed by DAERA 2019/2020422020/2021432021/2022242022/2023252023/202424(ii) Section 1(1) of the Rural Needs Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 (the Act) places a statutory duty on public authorities listed in the Schedule to the Act to have due regard to rural needs when developing, adopting, implementing and revising policies, strategies and plans and when designing and delivering public services. Section 3(2) of the Act requires my Department to collate and publish an annual report containing the information sent to it by public authorities on the exercise of its functions under section 1 of the Act and lay this report before the Assembly. The statutory duties under the Act do not confer the power to my Department to evaluate the information provided in the RNIAs and therefore my Department does not hold information on the number of, and which policies, strategies and services were enhanced as a consequence of the RNIA, in each of the last five years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that the most recent official statistics data currently available from the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry is from 2022, to detail how the Public Health Agency (i) investigate; and (ii) respond to, reports of more recent incidences of geographically concentrated rises in childhood cancers.
answer_text: When concerns regarding cancer occurrence or incidence are raised by members of the public or healthcare professionals, the Public Health Agency (PHA) engages with the relevant party to determine the basis of the concern, the circumstances in which it arose, and to define the scope of the issue as a foundation for any investigation and formal data analysis.The PHA collaborates with the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) to utilise routinely available data in order to determine incidences of specific cancers, including any year-to-year variations. Consideration is also given to demographic factors and population changes, such as overall population size and age distribution, as these variables influence cancer rates within a population.When comparing cancer incidence across different geographic areas, population groups, or time periods, it is essential to apply consistent methodologies to quality-assured data from each source. This ensures alignment with historical data and enables valid comparisons with other regions beyond the immediate scope of concern. Selective data collection may introduce bias and distort comparisons.The Northern Ireland Cancer Registry produces the official cancer statistics for Northern Ireland and undertakes rigorous processes to ensure that all cancer cases are accurately recorded and categorised by type, incidence, and stage. Consequently, there is an inherent delay between diagnosis and inclusion in the official registry statistics.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rationale as to why the most recent official statistics data available from the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) is from 2022; and (ii) any steps that can be taken to reduce the time taken on NICR official outputs.
answer_text: Official statistics from the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) have been published up to 2022. Cancer registry data requires substantial time to collect, process, and undergo quality assurance. To achieve high levels of data completeness, cancer registries cross validate data from multiple sources such as hospital administrative data and death registrations. These sources undergo clinical coding by staff, a process that can take several months before NICR receives the data. At present, NICR does not have validated data beyond 2022.Once NICR receives data, a team of skilled Cancer Intelligence Officers (CIOs) process it in line with cancer registration rules and guidance from the European Network of Cancer Registries. Standardisation across cancer registries enables national and international comparability. Importantly, each release of NICR official statistics contains comprehensive information not only on cancer incidence in Northern Ireland but also, unlike some other registries, on cancer stage at diagnosis, mortality, survival and prevalence. Cancer survival data is important when assessing the impact of cancer control measures, while cancer stage is vital when monitoring trends in early cancer diagnoses and assessing efforts to improve this.  NICR has a consistently high proportion of cancer staging across the UK and Ireland with over 80% of malignant tumours staged. It takes a significant amount of time for CIOs to determine and assign tumour staging in line with American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) guidance and involves using a range of clinical data, including pathology and radiology information. Producing cancer data at this level of detail and quality takes time. NICR continues to explore ways to improve timeliness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the adoption of the Gallion housing estate, Moneymore.
answer_text: Officials advise that the Gallion housing development has been taken forward in a series of phases, with phase 1, 2 and 3 which include Gallion Heights, Gallion View and Gallion Way already adopted into the public road network.Phase 4 and 5 which include Gallion Close and Gallion Drive are currently under construction. I understand that many of the houses are completed, with the roads and footways completed to base course, street lights installed and the development has recently achieved NI Water approval. The adoption of sites is a developer-led process requiring the developer to submit a request for a preliminary certificate.  Until the remaining infrastructure works are completed the responsibility for the site remains with the developer. My officials continue to encourage the developer to complete the works and bring the site to an adoptable standard.Once the sites are completed to an adoptable standard and a preliminary certificate issued, there will be a 12-month maintenance period before my Department formally adopts the road infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to letters issued by the Education Authority to private taxi operators for school pupils' home to school transport service, stating that contracts would be terminated and re-tendered unless operators agree to reduce their invoiced rates by 10 per cent, for his assessment of whether (i) this was a reasonable request given rising fuel prices and operating costs; (ii) these changes place undue pressure on operators and drivers; and (iii) these changes would be a breach of contract by the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of home to school transport, which includes the procurement and management of contracts held with private taxi operators. Due to the scale of the anticipated shortfall in the EA’s overall budget this year, it has had to make challenging decisions aimed at reducing costs while maintaining essential services. The EA’s request of taxi operators to voluntarily reduce their invoiced rates by 10% followed an in-depth analysis of current contracts, benchmarking cost per mile against the maximum fare rates for metered taxi transport, which are set out in legislation and were updated in December 2023 to reflect rising costs in the taxi industry. This analysis identified significant variations in fare rates between operators and whilst DfI maximum fare rates for metered journeys form a benchmark only as they do not apply to EA contracts, many operators are charging significantly more than the referenced rates. (i) I would like to acknowledge that taxi operators play a vital role in delivering home to school transport for some of our most vulnerable children. However, the rising cost of taxi transport is a significant concern for my Department and the EA. In writing to all taxi operators, the EA is seeking their cooperation in reducing costs to protect the transport service and ensure that as many pupils as possible can access transport to school. The EA assures me it is taking a balanced and considered approach in reviewing the responses received from taxi operators. (ii) The EA acknowledges the concerns raised by operators and drivers and it is committed to working closely with them. It informs me that early engagement has been positive with a number of operators contacting EA with savings proposals. (iii) The EA has confirmed that it has not breached any contract in making this request and no contracts have been terminated to date. The terms and conditions of contracts under the Dynamic Purchasing System allow the Authority to terminate contracts if necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement her Department has had with the PSNI, over the last five years, in respect of adding calibres to the banded system to make the firearms licensing system more efficient, by reducing the administrative burden on the PSNI's Firearms and Explosives Branch.
answer_text: My officials in Firearms and Explosives Branch have regular engagement with their counterparts in PSNI’s Firearms and Explosives Branch on firearms related matters of mutual interest, including discussions on the current banded system and suggestions that have been put forward by interested parties for amendments.Following a request via the Justice Committee in 2020, my officials requested PSNI’s views on the proposed changes to the banded system.  At that time PSNI wished to conduct an operational review of the operation of the banded system.  Further discussions took place in December 2024 and in early 2025.  PSNI has indicated support for some of the changes proposed, but further discussion on the detail will be required.  Any formal proposals for change will require public consultation and legislation to be brought forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures that have been put in place at the Children’s Haematology and Oncology Unit in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children, given the extra pressures of flu with two fewer beds on the ward.
answer_text: Colleagues at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) have confirmed that seasonal flu has increased demand for inpatient care across all areas of the hospital, creating additional pressure on bed availability. The Children’s Haematology / Oncology inpatient unit has the ability to flex up and utilise the two inpatient beds to meet service demand for this specific cohort of patients who require Haematology/Oncology inpatient care, as is required. The flexibility is supported by robust contingency measures including the redeployment of staff from other areas when required, ongoing close monitoring of staff sickness, continuous review of patient flow across all clinical areas, discharge planning to optimise bed availability and the use of the site-wide escalation plan. This ongoing approach ensures safe care delivery remains the highest priority.The Children’s Haematology ward and the Children’s Haematology Outpatients department operate as a whole unit, providing support and cross cover of nursing staff between the two departments, as required, to safely deliver care. Using a risk assessed approach, the 10 bedded ward has temporarily reduced to eight inpatient beds, with the two remaining beds currently being used for day case chemotherapy cases. Using a day case model allows for optimal utilisation of skilled nursing staff across both areas. Any child who it is determined requires an in-patient bed, following Clinician-led triage, is admitted to an appropriate bed, based on clinical need, in line with the normal site-wide escalation plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any timeline for resurfacing measures to repair the Battery Road, from Ardboe to Coagh.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.  In the interim, officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

NI Water has advised my department that the detail you are seeking is as follows: (i) 	(a) The Purchase Orders were raised by a Senior Manager (Level 3) in NI Water; (b) Two Purchase Orders of £41,021.00 and £67,765.78 were approved by NI Water’s CEO.(ii) 	The Payments were made on 9 and 25 September 2025 respectively.(iii) 	Approval to make these payments was granted by the NI Water Board.(iv) 	The Payments covered the full employment costs for the Protected Employees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps his Department has taken to create safeguards for farmers who cannot avail of the Farm Sustainability Payment, particularly the mushroom industry.
answer_text: For those farm businesses, including those within the mushroom sector, that cannot avail of FSP there are other schemes within my Department’s Sustainable Agriculture Programme that are relevant. The first strand of the Farming with Nature Package, the Farming with Nature Transition Scheme, launched in June 2025, and producers with a Category 1 Farm Business ID, including mushroom growers, are eligible to apply for financial support to implement environmental actions on agricultural land.  It is planned that the Scheme will open again for applications in 2026.The Horticulture Pilot Scheme, which has recently launched includes a bespoke range of support measures, tailored for the specific needs of this sector. It will provide focused knowledge transfer, innovation, supply chain and capital support to the production horticulture industry through three schemes: Sustainable Sector Growth Groups, Innovation Drivers and Support, and a Growers’ Training and Support Pilot Scheme.The Sustainable Farming Investment Scheme will provide financial support to help primary producers with a Category 1 Farm Business ID, including mushroom growers, to improve business efficiency and environmental performance. It is planned that the scheme will be available from early 2026.Supply Chain schemes are being developed for NI agriculture and horticulture sectors, including the mushroom industry. The aim of these schemes is to encourage and facilitate improved collaboration between producers and growers within the supply chain, encouraging them to innovate, problem-solve and value-add beyond what they can achieve in isolation. 	In March 2025, I also confirmed that the Fruit and Vegetable Aid Scheme will continue in Northern Ireland until a suitable successor, co-designed with the horticulture sector is in place. Under the scheme, my Department will provide match-funding for recognised NI Producer Organisations undertaking an approved 3-year operational programme, including those operating in the mushroom industry.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the two-child limit in the Universal Credit Child Element being removed from April 2026, whether his Department has assessed any potential impacts on Universal Credit awards.
answer_text: I welcome the decision of the UK Government to remove the Universal Credit two-child limit from April 2026.I am committed to tackling child poverty, and this reform is a significant and positive step in helping to meet that objective.Removing this restriction will lift thousands of children out of poverty and support families who have faced unnecessary hardship under the previous rules.Statistics from my Department show that in August 2025 there were 17,600 children that were not eligible for the child element of Universal Credit because of the limits imposed by the two-child limit.My officials are undertaking further analytical work to determine the wider impact of this change.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Public Health Agency or the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry has made any request to his Department since June 2025 for environmental monitoring data on air, water and soil in relation to the reported increase in childhood cancers in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: Since June 2025, neither the Public Health Agency (PHA) nor the Northern Ireland Cancer Registry (NICR) has requested environmental monitoring data on air, water, or soil from the Department of Health in relation to childhood cancer occurrence in Mid Ulster.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timetable for the introduction of legislation that prohibits overtaking of school buses at bus stops.
answer_text: A team within my Department is examining options to improve safety for school journeys, including potential legislation. This work is evidence-led to ensure interventions are effective and proportionate. Officials are progressing at pace with the aim of bringing forward recommendations by early to mid-2026.The Department remains committed to improving road safety and will continue to engage with stakeholders to deliver the most effective measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to fund a real Living Wage for all home care workers.
answer_text: The Health and Social Care system in Northern Ireland continues to face unprecedented financial pressures. My Department is managing a funding gap of approximately £615 million, which includes around £200 million for HSC pay.  		My Department has been driving efficiencies and savings to reduce the financial deficit as far as possible with a target to deliver some £300 million savings internally and seek help from the Executive to cover the remaining £300 million.		The Executive have recently approved my Ministerial Direction to commit up to £209 million for pay uplifts to staff groups covered by the Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Body and the NHS Pay Review Body. 		I remain fully committed to resolving pay issues across the sector and to funding the RLW at the earliest affordable opportunity. Unfortunately, I do not currently have the capacity within my budget to deliver this commitment in the current financial year. It will, however, be a priority for me in planning expenditure for 2026–27. When this uplift becomes possible, I intend to ensure that funding is allocated transparently, with clear timing and ringfencing for the pay uplift.		I recognise this will be disappointing for many but my officials will continue to engage with independent sector organisations, which are integral to the delivery of care across Health and Social Care services and the wider programme of social care reform.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures her Department is taking to increase capacity at the Loup Wastewater Treatment Works.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:During the PC21 period, NI Water plans to undertake enhanced base maintenance work to improve the efficient operation of the works and ensure that the works can comply with the Water Order Consent issued by NIEA.This programme of work aims to maintain compliance and improve efficiency but will not increase design capacity. Completion of these works, combined with enhanced monitoring, will help NI Water to treat flows up to the current design capacity and gather data to inform any future capital upgrades.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the timeline for repairs to the Bann Bridge, Kilrea; and (ii) any measures her Department is taking to alleviate the volume of traffic being diverted via the Portglenone Bridge.
answer_text: My Department’s Contractor commenced works on site at Kilrea Bridge on the 10 November. The permanent solution requires specialist drilling equipment to install ‘tie-bars’ between the two approach walls beneath the existing road surface. This is a complex and time consuming process with limited scope for acceleration and will take 14 weeks to complete the work.  However, officials are taking forward a temporary solution to stabilise the wall whilst the main repairs are being carried out which will allow the road to reopen to cars and light vehicles in early December, subject to suitable weather conditions.  I acknowledge the road closure and subsequent diversion has led to an increase in traffic in Portglenone, and assure you my officials are actively monitoring the situation. My Department has installed additional signage in areas such as Ballymoney, Garvagh, and Ballymena to provide motorists with early notice of the closure, advise of potential delays, and encourage the use of alternative routes where possible. Officials have also engaged with the PSNI to explore measures aimed at reducing congestion within Portglenone.I fully appreciate the impact of the ongoing closure and the understandable frustration of the local community, including businesses in the area and thank them for their patience while we work to deliver a safe and effective solution.  We will keep the local communities and elected representatives up to date on the timeline throughout the works.  My Department will continue to provide updates on the repair works on our website at: Kilrea Bridge, B64 Bann Road, Kilrea – Closure

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of members of the Board of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression who are competent in the Irish language.
answer_text: Testing in Irish Language proficiency did not form part of the selection criteria for the appointment of the members of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression, as detailed in the Candidate Information Booklet.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 33880/22-27, for a a detailed breakdown, of the £108,786.78 expended under, any other payments.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that the detailed breakdown of the £108,786.78 expended is as follows: JulyAugustSeptemberTotalAdmin Support£3,855.18£3,855.18£3,855.18£11,565.54Contract Manager£5,797.52£5,797.52£63,910.60£75,505.64Quality Manager£3,461.85£3,461.85£6,923.70Engineers£7,395.95£7,395.95£14,791.90Total£20,510.50£20,510.50£67,765.78£108,786.78NI Water has further advised that the expenditure in the above table includes a redundancy payment of £63,910.60 for a protected employee, with the remaining costs relating to gap in service salary costs for other protected employees. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any steps her Department is taking to address job vacancies caused by a skills shortage.
answer_text: The latest economic evidence shows that the local labour market continues to remain challenging with an annual undersupply of 5,400 workers, unemployment relatively low at 2.6%, economic inactivity remaining high at 26.9% and many sectors reporting recruitment challenges. However, the last Employer Skills Survey provides some encouragement with a 10-point percentage fall in skills shortage vacancies from 2024.  We must work with employers and sectors to support a culture of lifelong learning and to invest in skills to address current shortages and meet future challenges.Skills shortage is an issue that I am committed to addressing.  My Department has created a Skills Fund, investing in a range of training interventions focused on growth sectors and transversal skills and I have launched our Skills Action plan which sets out the key actions that we will focus on to ensure that everyone has a pathway to enhance their existing skills and contribute to my Economic Vision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans she has for the provision of electric vehicle charge points in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: The provision and operation of both Electric Vehicles and publicly accessible Electric Vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity in the North.  Responsibility for the maintenance, development and utilisation of charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you on the position of their current installations.There has been significant investment from CPOs in EV infrastructure over the last two years. The commercial publicly accessible charge point network now almost 700 chargers in place here, a growth in EV charging provision of 75% since April 2023. The Rapid/Ultra Rapid charging public network, which offers the fastest, most convenient method of charging has grown by over 400% here in the same period. Local Councils have availed of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’. This project, part funded by my Department and WEEV, is set to install up to 250 charge points across the North.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) their Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: The Executive Office (TEO) and its Arm's-Length Bodies (ALBs) have made use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) by utilising Microsoft Copilot Chat and M365 Copilot.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) his Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: My Department uses Microsoft Copilot, an AI assistant which can be accessed as part of Office 365. It is used to align with my Department’s digital transformation goals by enhancing productivity, improving consistency, and supporting staff across the Department. In addition, my Department’s Mail Opening Unit employs an AI powered Strategic Scanning Solution, to assist with classifying incoming benefit-related correspondence. All Arm’s Length Bodies sponsored by my Department have access to and use Microsoft Copilot, except for Foras na Gaeilge, the Local Government Staff Commission, the Northern Ireland Commissioner for Children and Young People, the Ulster-Scots Agency, and Ulster Supported Employment Limited. The Arm’s Length Bodies which use additional AI to Copilot are detailed below:Department for Communities Arm’s Length BodiesALB NameAI ProductUseDevelopmentArmagh Observatory Planetarium (AOP)Chat-GPT Github-CopilotFor the efficient developing of scientific codesNILGenerative AI tools (e.g. Canva, Dall.E)To prepare education resources and promotional materialNILMachine Learning Research Tools (e.g. Python packages like TensorFlow / PyTorch, SKlearn)To build custom AI tools as part of astrophysics research. E.g. Build specific neural networks for clustering/ generationPart of scientific researchLibraries NIService Now (Fujitsu)Helpdesk SupportNILNorthern Ireland Housing Executive(NIHE)Supplier: Microsoft Tool: Fabric Uses AI to simplify data analysis, automate tasks, and improve decision-making.NILSupplier: MRI System: Income AnalyticsPredictive Analytics: The system uses data to predict which residents are most likely to fall into rent arrears, enabling early intervention.NILSupplier: Enterprise RPAProduct: UCV SaaS Solution The system deploys robot(s) to interact with the Department for Work and Pensions Portal, Housing Management, and Finance systems, to check and tenancy details automatically.  NILSupplier: BT/CISCO System: Unified Communications system For development purposes.Proof of concept underway to investigate the use of AI to enhance collaboration, customer experience, and IT operations.Supplier: Asprey Asset Management IT System  For development purposes.Review how AI could be used to streamline the processing of compliance documents using Optical Character Recognition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) his Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: (a) Department of Finance(i) Use of Artificial IntelligenceThe Department of Finance has utilised Artificial Intelligence (AI) in several areas to improve efficiency and support service delivery:AI-powered productivity tools have been deployed to provide real-time assistance for tasks such as drafting documents, summarising meetings, analysing data, and streamlining communications. These tools help civil servants generate content, identify action items, and automate repetitive processes while maintaining departmental security and privacy standards.Officials are currently exploring the use of AI to support and promote learning and development activities. This includes exploring how AI can assist in creating learning content and materials and how it can be utilised to increase awareness and engagement with learning opportunities across NICS.Additionally, AI coding assistants have been used to help developers become more efficient in writing code, quickly identifying and rectifying errors, and increasing productivity.Teams are increasingly using modern programming languages and tools to process and present statistical data. Staff have leveraged AI to assist in translating code from legacy systems into languages such as R, SQL, HTML, and JavaScript in line with the NISRA Reproducible Analytical Pipelines (RAP) Strategy. Beyond migration, AI has supported the development of new functionality, improvements to existing processes, and the creation of interactive, user-focused outputs that are accessible and mobile-friendly.(ii) Development of Artificial Intelligence products:The Department is currently designing and implementing a new cloud-based technology solution (Oracle Fusion) which will incorporate AI components to support Finance and HR delivery.(b) Arm’s-Length Bodies: Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB)(i) Use of Artificial Intelligence Microsoft Copilot is used to assist staff with administrative tasks.(ii) Development of Artificial Intelligence products:Testing is underway to develop an AI chatbot on the SEUPB website to assist with PEACE PLUS Programme queries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) her Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: The DepartmentMy Department has adopted a DoJ policy on the use of AI which follows an internal approval process involving technical experts and the Data Protection Officer as well as oversight from the Department’s Senior Information Risk Owner. This ensures a full assessment of the risk is carried out before any business areas proceeds with a consideration to use AI for a task in the Department. My officials have started to evaluate using Microsoft Co-Pilot to aid with tasks such as background research, summarising complex documents, preparation of document templates and prioritisation of emails.Although not a Department of Justice Programme, The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) is hosted from within my Department.  EPPOC has been exploring the use of AI to enhance efficiency and support strategic delivery.  This work involves development of a Proof of Concept to demonstrate how AI can automate the extraction of management information from project returns for a management dashboard. ALB’sThe Probation Board (PBNI), Criminal Justice Inspectorate, and Policing Board are all evaluating the use of Microsoft Co-Pilot to aid administrative tasks.In PSNI use of AI is limited and focused on supporting administrative efficiency rather than operational decisions. This includes work on Voice-to-Text transcription, the review of harmful digital material, and video analytics to assist in evidence assessment.All the ALB’s using AI have processes in place to oversee the governance and assurance on the use of artificial intelligence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) her Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: (a) My Department has successfully completed a structured 12-week trial of Microsoft Copilot within Microsoft 365. This pilot focused on practical applications such as document summarisation, drafting, and data analysis.Following the successful pilot, the Department is progressing plans for a phased rollout of Copilot across all business areas to embed AI-driven productivity tools. The rollout will ensure staff have access to training and support.My Department is actively supporting AI development through strategic investments and partnerships. In collaboration with Invest NI, we launched the Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre (AICC), a £16.3 million initiative delivered by Queen’s University Belfast and Ulster University. The AICC is providing support to SMEs through its Business Transformer Programme to drive AI adoption and awareness.(b) The main Artificial Intelligence software product used by our arm’s length bodies is Microsoft Copilot.Further Education colleges also use education-focused platforms. As of October 2025, the FE colleges have endorsed the use of LearnWise (to deliver personalised support for students) and TeacherMatic (to improve efficiency for educators). In addition, the FE Colleges are reviewing a range of AI products including Notebook LM (a research and writing assistant from Google).The Labour Relations Agency (LRA) is working in partnership with the Ulster University and Talk Terms to develop a Holiday Pay tool using AI technologies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) his Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: (a) (i)	My Department uses artificial intelligence (AI) products in several ways, including, for example, to enhance staff productivity in areas such as drafting meeting notes and summarising reports; provide coding assistance for IT developers; automating documentary checks; carrying out remote sensing analysis on satellite, orthophotography and LiDAR imagery for environmental monitoring purposes; and undertaking predictive analysis and modelling for facilities development.(ii)	My Department is also investigating ways to make further use of AI products in the areas outlined above, including through the development of machine learning solutions that can support improved service delivery and data analytics. To ensure responsible use of AI, all potential use cases must have a risk assessment carried out, and approval from the DAERA Senior Information Risk Owner.(b) (i)	I am advised by my Department’s arm’s length bodies that the use of AI is not currently prevalent although there is some use of Generative AI to enhance staff productivity in areas such as drafting emails or meeting notes and summarising reports, as well as some AI assisted data analysis and image classification. (ii)	Turning to plans for development, I understand that the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) will be testing Generative AI technology in several research and corporate functions over the next number of months. It also has ambitions to use other types of AI technology in upcoming research projects to assist processing and analysis of data and supporting and enabling delivery of AFBI’s Science Strategy 2030.  AFBI is also currently reviewing an AI Usage policy which will be in place before further deployment of AI tools.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) her Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure has made available Microsoft Copilot Chat, an enterprise level secure AI assistant, to all staff and a more advanced separately licensed Microsoft Copilot for 365 AI assistant as a pilot for some senior staff. The Department has also commenced several proof of concept exercises using AI Chatbot technology using departmental data and information to train the AI Chatbots to assist staff in responding to queries or providing advice. Evaluation of these exercises are ongoing. Driver and Vehicle Agency are currently trialling an AI solution to capture, record and analyse customer queries.The Department is also working closely with other areas of the NICS to develop NICS wide uses for AI to deliver economies of scale and shared learning in this area.Within Arm’s Length Bodies, NI Water and Translink have also deployed Microsoft Copilot and are using commercial off the shelf products such as chatbots, cyber security tools and data analytics solutions that have embedded AI functions. Waterways Ireland does not currently use AI tools but is expecting to start a deployment in 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) her Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI), and Generative AI in particular, presents significant opportunities to enhance both the productivity of the existing Health and Social Care (HSC) workforce and the quality of care delivered to service users.  Potential benefits include freeing up staff capacity through improved communication tools and the development of personalised treatment plans.The Department of Health’s Digital Strategy for Health and Social Care underpins this transformation, aiming to modernise service delivery through digital innovation. The development of a single electronic care record for every citizen, improving visibility and coordination of care across the system.AI is already being applied in several clinical and operational areas with upcoming plans include testing AI algorithms to support referral and decision-making processes, scheduling and acquisition, reporting and triage, and the generation of alerts for results acknowledgement. However, these capabilities must be carefully balanced against potential risks particularly in relation to clinical efficacy and information governance. It is essential that any AI tools deployed are trustworthy and uphold the privacy and rights of patients and service users.The successful adoption of advanced technologies such as AI and Generative AI depends on several key enablers:High-quality, codified data that accurately reflects service users’ needs and historical treatments, which is essential for training AI models.Robust clinical safety and ethical governance frameworks to oversee the safe and responsible introduction of AI tools.Specialist skills in cloud engineering and cybersecurity, recognising that the computational demands of AI are often best met through cloud-based infrastructure.Successful adoption of AI depends on high-quality data, strong governance, and cloud engineering skills. The HSC AI Steering Group and departmental working groups are actively reviewing opportunities and risks to ensure safe, responsible AI use for Northern Ireland citizens.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the (i) use; and (ii) development, by (a) her Department; and (b) its arm's-length bodies, of Artificial Intelligence products.
answer_text: The use and development of Artificial Intelligence (AI), such as Microsoft (MS) Copilot to support departmental business is currently under consideration by my department. A range of purposes are being considered, including the analysis of consultation responses, the summation of lengthy published and unpublished reports, researching policy across jurisdictions and legislative interpretations, assisting the preparation of minutes of meetings, reviewing and revising departmental policies, procedures and guidelines, amongst other administrative tasks. The use of AI in my department, is subject to staff oversight and follows the guidance in the NICS AI Policy – Securing Maximising the Benefits of Artificial Intelligence. In schools, a specific example of AI under development is the RAISE Programme, which provides schools with an opportunity to see how AI tools can support literacy. The Reading with AI Research Project will assess the effectiveness of evidence-based AI literacy tools on improving reading outcomes. While CCMS, CnaG, GTCNI report that they are yet to consider how AI might support business practice, NICIE and MCA are using AI products, such as MS Copilot to support business efficiencies, CCEA are considering how generative AI can be used in areas such as multimedia and graphic design and the EA reports it is developing an AI strategy to guide adoption.EA is also working with schools on the effective use of two Generative AI tools MS Copilot and Google Gemini and EdIS.C2k has been leading a strategic and operational programme of work focused on the safe, ethical, and impactful integration of Generative AI across the education sector in Northern Ireland, with staff across EA now using MS Copilot to support productivity, collaboration, and innovation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31950/22-27 and AQW 32366/22-27, for her assessment on whether NI Water's whistleblowing policy is aligned with the Northern Ireland Civil Service Raising a Concern Policy Framework.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information:NI Water has assured my department that the NI Water Speak Up – Raising a Concern (Whistleblowing) Policy was developed in line with legislation and best practice guidance in terms of the Public Interest Disclosure Order (Northern Ireland) and Northern Ireland Audit Office guidance. NI Water has further advised that the policy is aligned with the NICS policy requirements and it was reviewed and approved by the NI Water Audit Committee at its meeting on 19 September 2025. The NICS Raising a Concern Policy Framework was considered as part of this standard review and the NI Water Audit Committee was informed of any amendments made to align the NI Water Speak Up – Raising a Concern (Whistleblowing) Policy with the NICS Raising a Concern Policy Framework. The NI Water Audit Committee informs the NI Water Board of its activities. The policy is also reviewed when there are any changes in whistleblowing legislation or guidance. In addition, it is NI Water’s internal practice to review the policy every two years. This is rooted in good practice and is not a legislative requirement.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.
