question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement her Department has had with stakeholders in relation to the contribution of the legal profession to the sustainability of the legal aid service.
answer_text: Sustainability of legal aid services is dependent upon the availability of quality legal services at the point of need.  While this is not a matter for my Department alone, I recognise that proper, proportionate remuneration contributes to ensuring an enduring profession and effective access to justice.Last year I announced a 16% increase in criminal, civil and family legal aid fees, and a range of other measures to support the sustainability of the profession.In March 2025, I approved the establishment of a Criminal Legal Aid Working Group, independently chaired by His Honour Judge Burgess, to develop advisory proposals to ensure the fee structure is sustainable, resilient and consistent with the changing landscape of the criminal justice system.  As part of that process, my officials have had extensive and ongoing engagement with representatives from the Criminal Bar Association and the Solicitors Criminal Bar Association to develop and progress proposals aimed at securing the viability of the legal professions, sustainability of the legal aid service and access to justice for citizens.In parallel, my Department will launch public consultations on the scope of legal aid, merits testing and financial eligibility. These consultations have been shaped by an initial targeted Call for Evidence and the extensive stakeholder engagement undertaken through the Foundational Review and are designed to give further opportunities for stakeholders to contribute views and evidence, helping to ensure that legal aid remains sustainable, targeted, and responsive to need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she is taking to ensure that the independence of the legal profession and judiciary will be maintained, following the implementation of reforms of the legal aid system.
answer_text: As Minister of Justice, I am deeply committed to safeguarding the independence of the judiciary. In progressing reforms to the legal aid system, I have been clear that judicial independence must not be compromised.As part of the Enabling Access to Justice Reform Programme Delivery Plan, the Department intends to launch a public consultation on a number of reform opportunities relating to the provision of legal services. This consultation will provide a platform for meaningful engagement with stakeholders, including the Lady Chief Justice and members of the Judiciary, to ensure that the proposed reforms are informed by a broad range of perspectives and expertise. The Department is committed to a transparent and collaborative approach, recognising that the success of these reforms depends on the insights and experiences of those who work within and rely on the justice system.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline when the language Commissioners will be in post.
answer_text: We are pleased to confirm that the recruitment process for the Irish Language Commissioner and the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition, as well as the Director and five other members of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression, is now complete.Dr Katy Radford has been appointed as the Director of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression (OICE), Pól Deeds as the Irish Language Commissioner, and Lee Reynolds as the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.They will take up their posts on 13 November, for a period of five years.Brian Dougherty, Ellen Finlay, Stephen Moore, Dr Callie Persic and Dr Jacqueline Witherow have also been appointed as members of the OICE Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she is taking to ensure that reform of the legal aid system does not result in unmet need and gaps in the provision of legal aid services.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the importance of ensuring that reform of the legal aid system does not result in unmet need or gaps in service provision. The Enabling Access to Justice reform programme, which I announced in December 2024, is designed to safeguard access to high-quality legal advice and representation for those who need it most.The reform agenda includes proposals to expand eligibility for legal aid, trial alternative dispute resolution models to reduce reliance on court proceedings, and undertake reviews of fee structures to ensure the system is both efficient and financially sustainable. These measures are intended to enhance frontline service provision and ensure that legal aid remains accessible. My Department will shortly launch public consultations on the scope of legal aid, merits testing, and financial eligibility. These consultations are intended to provide stakeholders with further opportunities to contribute views and evidence, helping to ensure that legal aid remains sustainable, targeted, and responsive to need.To prevent gaps in provision, the Department has taken a holistic approach to reform, considering the impact across criminal, civil, and family law services. We are working to ensure that changes in one area do not inadvertently undermine access in another. Extensive stakeholder engagement has been a cornerstone of the reform process. The Department has conducted detailed consultations with the legal profession, civil society organisations, and service users. This includes a call for evidence as part of the Foundational Review of Civil Legal Aid, the Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid led by His Honour Judge Burgess, and a priority-setting exercise for civil legal aid for victims of domestic abuse. These engagements have helped identify areas of unmet need and informed the development of targeted interventions.I have also met with representatives from the Law Society and the Bar of Northern Ireland to discuss concerns around service sustainability and access to justice. I will continue this engagement to ensure that the representative bodies of these valued public service providers have the opportunity to shape the reform programme and help the Department to remain responsive to emerging challenges. My Department will continue to monitor the impact of reforms closely and engage with stakeholders to ensure that legal aid services remain responsive to the needs of the public.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she is taking to ensure that reform of the legal aid policies and payments system will attract and retain an increased diversity of practitioners, to provide legal aid services.
answer_text: His Honor Tom Burgess CBE led an independent review of criminal legal aid. This holistic review of the structure and associated remuneration levels of the criminal legal aid framework resulted in several proposals including a 16% uplift to fees which I also committed to grant to civil and family legal work.Additionally, increases to remuneration for police station advice and parole matters are being progressed along with early payment of disbursements, interim payments and a refresher fee pilot to aid the financial viability of smaller firms and career progression particularly for women and younger members of the professions.Furthermore, officials are advancing enhanced payment for travelling time and mileage rates. This will particularly support the viability of legal representatives working in more rural areas of Northern Ireland, where travel is often a bigger part of the job. The aim is that this will sustain the availability of legal practices in the towns and on the high streets, providing citizens with access to justice.By enhancing legal aid remuneration rates, the Department are making criminal, civil and family legal work more attractive. Proper, proportionate remuneration is critical to ensure an enduring profession and effective access to justice. The necessary steps to deliver increased legal aid fees are being progressed by officials across the department including finalising the business case which also covers the wider Enabling Access to Justice Delivery Programme.It must be acknowledged that a robust and enduring profession is not solely contingent on fees alone and that legal aid is only one income stream available to practitioners. The legal professions must also be proactive in supporting and encouraging more people to enter this area to ensure long-term viability. It is not solely a matter for the Department.Professional bodies need to ensure there is a proactive approach taken to succession planning. They must ensure that their training models build capacity, support inclusivity and nurture talent. The professions have already taken some steps in that regard.Work is continuing with the professional bodies and local associations to identify whether there are any other actions that will ensure the continued availability of high-quality advice and representation. A commitment to progressing extended rights of audience to increase capacity has already been made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how she will ensure that (i) any new delivery models for legal aid services will be financially sustainable; and (ii) the current regulatory and consumer protections will continue to apply to those services.
answer_text: The Department of Justice is committed to ensuring that any new delivery models for legal aid services are both financially sustainable and subject to robust regulatory and consumer protections. These commitments are central to the Department’s wider reform programme, Enabling Access to Justice, which seeks to modernise legal aid provision while safeguarding access, quality, and accountability.The reform programme is informed by several key reviews and consultations. The Fundamental Review of Criminal Legal Aid, led by His Honour Judge Tom Burgess, examined the structure and sustainability of criminal legal aid, including remuneration and delivery models. This review was underpinned by extensive stakeholder engagement, including consultation with the judiciary, the Bar of Northern Ireland, the Law Society, and legal aid practitioners. The Foundational Review of Civil Legal Services included a wide-ranging call for evidence, engaging legal professionals, civil society organisations, young people, and individuals with lived experience of accessing legal aid. This process helped identify barriers to access and informed the development of reform priorities that reflect the needs of diverse service users.The Department of Justice has proposed an uplift in legal aid fees following the recommendations of the Burgess Review. The Department has also implemented operational improvements, including a 58% reduction in payment processing times by the Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland, which has improved cash flow for providers and enhanced system sustainability.With regard to regulatory and consumer protections, the Department will ensure that any new delivery models continue to be governed by the existing statutory framework and oversight mechanisms. The Legal Services Agency Northern Ireland will retain responsibility for enforcing eligibility checks, fraud prevention, and quality assurance. The Department remains committed to transparency, accountability, and meaningful stakeholder engagement throughout the reform process. Future reforms will be co-designed with service users and providers to ensure that they are inclusive, effective, and aligned with the principles of access to justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment he has made of the benefits of introducing measures to encourage the use of Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil in domestic oil-heating systems.
answer_text: As Minister for the Department for Communities, I have overall responsibility for the Residential Buildings sector chapter within the draft Northern Ireland Climate Action Plan.  However, while the chapter contains policies and proposals to reduce emissions within the Residential Buildings sector, many of these fall to other Departments to deliver.  The question of assessing the benefits of introducing measures to encourage the use of Hydrotreated Vegetable Oil in domestic oil-heating systems, would be for the Minister for the Economy to answer, as this falls within her remit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment she has made of the benefits of introducing measures to encourage the use of hydrotreated vegetable oil in domestic oil-heating systems.
answer_text: Earlier this year, following a period of stakeholder engagement, my Department carried out call for evidence to assess the role liquid biofuels, including HVO, could play in moving households and businesses away from fossil fuels for heating. This work is progressing through the final stages of analysis and evaluation prior to publication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the overall expenditure figures for legal aid services, along with the associated number of cases granted legal aid, in each of the last 10 years.
answer_text: Table 1 presents total annual expenditure for legal aid in Northern Ireland alongside the number of cases granted in each year since 2015/16.When interpreting the information in Table 1, it should be noted that statistics covering legal aid grants and expenditure within any given time period are not directly comparable and should be viewed as independent figures. As legal aid cases can last for several years, payments made in one year can and will reflect work undertaken from legal aid applications granted in previous years. Similarly, not all legal aid cases granted in any given year will have been completed or resulted in a payment being made during that same time period.Table 1: Legal Aid Expenditure and Cases Granted, 2015/16 to 2024/25Financial YearExpenditure (£000’s)Grants2015/1691,18197,1852016/1782,32884,9242017/1879,23278,6802018/1984,23080,4992019/2082,09874,2982020/2175,21464,7752021/2294,71668,9622022/23101,29863,9762023/24113,50063,8812024/25119,73161,573Source: Legal Services Agency (LSA)Notes:Grants reflect ‘Acts of Assistance’ for the earlier years 2015/16 to 2018/19. These figures constitute management information, derived from Phoenix (LSA’s previous case management system), and were calculated on a legal aid ‘certificate’ basis. Equivalent figures from 2019/20 onwards are Official Statistics, derived from LSA’s new, digital case management system (LAMS) and are calculated at a case level, reflecting the post-transformation environment. As multiple certificates could be granted on a legal aid case, acts of assistance automatically tend to be higher. Accordingly, total grants from 2019/20 onwards are not directly comparable with earlier years.Grants of Exceptional Funding (22 cases in 2024/25) are included from 2019/20 onwards, following the introduction of LAMS. While LSA adjudicate on civil applications, criminal legal aid is a judicial function and is granted by the Courts. The decrease in grants from 2022/23 is evident within civil advice cases and reflects more stringent verification of capital threshold as part of the financial eligibility criteria for civil legal services, as prescribed by The Civil Legal Services (Financial) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015. While there has been no change to the regulations, the LSA identified a body of applications for individuals whose capital is in excess of the prescribed threshold for advice and assistance. These applicants do not meet the financial eligibility test and as such the applications have been refused.As part of the Enabling Access to Justice Delivery Plan, financial eligibility for civil legal services (as per Note 4) will be looked at.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps she is taking to reduce the average length of time, that Legal Aid practitioners are waiting to receive payment from her Department, for bills submitted for services provided.
answer_text: The table below indicates the payment times in August 2023 which the Law Society and Bar referred to in pre proceedings correspondence to my Department on legal aid payment times October 2023. The table also sets out the current payment timescales. You will note that the timescales have reduced significantly.Weeks to Process 95%August 2023October 2025Advice and Assistance132Criminal Legal Aid144Civil Representation Higher Courts94Civil Representation Lower Courts188I have consistently argued that my Department’s budget must recognise the demand-led work of the Department. We are more than £200 million worse off than if my budget had merely kept in line with inflation. Despite that, in 2025/26 I was able to approve the highest opening budget in the history of legal aid. This has contributed to the transformation in payment times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any discussions her Department is currently undertaking with the Criminal Bar Association, to fully restore legal aid services.
answer_text: My officials continue to engage directly and extensively with the Criminal Bar Association, primarily under the auspices of the Criminal Legal Aid Working Group, independently chaired by His Honour Judge Burgess. The most recent meeting of the Working Group was on 16 October 2025, followed by a further meeting with the Criminal Bar Association on 22 October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail all payments made (i) for works carried out; (ii) as a loan or easements; (iii) for compensation; and (iv) for any other payments, under the NI Water Electrical Inspection, Testing Repair Contract, C1247, since 27 May 2025.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. In terms of your request, NI Water has advised my department that the payments under this contract are as follows:(i) 	For works carried out           		£19,304.47(ii) 	As a loan or easements    		£0(iii) 	For compensation               		£0(iv) 	For any other payments   		£108,786.78For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the criteria used by his Department’s Sure Start programme, to identify children under the age of four, and their families, with demonstrable need, who live outside electoral wards that are currently designated as disadvantaged areas, to provide targeted outreach support; and (ii) any additional programme funding that has been made available for 2025, to enable all of the Sure Start projects to expand their services in this way.
answer_text: A family living outside current catchment areas may be referred to Sure Start via a new Outreach model if a child of the family is of Sure Start age (0-3 years) and has a significant developmental support need, is known to Social Services, is a Child Looked After, is from a minority or marginalised group or is a child of a parent experiencing antenatal or postnatal mental health issues or a disability.Additional funding of £1.56m was made available in 2025-26 to enable the 38 Sure Start projects to implement the new Outreach model.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department’s plan to reduce ambulance handover times to meet the 15-minute standard.
answer_text: Improving ambulance handover times is a key priority for my Department. Earlier this year, my officials, alongside clinicians from Health and Social Care Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service, visited a hospital in London to learn from their approach to releasing ambulances more quickly. Following that visit, Trusts were asked to implement similar changes by October 2025, with the initial goal of eliminating handover delays exceeding two hours by December 2025, working towards improvement against the 15 minutes standard. I have asked Trusts and the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service to adopt a similar model. To support this, £10 million repurposed funding has been reallocated to Trusts from existing baselines, with the aim of implementing targeted interventions by October 2025. My Permanent Secretary, supported by the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, has also endorsed the implementation of regional ambulance handover guidance, with the aim of eliminating handovers exceeding two hours by 1 December 2025, as part of plans to address winter pressures. In addition, my Department is working with the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service to finalise a 10-year workforce and financial plan to improve emergency response capacity. This includes a previous commitment to train 50 new paramedics annually, which has increased to 65 places from September 2025, in Northern Ireland and a Clinical Capacity Review to assess staffing needs and support the Ambulance Service’s evolving clinical model. This is a system-wide challenge, and my Department is committed to leading the co-ordinated action required to deliver these improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when NI Water will make public the report by Deloitte on its investigation into alleged fraud at NI Water.
answer_text: My Department wrote to the NI Water Chief Executive on 8 October 2025 and asked for the report to be published promptly. My Department is continuing to engage with NI Water to ensure that the report is made publicly available in the interests of openness and transparency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assessment her Department has made of the impact on the proposed A29 Cookstown Bypass of the factual position and legal position around (a) emission targets; (b) carbon budgets; and (c) the Climate Action Plan; (ii) when the assessment was carried out; and (iii) the number of times the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs was consulted on this.
answer_text: The A29 Cookstown Bypass will provide a new 4km single carriageway with overtaking lanes in both directions; the upgrading of connections between the Omagh and Dungannon roads; the provision of a combined footway/cycle way along the entire length of the scheme.As part of the Environmental Impact Assessment, analysis of the effect on climate resulting from the scheme shows an overall reduction in carbon emissions in the long term, having a beneficial impact on climate when compared against no scheme.As with all projects within the road infrastructure portfolio and in light of the A5 judgement and my subsequent appeal, I am taking this opportunity to consider its potential impact before determining the next steps for this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will publish the minutes of the 13 meetings, between the 9 August 2022 and 27 June 2024, that officials from her Department held with officials from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on the factual position and legal position around (i) emission targets; (ii) carbon budgets; and (iii) the Climate Action Plan and its impact on the proposed upgrade of the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
answer_text: The Department’s position on these matters is clearly and comprehensively set out in the ‘Statement by the Department on the Report on the Public Local Inquiries into the A5 Western Transport Corridor Road Scheme’, as published in October of 2024 (specifically pages 148 – 166 therein address the PAC’s recommendations around climate change).My officials are currently dealing with a Freedom of Information request in relation to the 13 meetings held with DAERA to which a response is expected to issue in the coming days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when financial payments will be provided to fishermen on Lough Neagh whose livelihoods have been affected by the impact of the continued occurrence of blue-green algae.
answer_text: I have engaged with the Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society to obtain information to contribute to the development of a Business Case for a financial assistance scheme. I will work to bring forward a scheme as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when she will answer AQW 30018/22-27.
answer_text: A response to AQW 30018/22-27 was issued on Monday 6th October 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 31950/22-27, to detail (i) when the Speak Up (Whistleblowing) policy text was formally adopted, in its present format, by NI Water; and (ii) the consultation that took place on that document with (a) Executive Departments; (b) trade unions; and (c) other stakeholder organisations.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information and can advise as follows:(i)           The current whistleblowing policy was approved by NI Water’s Audit Committee at its meeting on 19 September 2025;(ii)          No consultation took place with (a) (b) or (c).For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support Invest NI has provided to companies involved in the supply chain of components and equipment for any armaments.
answer_text: Invest NI does not provide financial support towards the manufacturing of armaments or their components.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to outline the reasons why NI Water does not have a whistleblower policy available for public perusal; and (ii) will she place a copy of the full policy in the Assembly library.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information and can advise as follows:(i)  NI Water has now published its Speak Up (Whistleblowing) policy on its website and this is available here Speak Up How to raise a concern .   NI Water has advised my department that this policy is aligned with the NICS Raising a Concern Policy Framework(ii) The policy is now publicly available through NI Water’s website.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps she has taken to ensure that any company, supported by Invest NI, is not involved in the supply chain of components and equipment for armaments, that are subsequently used by the Israeli Defence Forces.
answer_text: Invest NI is carrying out a review of its investments and the report will be published shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support Invest NI has provided to companies involved in the supply chain of F-35 components and equipment.
answer_text: Invest NI does not fund projects that support the manufacturing costs of arms or their components. Companies operating in the advanced manufacturing sector receive funding for projects delivered under a range of Invest NI programmes. These include Grant for R&D, Skills Growth Programme, Selective Financial Assistance, Skills development, Energy Efficiency Capital Grant, Process Excellence and Trade Export support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30201/22-27,  to detail any steps he is taking to reduce the number of people still waiting for all or part of their domiciliary care packages.
answer_text: With a growing ageing population, the demand for older people’s social care services has increased significantly.  Social Services do work with individuals and their family/carer to explore all options around family/informal/community and voluntary supports available before exploring the need for domiciliary care services.  My Department, last year provided a small amount of money to each of the five Trusts to implement the ‘Early Review Team’ with the aim of reviewing new or increased packages of care at an early stage to ensure individuals have the correct package of care to meet their needs and to ensure some of the capacity can be redirected to other individuals assessed as requiring a domiciliary care package. The early review is targeted at the over 65 population.  This concept has proved quite successful in that some domiciliary care hours have been recycled to those most in need and has helped reduce waiting lists for domiciliary care.   Currently my department is scoping out requirements and costings to extend the early review concept to cover all other adult programmes of care.The HSC trusts are working to review domiciliary care staff rotas and runs to maximize capacity.The Social Care Collaborative Forum and the Fair Work Forum are maintaining a focus on home care across the region.  There is   recognition of the vital work home care workers provide and the need to improve renumeration and career pathways within home care, in order to attract more people into this career.  This will inevitably improve capacity and reduce unmet need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) all those, including public bodies and private organisations, who have ownership of (a) any part of the Lough Neagh bed; (b) land with access to the Lough shore; and (c) fishing or shooting rights on this land; and (ii) how this has impacted efforts to protect the Lough and take effective action.
answer_text: The Lough Neagh bed is owned by the Shaftesbury Estate.  With regards to ownership of land with access to the Lough Shore, the Department of Finance’s Land and Property Services (LPS) is responsible for the Land Registry for Northern Ireland, which will give details of individual lands registered.  		My department makes no determination of and maintains no record regarding the ownership of fishing rights on Lough Neagh. The Lough Neagh Fishermen’s Co-operative Society Ltd. asserts control of and manages the commercial fishing rights for Lough Neagh. Shooting rights for the land around Lough Neagh are a matter for the owners of those lands.  		Notwithstanding this, my department continues to regulate water quality, protect habitats and biodiversity, and respond to environmental incidents, irrespective of land ownership. 		We are on a shared journey to improve the water quality of Lough Neagh and its wider catchment.   I would emphasise the importance of collaboration and collective action in addressing the environmental challenges facing the Lough.  I am committed to working together towards a positive outcome that benefits both the environment and the local community.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any discussions he has had with the UK Government on the operation of Local Growth funding when it replaces the Shared Prosperity Fund, which is due to end in March 2026.
answer_text: I have regularly pressed and written to British Government Ministers on this matter. I have written to both the previous Minister for Local Growth in the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government (MHCLG), Alex Norris, and the then Deputy Prime Minister regarding this issue. I have also raised the lack of detail and progress on Local Growth in meetings with Darren Jones, previous Chief Secretary to the Treasury, and Hilary Benn, the Secretary of State. This issue was also raised when the Deputy First Minister and I met with the Chancellor in August.Following the recent Westminster re-shuffle, I wrote to the new MHCLG Minister with responsibility for Local Growth, Miatta Fahnbulleh, seeking an urgent meeting to press for progress on this issue, alongside Hilary Benn, Secretary of State (16 September). Having had no response to that letter, I wrote again to the NIO Secretary of State on 30 September, emphasising the importance of progressing this matter and the impact of continued delay on our communities. I asked him to meet me as a matter of urgency.I met again with Hilary Benn on 1 October to discuss the implications of Whitehall decisions to end the Shared Prosperity Fund without a proper successor and to skew the new Local Growth Fund towards Capital. He has committed to arranging a trilateral meeting involving the MHCLG Secretary of State to explore any potential flexibility on the Capital/Resource funding split. I anticipate this meeting will take place in the near future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of patients waiting for an occupational therapy assessment for a Disabled Facilities Grant; and (ii) the number of patients waiting for an occupational therapy assessment for a Disabled Facilities Grant for more than (a) three months; (b) six months; (c) 12 months; and (d) 24 months, broken down by Health and Social Care Trusts

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the total number of applications for a Disabled Facilities Grant currently in the system; and (ii) the number of patients waiting for a decision on an application for a Disabled Facilities Grant for more than (a) three months; (b) six months; (c) 12 months; and (d) 24 months.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has provided the following information.“(i) There are currently 2,040 DFG cases being administered by the Housing Executive through the various stages of the process.(ii) 73 of these cases have reached the stage in the process where all completed documentation has been received and are waiting for an approval to start works.  The total number of applicants who have been waiting for less than 3 months for this decision is 69.      The number of applicants waiting for this decision for more than (a) three months is 4; (b) six months is zero; (c) twelve months is zero and (d) 24 months is zero.”

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any discussions they have had with the UK Government on the operation of Local Growth funding when it replaces the Shared Prosperity Fund, which is due to end in March 2026.
answer_text: We have raised this issue during all our engagements with the British Government and will continue to do so to ensure the ongoing delivery of the important work funded under the current arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to increase the number of Occupational Therapists in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to improve the provision of domiciliary carers in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Approximately 20% of domiciliary carers are employees of the Northern Health and Care Trust and the remaining 80% are employed by the independent sector. An official from my Department has contacted the Northern Health and Social Care Trust regarding your query and they have provided an update.  The Northern Trust are encouraging providers to expand capacity in geographical areas where there is identified significant unmet need that would require additional runs to be created.The Trust is currently in the preparatory stage of a 2-year major reform programme with the aim of optimising, stabilising and levelling-up provision across the Trust’s Geographical Area.  The programme of reform will be across both statutory Home Care provision and Independent Sector Home Care provision with the ultimate aim of addressing unmet need and creating home care capacity.In addition, the Trust continues to ensure focused recruitment to its statutory in-house service, has introduced Early Review Teams to ensure the timely review of Home Care packages of Care following discharge from hospital and welcomes the roll out of Care line Live, a digital solution to ensure service capacity is maximised.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans the Driver and Vehicle Agency has to modify existing (i) services; (ii) facilities; and (iii) staffing arrangements, in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has no immediate plans to modify existing services and facilities in the Mid Ulster constituency which is served by Cookstown test centre. The DVA has recently concluded a recruitment competition to appoint up to 42 new vehicle examiners across their network of test centres. The first cohort of 12 successful candidates has commenced training and is expected to be deployed to test centres, including Cookstown, upon successful completion of their training in early October. The DVA has invested in two new test centres at Hydebank and Mallusk. They are scheduled to have new vehicle testing equipment installed in 2026, after which the DVA will agree a programme to roll out new vehicle testing equipment across the rest of its test centre network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of roads which currently require (i) resurfacing; and (ii) partial resurfacing in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads within the Mid Ulster District Council area that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the resurfacing schemes planned for the 2025/26 financial year will be included in my Department’s Annual Report to Council.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections of the road network in Mid Ulster and repair any defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the average waiting time for an emergency ambulance response in Mid Ulster, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Ambulance call categorisation for emergency calls is standardised across the UK.  For your information, ambulance call category definitions and response time standards are set out in Table 1 below. Table 1Call TypeCategoryMean StandardImmediately life threateningCat 18 Mins999 Emergency – potentially serious incidentsCat 218 MinsUrgent problemCat 3*120 MinsNon- emergency ED visitsCat 4 *180 Mins	*This is the 90th centile standard for category 3 and category 4 calls as there is not a mean standard	Table 2 below shows the mean response times across categories 1-4 for the Mid-Ulster area, for the period January 2020 to August 2025.Table 2YearCategory 1Category 2Category 3Category 42020 Mean0:13:070:24:190:54:191:39:102021 Mean0:13:480:32:391:15:002:05:152022 Mean0:14:540:39:251:31:062:31:182023 Mean0:14:190:42:211:56:033:01:212024 Mean0:15:270:58:512:17:551:54:432025 Mean0:15:421:05:182:15:421:14:08Overall Mean0:14:400:43:031:34:311:52:51The impact on patient outcomes of appropriate and timely ambulance responses for every call received was recognised in the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) Review of Emergency Medicine.  Ambulance handover delays at emergency departments (EDs) impact on the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service’s (NIAS) ability to respond to emergency calls in the community.  Improving ambulance handover times is a key priority for my department. Earlier this year, departmental officials, alongside clinicians from the Trusts and NIAS, visited a hospital in London to learn from their approach to releasing ambulances more quickly. Following that visit, the Trusts and NIAS were asked to adopt a similar model. At a regional workshop in August 2025, the Permanent Secretary, supported by the Chief Medical Officer and Chief Nursing Officer, endorsed the implementation of regional ambulance handover guidance aimed at eliminating handovers exceeding two hours by 1 December 2025.  Achieving the 15-minute handover standard remains a longer-term objective.This is a system-wide challenge, and my department is committed to leading the co-ordinated action required to deliver these improvements.In addition, my department is working with NIAS to ensure sufficient ambulance and staffing capacity to meet Category 1 and Category 2 response time standards through the development of a 10-year workforce and financial plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she plans to commence a further roll-out of the part-time 20mph speed limit schemes at schools across Mid Ulster.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North and are listed on my Department’s website, link; 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3 | Department for Infrastructure.  This third tranche focuses on rural schools that are located on roads where the national speed limit applies while considering the safety implications of introducing lower speed limits.  This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.My Department is also currently carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including 20mph speed limits in general and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any discussions she has had with the Minister of Education on a new advertising campaign for schools on road safety.
answer_text: I recently met with the Minister of Education to discuss the need to raise awareness among pupils and parents about the risks associated with road use, particularly in relation to school buses, embarking and disembarking a bus.We have agreed to work together on the promotion of road safety for children and young people, recognising the importance of reinforcing key safety messages within the school environment. Both Departments are committed to identifying practical and age-appropriate methods to help young people better understand their responsibilities as road users and to encourage safer behaviours from an early age.A school bus safety campaign, featuring rear bus and on-street advertising, was initially introduced in May and relaunched on 8 September, promoting vital safety messages to road users.  I am currently considering enhancing this campaign by introducing point of danger radio campaign with drivers as the target audience. In addition, Road Safety Teaching Aid Calendars have been digitally distributed to over 760 primary schools for the new academic year. These calendars, tailored for Foundation Stage, Key Stage 1, and Key Stage 2, are now available for download via NIDirect. Each week, they provide key road safety messages, including safety around buses, that teachers can easily incorporate into classroom discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for a timeline of his Department's next steps in relation to the Anti-Poverty Strategy.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans in place for a new advertising campaign for schools on road safety.
answer_text: Road safety advertising campaigns are the responsibility of the Minister for Infrastructure and I fully support the new campaign ‘Roadwise Up' which aims to raise motorists’ awareness of the risks that children face when boarding and alighting buses. The safety of children travelling to school is of utmost importance and I met with the Minister for Infrastructure and the Education Authority recently about this issue. We agreed that our officials would collaborate closely to ascertain if enhanced messaging or additional resources can be developed to raise awareness and promote bus safety for school pupils. My department is represented on the Department for Infrastructure led Road Safety Strategic Forum, which brings together members from relevant departments and organisations to focus on matters directly impacting Northern Ireland’s road safety targets. This forum provides a mechanism through which the Department of Education and the Education Authority can contribute to the development of measures to improve road safety for children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children who have registered for education orchestras and ensembles supported by his Department in each of the last eight academic years, including the current academic year, broken down by Education Authority region.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), which holds operational responsibility for the Music Service, has provided the following data outlining pupil registration figures for the Orchestra, Band, and Ensemble programmes over the past eight years, broken down by legacy Education and Library Board (ELB) areas. 2018/192019/202020/212021/222022/232023/242024/252025/26Belfast882859132577619588525529Western50048460180365430450480North East59159365308528498503458Southern484551461270380477463458South East246251162175217244261275Totals2703273888015102109223722022200

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the mechanisms used by NI Water to measure the level of microcystins in the public water supply; and (ii) how often the measurement is taken at each water treatment plant, over the course of a year.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. (i)NI Water has advised that it uses the Drinking Water Safety Plan (DWSP) approach to assess the risk for Microcystins in the public water supply.   The DWSP approach is based on the World Health Organisation’s Water Safety Plan guidance and is used to systematically assess and manage drinking water quality risks from the catchment and raw water source to the customer tap. NI Water has developed an in-house method for the analysis of Microcystins, based on the industry standard Standing Committee of Analysts method. This method provides NI Water with a rapid, sensitive, and reliable analytical method for the measurement of microcystin’s in the public drinking water supply and also to monitor the risk in the raw water and the performance of the multibarrier treatment barriers in place at the WTW.(ii) NI Water has advised that sampling and analysis programmes for Microcystins are based on the DWSP risk assessment for each Water Treatment Works (WTW). This is kept under review in line with the DWSP risk assessment and sample numbers increased when required. Raw and treated final water samples are tested at least fortnightly at water treatment works where there is a potential for algal blooms to occur in the raw water source. For sites where the risk is greatest the frequency of testing is increased to at least weekly, with some sites such as those that use Lough Neagh as the raw water source, sampled at least three times per week over the high risk period or when there are algal blooms reported.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to improve access to consultant-led neurology services in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Every patient should have equal opportunity to access services. My Department is working collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure equity across the region, which includes the Northern HSC Trust.  Equity of access is a central theme in both the Elective Care Framework and the Health and Social Care Reset Plan.Furthermore, the Regional Review of Neurology Services final report sets out a long-term vision for neurology services that are person-centred, joined up, responsive and sustainably resourced. 		To support the achievement of this vision, the Review Team identified four priority areas with associated recommendations to drive improvements in services, one of which is addressing gaps in current services. This priority focuses on addressing shortfalls in current services to ensure equity of access to timely, responsive Neurology services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of (i) outstanding road potholes for repair; (ii) completed pothole repairs; and (iii) pothole compensation payments, in each of the last four years, broken down by constituency or local council area.
answer_text: My Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.This data is regularly published and available for public view through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects and can be filtered by defect type and geographical area.In addition, official statistics on the condition of the road network are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics. The 2023-24 publication can be found at Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics 2023-24 | Department for Infrastructure.The management information my Department holds in relation to roads related public liability claims can be found online at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20202021-20242025 Table 7 provides details of Public Liability Claim expenditure for the last five full financial years broken down by Section Office, claim type and expenditure type. Please note Section Offices broadly align with Council areas.Claim figures have been extracted from DfI's Claims Management System, whereas the financial data has been extracted from the NICS accounting system, Account NI.Unfortunately, as the NICS Accounting System does not record the cause of incidents that lead to compensation payments, it is not possible to disaggregate the amount of compensation paid out by defect type.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the current applications for the funding of new school buildings in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: There are currently seven schools in Mid Ulster who have been announced for funding under the Major Capital Works Programme.The status of these projects are as follows:•	Edendork Primary School, Dungannon – procurement of a building contractor is in progress.  •	Gaelscoil Ui Neill, Coalisland – building contractor appointed and pre-construction activities ongoing. •	Holy Trinity College, Cookstown – construction ongoing. •	St Conor’s College, Coleraine – currently in design development.•	St Patrick’s College, Maghera – currently in design development.Sperrin Integrated College, Magherafelt, and St Pius X College, Magherafelt, were included in the 2022 Major Capital Works Programme announcement, however they are two of twenty one projects from this announcement which are currently on hold due to budget pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the schools in Mid Ulster that are awaiting the beginning or completion of a new build project; and (ii) the current prioritisation ranking of works for those new school builds that are yet to begin.
answer_text: Currently seven major capital works projects have been announced for schools in Mid Ulster.The status of these projects are as follows:Edendork Primary School, Dungannon – procurement of a building contractor is in progress.  Gaelscoil Ui Neill, Coalisland – building contractor appointed and pre-construction activities ongoing. Holy Trinity College, Cookstown – construction ongoing. St Conor’s College, Coleraine – currently in design development.St Patrick’s College, Maghera – currently in design development.Sperrin Integrated College, Magherafelt, and St Pius X College, Magherafelt, were included in the 2022 Major Capital Works Programme announcement, however they are two of twenty one projects from this announcement which are currently on hold due to budget pressures. All live projects are currently being progressed through the design stages up to the pre-construction stage. I can assure you that I have and will continue to make the case for significant additional and sustained capital funding for my department, to permit projects to proceed to construction as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how he plans to improve the provision of mental health services across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 launched in June 2021 sets the vision and future strategic direction for mental health services in Northern Ireland over the next decade. This strategy is for all people of Northern Ireland regardless of their geographical location. Everyone in Northern Ireland is expected to benefit from this strategy as it is a holistic societal approach to mental health and wellbeing with a whole life approach to ownership. Action 1 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to increase public awareness of the distinction between mental wellbeing, mental ill health and mental illness, encouraging public understanding and acceptance of how life can impact upon mental wellbeing, and recognition of the signs of mental ill health and mental illness.  Action 2 of the Mental health Strategy commits to create an action plan for promoting mental health through early intervention and prevention, with year on year actions covering a whole life approach, reaching from infancy to older age. The action plan must consider groups disproportionally affected by mental ill health who often struggle to access early intervention services and seek to reduce stigma associated with mental ill health. Action 31 of the strategy is to, “Develop a regional mental health service, operating across the five HSC Trusts, with regional professional leadership that is responsible for consistency in service delivery and development”. This is to be achieved not by changing organisational boundaries but by ensuring there are systems in place to deliver regional consistency, quality and access across Northern Ireland. Implementing the new regional crisis service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, as set out in Action 27:     Create a Regional Mental Health Crisis Service that is fully integrated in mental health services and which will help and support persons in mental health or suicidal crisis.Work in this area is progressing as a lifespan approach ensuring an equitable crisis response service in all geographical areas of Northern Ireland.It is co-produced with significant engagement across a broad range of stakeholders including people with lived experience as service users and as carers. It sets out the structures and arrangements necessary for a Regional Mental Health Service encompassing Trusts’ adult mental health services, primary care and services delivered by community and voluntary sector partners. CAMHS and Mental Health Services for Older People managed care networks will link in as part of the arrangements for a regional mental health service supporting an overall life span approach.  The Strategy is person centred, takes a whole life approach and a whole system focus and the key aim is to ensure long term improved outcomes for people’s mental health.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she plans to review the suitability of the current criteria for the installation of street lighting in rural areas, where significant numbers of children use rural roads for school transport.
answer_text: My Department’s policy for the provision of street lighting in rural locations considers:-The density of housing within a community, including public buildings with significant nighttime use; and, Road safety, where street lighting would contribute to a reduction in the number of night-time collisions.The policy is subject to continual informal review and ensures that the limited resources available for the provision of street lighting are directed to those areas where they can provide the maximum benefit to the public, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned.It also serves to balance the demand for more rural lighting against the unwelcome effects of increased urbanisation of the countryside, the environmental impact of night sky light pollution on people, wildlife and flora and the financial costs of providing and maintaining additional public lighting installations.As such, I have no plans to change this policy at this time

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will review the provision of street lighting on the Moneyhaw Road, Magherafelt, in proximity to the junction with Drummullan Road, given concerns about safety, particularly for school children from the area travelling on buses.
answer_text: My Department’s policy for the provision of street lighting in rural locations considers:The density of housing within a community, including public buildings with significant nighttime use; and, Road safety, where street lighting would contribute to a reduction in the number of night-time collisions.These criteria serve to balance the demand for more rural lighting against the unwelcome effects of increased urbanisation of the countryside, the environmental impact of night sky light pollution on people, wildlife and flora and the financial costs of providing and maintaining additional public lighting installations.My officials have assessed the Moneyhaw Road and have confirmed that the section does not fulfil the above criteria. Consequently, there are no plans to install street lighting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what plans he has for a review of mental health services across Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031 sets out a vision for the future direction of mental health services in Northern Ireland, with a number of actions currently underway to enable reform and improvement of existing provision. A key element of this work involves reviewing existing mental health services to identify priority areas for improvement.For example, the recently established Regional Mental Health Service (Mental Health Strategy Action 31) is currently undertaking a review of existing community mental health provision with a view to improving regional consistency and removing unwarranted variation. To date, this work has involved completion of a mapping exercise to scope existing provision, and a review of comparable community mental health arrangements in other regions. A task and finish group has established to analyse the outputs from this work and to progress the design of a regionally consistent model, which it is anticipated will be completed by Spring 2026. In parallel to work on implementing the Mental Health Strategy, individual health trusts will also undertake reviews of mental health provision within their areas as part of a continuous improvement process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of a new centre for mental health at Antrim Area Hospital.
answer_text: Following the announcement of the outcome of the 2025 UK wide Spending Review in June, the Department of Finance has commissioned a Budget exercise that will determine the capital budgets for Northern Ireland Department’s for the years 2026-27 to 2029-30. Whilst I remain hopeful, my ability to commit to funding capital projects, including the important Birch Hill Centre for Mental Health, will depend on the Department of Health capital allocation from the Budget process. The draft Budget outcome is not expected to be confirmed until later this year and will inform the development of the Department of Health capital investment plan which will set out the projects to be taken forward over the next ten years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the implementation of the Lough Neagh Action Plan.
answer_text: The Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan, agreed by the Executive in July 2024, contains science led actions to tackle the blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland. Of the 37 actions included in the plan, 14 have been completed, 22 are currently underway, and one is pending due to its reliance on another action being implemented.I remain fully committed to driving forward implementation of the Action Plan, working closely with other Departments, particularly the Department of Infrastructure and the Department of Justice, where their input is required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the number of occasions the Health and Safety Executive had reason to contact NI Water about health and safety practices at that organisation, since September 2024; (ii) any health and safety shortcomings identified as a consequence of that contact with NI Water; and (iii) what measures have been taken by NI Water to address those identified health and safety deficiencies.
answer_text: Since September 2024 the Health and Safety Executive for Northern Ireland (HSENI) have contacted NI Water on 48 occasions and this includes both proactive and reactive engagement. In terms of any shortcomings and remedial measures taken by duty-holders, Article 30 of the Health and Safety at Work (Northern Ireland) Order 1978 places a restriction on the release of such information by HSENI. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any recent discussions with the Minister for Infrastructure and the Education Authority on the need for the implementation of new school bus road safety measures.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when she will announce her decision on appealing the High Court ruling blocking construction of the upgrade to the A5 road in its current form.
answer_text: I wrote to Executive colleagues on 11 July 2025 to provide an update on the judgment and to clarify that, as Minister for Infrastructure, the decision to appeal rests with me. Given the project’s flagship status and the Executive’s unanimous support in October 2024, I have invited their views before reaching a final decision. I remain committed to working constructively and collaboratively with colleagues to secure a solution that delivers the road and saves lives. Any appeal must be lodged within six weeks of the judgement taking effect.

department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he plans to publish the revised Nutrients Action Programme, following the current public consultation on the existing proposals.
answer_text: The current public consultation on the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026-2029 will close on 24 July 2025.  After the consultation closes, I will set up a timebound stakeholder task and finish group to work collaboratively to propose measures that are evidence based and workable at farm level with realistic timeframes, while also meeting legislative requirements. This will be followed by a further 8-week public consultation on updated proposals after which officials will consider responses.
