department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the measures taken at NI Water to prevent whistleblowers from being penalised.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that its Speak Up (Whistleblowing) policy sets out the circumstances in which those making disclosures about wrongdoing will qualify for protection.  In accordance with that policy, NI Water will not tolerate discrimination, bullying, harassment or victimisation of anyone under any circumstances; any of these actions constitutes a serious disciplinary offence which can lead to dismissal.  Protected disclosures are handled in accordance with the Public Interest Disclosure (Prescribed Persons) Order.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the most recent annual estimate for the amount of waste deposited illegally in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency’s Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) is focused on investigating and pursuing offenders involved in the illegal depositing of waste. 		ECU focuses its resources on those cases which present the most significant risk to the environment and public health, in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy, such as the disposal of larger quantities of waste or the disposal of hazardous waste.		Where sufficient evidence is available ECU will conduct criminal investigations in pursuit of offenders responsible for conducting unlawful activities relating to controlled waste under the Waste and Contaminated Land (NI) Order 1997 (The 1997 Act).		In 2024 ECU secured 11 convictions relating to the illegal depositing, keeping, treating and disposal of approximately 16,807.80 tonnes of controlled waste.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the effectiveness of the Dalriada Urgent Care provision of an out-of-hours GP service for all residents within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust area.
answer_text: The GP Out of Hours service is a core element of health and social care provision in primary care.  Dalriada Urgent Care (DUC) is commissioned to provide the GP Out of Hours (OOHs) service across the Northern area.  The GP OOHs service is available Mon-Fri from 6pm to 8am the next morning, as well as 24 hours at weekends and during public holidays.Patients who contact the service will be triaged by a telephone or video call by clinical staff. Many patients can be safely and effectively clinically triaged by either a Nurse Advisor, Prescribing Pharmacist, an Advanced Nurse Practitioner (ANP) or GP.  The majority of patients who contact Dalriada Urgent Care OOHs service do not require to travel to be seen face-to-face.For 2024/25 contact numbers were 134,597; 75% of Dalriada’s patient contacts were managed by telephone or video call with a Clinician; home visits accounted for 5% of their contacts; and patients seen face to face in a GP OOH centre accounted for 20% of contacts.GP rota fill rate for 2024/25 was an average of 96%.   Since the adoption of a two centre model based at Causeway and Antrim Area Hospitals Dalriada has been more successful in staffing its GP duty rotas during the Out of Hours period.  Dalriada covers the largest population of the 5 GP Out of Hours providers, circa 479k, being the resident population of the Northern Trust area.  The population of the Northern Trust area has a higher proportion of older people than the average for N Ireland and is impacted to a higher degree by rural needs.Patient contacts are triaged with the service providing multiple care pathways: telephone advice, clinic visits at Antrim or Causeway hospitals, or home visits when clinically appropriate for those unable to travel. Dalriada employs a substantial workforce including sessional doctors, triage nurses, advanced nurse practitioners, prescribing pharmacists, mental health practitioners and operational staff, ensuring a multidisciplinary approach to urgent care.The service has faced staffing challenges in the past, similar to other urgent care providers. The triage system and multiple care pathways (phone advice, clinic visits, home visits) ensure flexibility in addressing urgent needs.Dalriada are highly responsive service providers and this has been seen in how the organisation has responded to demanding challenges for example the outbreak of Scarlet Fever in 2022/23 and previous to that, the Covid Pandemic. Dalriada is governed by a Steering Council established in 2005 and constituted of members in line with national guidance, developed to support and oversee the work of mutual organisations. The Council includes a Dalriada staff representative, 4 GPs, Northern Trust senior management and service user representatives. It meets quarterly. Dalriada Urgent Care is a vital component of the Northern Health and Social Care’s healthcare infrastructure, providing essential out-of-hours GP services to a large and diverse population.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the steps he will take to ensure effective regulation and enforcement is in place to tackle the environmental and financial impact of waste crime.
answer_text: My department delivers effective regulation and enforcement through a structured, multi-agency approach, led by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA).	The NIEA regulates waste through a robust framework that includes issuing authorisations, conducting inspections and audits, maintaining public registers, and providing regulatory guidance. Enforcement actions are taken to deter non-compliance and uphold environmental standards.The Environmental Crime Unit (ECU) of NIEA continues to investigate and prosecute the most serious and persistent waste criminality in Northern Ireland.  The ECU focuses its resources on those cases which present the most significant risk to the environment and public health and utilises powers contained within the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 to pursue prosecutions. Such prosecutions are often coupled with confiscation proceedings under the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002, aimed at depriving those convicted of the proceeds generated from their crimes whilst asserting the ‘Polluter Pays Principle’. NIEA continues to adopt a multi-agency approach through operational cooperation with other government agencies.  By working together with partner agencies, the NIEA takes a comprehensive approach in tackling illegal waste criminality through effective inter-agency collaboration.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the out-of-hours service at Dalriada Urgent Care at the Mid Ulster Hospital, Magherafelt, will be reinstated  to ensure access for local communities to the necessary face-to-face contact, when required.
answer_text: The GP Out of Hours (GP OOHs) service is a core element of health and social care provision in primary care and the service is available Mon-Fri from 6pm to 8am the next morning, as well as 24 hours at weekends and during public holidays.The majority of patients who contact GP OOHs services across the region do not require to be seen face-to-face.  The percentage of patients who contact the GP OOH service, and who need to be seen in an OOH centre (known as base visits) has changed over the years. In 2015/16, base visits accounted for 40% of Dalriada Urgent Care’s (DUC) patient contacts, in 2019/20 base visits had reduced to 30% of contacts.  In 2023/24, 76% of DUC’s patient contacts were managed by telephone or video call with a clinician, home visits accounted for 5% of their contacts and patients seen face to face in a GP OOH centre accounted for 19% of contacts.Due to workforce challenges, mainly across the GP community, DUC has indicated that they do not currently have the capacity to provide a service from the Mid-Ulster hospital base.  DUC provides services to patients in the Mid-Ulster area by telephone, by home visit when clinically appropriate, and in Antrim or Causeway Hospital by appointment.  Whilst this approach facilitates more integrated working between the primary care and hospital based urgent and emergency care services, this is constantly kept under review.These workforce challenges are common to all GP OOH services across the region. Consolidating services to a smaller number of larger centres has improved the recruitment and retention of staff, due to the benefits associated with collaboration, peer support, and equitable workload distribution.All GP OOH providers are expected to review activity levels and capacity challenges to ensure the efficient use of resources and provision of an effective service, within the budget allocated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of occasions, in the last ten years, that NI Water has employed the services of an experienced counter fraud specialist, in line with Recommendation 42 of the March 2013 NI Audit Office report, Northern Ireland Water's Response to a Suspected Fraud.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that all NI Water investigations are carried out by trained investigators and that, on two occasions in the last 10 years, NI Water has employed the services of an external counter fraud specialist.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the Transition Year model in the Republic of Ireland; and (ii) and whether any consideration has been given to introducing a similar programme in post-primary education in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any assessment of the Transition Year model in the Republic of Ireland and it is not currently under active consideration by the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how NI Water has complied with Recommendation 64 of the March 2013 NI Audit Office report, Northern Ireland Water's Response to a Suspected Fraud.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised my department that NI Water’s Internal Audit is informed of a suspected fraud at the same time as the Director of Finance and the Chair of the Audit Committee, as per NI Water’s Fraud Prevention Policy and its Fraud & Bribery Response Plan. NI Water’s initial enquiries and reporting timelines are aligned with the Department’s Anti-Fraud Policy and Response Plan.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to any suspected fraud at NI Water reported to the Shareholder Unit, to detail (i) the date reported; and (ii) the action taken, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. (i) NI Water has advised my department that in the last 10 years, suspected frauds have been reported to Shareholder Unit on following dates: YearDates suspected fraud reported20154 November 201524 November 2015201630 December 201620171 June 201710 November 2017201823 February 201815 March 20184 April 201820198 August 2019202027 May 202031 July 2020202119 May 202125 August 20212022None2023 20 September 2023202414 June 202422 November 2024202530 January 20255 June 2025(ii) NI Water has advised that all instances were dealt with in accordance with NI Water’s Fraud Prevention Policy and Fraud and Bribery Response Plan.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the outcome of the June Monitoring Round, that resulted in no additional funding for road maintenance, to detail what measures she will take to (i) ensure the safety of all road users; and (ii) protect the jobs of skilled road workers.
answer_text: My Department places a high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as patching, grass cutting, and also through a properly resourced programme of Structural Maintenance to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, funding to maintain our roads has been less than required for many years. Given the current constrained financial environment, my Department will continue to prioritise its road maintenance activities to ensure that the safety of road users is not compromised.	I am also very mindful of the impact that a reduced maintenance budget has on the wider construction industry and in particular those skilled workers that work hard on a daily basis to deliver essential road safety related activities. As such I will continue to work with my Executive colleagues in an effort to ensure that this essential work is properly funded.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what newbuild projects under the remit of his Department are currently unable to proceed due to the absence of adequate water infrastructure.
answer_text: Currently, no new school build projects have been explicitly halted due to the absence of adequate water infrastructure. However, the capacity of existing water infrastructure, particularly wastewater infrastructure is a significant and escalating reason for delays in building new schools across Northern Ireland.    In addition, wastewater infrastructure issues have led to increased capital project costs for new school builds, including the installation of localised wastewater treatment systems, due to an inability to connect to the public wastewater network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the new build projects under his Department's remit that are unable to proceed due to the absence of adequate water infrastructure.
answer_text: No new build projects under my Department’s remit have been unable to proceed due to the absence of adequate water infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what newbuild social housing projects are curerntly unable to proceed due to the absence of adequate water infrastructure.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive is involved in on-going engagement with NI Water to review the status of all proposed new social housing schemes currently included in the Social Housing Development Programme (SHDP) for the period 2025/26 – 2027/28. Initial feedback provided by NI Water suggests that there are a small number of planned social housing schemes (c 14%) for which an NI Water capital upgrade is required before a connection can be permitted i.e. a connection to the wastewater system is outside the scope of a ‘zero-detriment’ developer intervention. Further engagement is required to determine if delivery of any of these proposals can be unlocked.However, most planned new social housing schemes over the next 3 years can proceed, as either an agreement has been reached or is in progress with NI Water via the Wastewater Impact Assessment process. NI Water have also offered to hold a one-day workshop with all developing housing associations in late summer 2025 to enhance the level of engagement with the sector.In addition to the on-going assessment of programmed new social housing schemes, the Housing Executive is commencing engagement with NI Water regarding new housing delivery beyond the current 3-Year SHDP in the context of long-term housing need and NI Water’s next Price Control period (PC28).”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the newbuild projects, under her Department's remit, that are unable to proceed due to the absence of adequate water infrastructure.
answer_text: My Department has no construction projects that are delayed or unable to proceed due to inadequate water infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 26610/22-27, to list the specific recommendations which have been implemented in full by NI Water.
answer_text: NI Water has advised my department that, pursuant to AQW 26610/22-27, all recommendations directly or indirectly relevant to NI Water have been implemented in full as listed below:Recommendation 5Recommendation 7Recommendation 8Recommendation 9Recommendation 10Recommendation 11Recommendation 12Recommendation 14Recommendation 15Recommendation 16Recommendation 17Recommendation 20Recommendation 21Recommendation 22Recommendation 23

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will introduce a 20mph speed limit at (i) St. Patrick's College, St. Mary Building, Tullyheran Road, Maghera; and (ii) St. John’s Primary School, Kingsisland.
answer_text: I was pleased to announce the next phase of part-time 20mph speed limit schemes for schools across the North. A full list of schools included in the next tranche is available on my department’s website 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3 | Department for InfrastructureAll schools within the Mid Ulster Council area have been assessed to ensure that those most in need of part time 20mph speed limits are prioritised. Regrettably, St. Patrick's College and St. John’s Primary schools did not score as highly as other locations and were not included in this tranche.  However, these schools will be considered in any future extensions of the programme.While the tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline (i) when a full Economic Impact Assessment into the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026-2029 will take place; and (ii) how the Department for the Economy will be involved.
answer_text: (i) The current public consultation on the proposed Nutrients Action Programme for 2026-2029 will close on 24 July 2025.  After the consultation closes, I will set up a timebound stakeholder task and finish group to work collaboratively to propose measures, for my consideration. Following this, I will decide the revised proposed NAP measures which will be subject to an 8-week public consultation period, before any final decisions are made.  A draft Economic Impact Assessment will also be consulted on at that stage. After the consultation closes and responses have been considered, a final Economic Impact Assessment will be completed on the final NAP measures.(ii) If any part of this policy development falls under the remit of another government department, I will ensure they are involved.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy who authorised the Mineral Prospecting Licences awarded on 8 May 2025.
answer_text: The Mineral Prospecting Licences were assessed against the legislative framework as outlined in the Mineral Development Act (Northern Ireland) 1969, and the decision to grant the licences was taken by officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the implications for Mineral Prospecting Licences, issued by her Department, as a consequence of the High Court judgment of Monday 23 June 2025 on the A5 Western Transport Corridor.
answer_text: There are no foreseen implications for Mineral Prospecting Licences as a consequence of the High Court judgement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the criteria used by a moderation panel to decide whether a child with special education needs is (i) provided with resources, advice and guidance; or (ii) provided with teaching support.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised thatThe moderation process determines the extent to which an appropriate graduated response has been provided by the child’s setting, and the strength of evidence presented to indicate that EA support at Stage 2 is the next appropriate step. Where Local IMPACT Team (LIT) support is deemed to be the most appropriate next step, the Request for Involvement will pass to the appropriate LIT specialist team member based on the child’s presenting needs. The programme of offer available from a LIT will be delivered through a tiered model of support reflecting increasing intensity as the child/ young person moves through the stages of the Code of Practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail if the conditions of employment for literacy teachers within the Local Impact Teams remain unchanged.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that:There will be no changes to Literacy Teachers’ terms and conditions of employment with the establishment of the Local IMPACT Teams.  This phase of the Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Transformation Programme is a reconfiguration of SEND Support Services and all staff will transfer on existing terms and conditions of employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what evaluation mechanism has been put in place to assess the effectiveness of moderation panels.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that: A moderation process was introduced from March 2025 to ensure standardised management of Requests for Involvement (RfI) and referrals to Education Authority (EA) Behaviour and Special Educational Needs and Disability (SEND) Support Services.The consistency and effectiveness of the moderation process will be continually assessed through sample audit by the Local IMPACT Team Specialist leads and Assistant Senior Education Officers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what direct input educational psychologists have into the decision-making process of moderation panels.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:Educational Psychologists (EP) currently have no direct input to the moderation process. The Educational Psychology Service is working closely with senior management within the Local IMPACT Team (LIT) structures to align EP operations more closely to those of the LITs with a view to further integrating those operations over time. This may ultimately involve EP representation at moderation panels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the qualifications for membership of Education Authority moderation panels.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:There is no fixed qualification requirement for membership of Request for Involvement (RfI) Moderation Panels. To date, the panels have been made up of the EA’s most senior Special Educational Need and Disability (SEND) support specialists (i.e. the Heads of the Literacy, Autism, Language and Communication and Behaviour Services) overseen by the division’s Assistant Senior Education Officers. The most senior SEND specialists across the Local IMPACT Team network will continue to have an advisory and quality assurance role across all Moderation Panels in September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what measures her Department will be taking to update protections in health and safety legislation.
answer_text: The current health and safety legislation contains wide reaching protections. However, environmental and other factors can create new and complex challenges that impact on all of society. The Minister has already made a commitment to working with Trade Unions and a range of relevant stakeholders to make improvements where possible and appropriate. This work will be progressed over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what financial support will be provided to the fishing industry on Lough Neagh.
answer_text: I remain committed to providing whatever support is practical and affordable to the eel fishers, including through existing grant schemes.  Any case for direct financial support in respect of the now truncated fishing season would need to be robustly supported with scientific and financial evidence for the suspension decision and the impact and the scale of the losses. I have instructed my officials to engage further with the industry in the development of any request.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps he is taking to reduce class sizes in (i) primary; and (ii) secondary schools, to ease pressure on teachers and improve learning outcomes.
answer_text: My Department delegates as much funding and decision-making as possible to schools, who are best placed to assess the needs of their pupils.  It is a matter for each school's Principal and Board of Governors to decide on individual class sizes, taking account of the school’s budget, its staffing complement, and the legislation and guidance in place which governs class sizes in our schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the Northern Ireland Fiscal Council's Sustainability Report 2025: special focus - Water, due to be published on 10 June 2025.
To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the whistleblowing protections which exist for private sector contractors and their employees employed on public sector projects.
answer_text: Departments have adopted a policy framework for raising concerns (often referred to as Whistleblowing). The policy framework allows a concern to be raised by a member of staff or a member of the public (which would include contractors or their staff). This framework protects whistleblowers’ anonymity. Links to the Departments' Designated Officers and more details on the policy framework can be found at https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/raising-concerns.As employers, private sector contractors will themselves be bound by the requirements around protected disclosures by employees set out in the Employment Rights (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 as amended by the Public Interest Disclosure (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any action his Department is taking to reduce delays in Educational Psychology assessments.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans she has to improve public transport services in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to Translink to provide public transport services and facilities. This means creating a consistent customer experience for everyone, ensuring accessible public transport for people of all ages and abilities, including those in Mid Ulster.Recognising that Translink services cannot reach every person, I also provide funding to community transport organisations to support the rural Dial-a-Lift and urban Disability Action Transport Scheme transport services for those within our communities who find it difficult or impossible to access mainstream transport services.  I acknowledge that employment and other costs across the community transport sector have risen significantly over the last couple of years.  To mitigate against these increases and to protect these vital services, I intend to increase the level of funding available to the community transport operators, that will enable them to deliver these vital services in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the progress of the Mid Ulster Local Development Plan, including (i) the timeline for adoption; and (ii) to detail outstanding issues under consideration by her Department.
answer_text: My Department is continuing to engage with Mid Ulster District Council on its draft Plan Strategy Submission on a number of strategic and operational policy issues. I cannot provide at this time a definitive timeframe for adoption of Mid Ulster District Councils own Local Development Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to outline the (a) timeline; and (b) criteria, for the further roll-out of the part-time 20mph speed limit scheme outside schools; and (ii) to detail how many additional schools are expected to be included in the next phase.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users.  A review of the recent initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools.  The review recommends the provision of a further tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding.  The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected, and work to update our overall road safety at schools policy document is underway.  In the meantime, I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in this financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies.  Our approach to selecting schools is outlined in our interim policy document DEM 194/25 ‘Part-time 20mph Speed Limits at Schools – Tranche 3 2025/26’ which is publicly available at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-05/part-time-20mph-speed-limits-at-schools-tranche3-2025-26-dem-19425.pdf I intend to make an announcement on the number of schools to be treated in the next tranche in the coming weeks.I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what whistleblowing protections exist within her Department for private sector contractors and their employees, employed on public sector projects.
answer_text: Since January 2023, the previous whistleblowing procedures within the Civil Service were superseded by the Raising a Concern Policy Framework. This framework established a consistent approach across departments for handling concerns about potential wrongdoing.In line with this framework, each department, including the Department for Infrastructure (DfI), was required to develop its own operational guidance tailored to its specific context. DfI has published its Raising a Concern Operational Guidance, which outlines the procedures for reporting, recording, and investigating concerns.Importantly, this guidance includes a dedicated section (Paragraphs 44–51) that specifically addresses the process for raising concerns by contractors and their employees engaged on public sector projects. This section provides clear instructions on how such individuals can raise concerns within the Department. It also provides details of proscribed persons/bodies to which staff may report a serious concern, with protection afforded by the Public Interest Disclosure (NI) Order 1998.Furthermore, employees of private sector contractors are also free to raise concerns outside the Department, including through external channels, with assurances of anonymity and confidentiality, should they prefer to do so.The full guidance is publicly available on the Department’s website: www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/raising-concern-operational-guidance

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the Wastewater Treatment Works at the Loup, Mid Ulster, will be fully operational.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:	NI Water is currently undertaking base maintenance work at the Loup wastewater treatment works (WwTW) and that work is nearing completion. The Loup WwTW remains fully operational while this work is ongoing. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline: (i) what assessment has been made of the suitability of the new 4:15pm start time for Education Authority Music Service ensemble hubs for rural working families, particularly those with children attending schools in geographically separate locations; and (ii) if a Rural Needs Impact Assessment and Equality Impact Assessment were carried out.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) advise the context for change in the Music Service’s approach to service provision is a regional service review and transformation process. This review and transformation process has included a focus on delivering consistent service delivery models with equitable service provision for children, young people and schools in all parts of Northern Ireland. The arrangements seek to ensure that children and young people were able to avail of after school tuition as early as possible at the request of the young people themselves, their parents and EA Music staff.Changes will also provide for a greater geographical spread of provision, accommodating pupils closer to their own schools and homes, with an aim of access within 30 minutes travel time.As part of planning for this change, EA Music contacted 420 parents, receiving feedback from 21 individuals. The overwhelming response was very positive and any concerns raised have been addressed. The EA considers that these changes will enhance the breadth and quality of access for many more children and young people across all localities. The EA states the Music Service review was included in EA’s Equality Screening Framework, with all appropriate aspects being subject to Equality Screening and/or Rural Proofing exercises, including the changes to service provision outlined above, which involve a move to a greater number of locality Hubs for delivery of ensembles. The EA advises that the Equality and Rural screening process has confirmed that the innovative approach proposed through the service review will help to ensure that the service is rurally accessible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the total number of applicants on the social housing waiting list for Banbridge, broken down by (i) general needs; and (ii) supported living.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows.“There are a total of 492 applicants for Banbridge Town Common Landlord Area including 95 applicants with complex needs as at the end of December 2024.Complex needs applicants may have special circumstances that mean they need extra care and support. The Housing Executive may consider these applicants for specialist housing schemes. These provide extra support or care best suited to meet applicant needs. Applicants may have a tailored support and care package in place to meet their needs and in that case may be considered for general housing.As of 1st January 2025, there were 26 supported applications registered on the waiting list for Banbridge Town Common Landlord Area (CLA).”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly with regard to matters that are their responsibility. Additional schemes can also be added to the SHDP ‘in-year’ through Competitive Land, Design & Build procurement route and/or the purchase of Existing Satisfactory / Off-the-Shelf properties.Table 1: Social housing units under construction in Banbridge.Housing AssociationHE RegionLocal Government District Parliamentary Constituency Scheme NameLocation TypeNeed GroupStrategic GroupUnitsOnsite YearCompletionYearArbour HousingSouthArmagh Banbridge & CraigavonUpper BannCommercial RoadBanbridgeCompetitive Land Design & BuildGeneral NeedsUrban442023/242026/27Arbour HousingSouthArmagh Banbridge & CraigavonUpper BannCommercial RoadBanbridgeCompetitive Land Design & BuildActive ElderlyUrban112023/242026/27Arbour HousingSouthArmagh Banbridge & CraigavonUpper BannCommercial RoadBanbridgeCompetitive Land Design & BuildWheelchair StandardUrban42023/242026/27’For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make the best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what plans are in place to ensure long-term sustainability of the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: The Voluntary and Community Sector is made up of a diverse range of independent, self-governing organisations delivering across a wide spectrum of social outcomes. The sustainability of the Sector as a whole depends on many factors, including government and non-government funding and sustainable volunteer effort.My Department works to support a thriving and sustainable sector by supporting the Sector’s working relationship with government and building capacity in the Sector.My Department convenes and co-chairs the Joint Forum between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector, which includes representation from across the voluntary and community sector and all Executive departments.  A new Partnership Agreement between Government and the Voluntary and Community Sector, developed by the Joint Forum received Executive approval on 5 June and will be published shortly.  My Department also provides a significant level of infrastructure support to the Sector.  Work to refresh this investment is ongoing, to ensure future programmes are targeted at addressing current and emerging needs. I remain committed to continued investment in the voluntary and community sector, to provide support for those working and volunteering in the sector; strengthen sustainability and deliver long-term impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans he has to provide sustainable funding for community and voluntary organisations delivering bereavement support, in light of increased demand following the COVID-19 pandemic.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the progress her Department has made on improving rural public transport links.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to Translink to provide public transport services and facilities. Recognising that Translink services cannot reach every person, I also provide funding to community transport, delivering access to transport for those, including older people, who find it difficult to access mainstream public transport.  The rural Dial-a-Lift service assists many people in rural areas to remain connected to their communities and get to the places they need to go locally.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the steps she is taking to ensure transport accessibility for older people and people with disabilities in rural areas.
answer_text: My Department provides funding to Rural Community Transport Partnerships to deliver the rural Dial-A-Lift service. This is a membership only service which provides transport to those who are rurally isolated and find it difficult to access mainstream public transport. I recognise that Community Transport provides a valuable service for older people and people with disabilities in rural areas and I intend to reflect that in this year’s budget allocations.  In addition to the concessionary fares schemes and accessibility measures already available, Translink will continue to update its fleet and enhance accessibility features over time, and it is in regular consultation with IMTAC in relation to the design of its public transport vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any engagement he has had with the Home Office on the UK Government’s upcoming Immigration White Paper.
answer_text: My Department provided substantial input to The Executive Office to inform the NI response to the Home Office’s request for proposals to be considered for inclusion in the White Paper.DAERA’s input outlined the situation in the mushroom and fishing sectors with regards to inadequate labour availability meaning the viability of these sectors is under threat.  There is also the potential for future labour shortages of butchers in the food processing sector. I have previously communicated these issues and suggested modest policy proposals to resolve the challenges with the Defra Secretary of State Steve Reed OBE and more recently with Minister Fleur Anderson, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at the Northern Ireland Office. The proposals included within the UK Government’s Immigration White Paper are deeply concerning in relation to their potential impact on NI agri-food businesses.  It does not appear that the White Paper takes account of the specific needs of agri-food or NI’s unique circumstances. The proposed increase in the overall skills threshold for migration to RQF 6 effectively removes the eligibility of butchers and fishing occupations from the Skilled Worker Route.My officials and I will be engaging further with the UK Government on the matters arising from the White Paper which impact on the NI agri-food sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what input has their Department had to date to the UK Government’s upcoming Immigration White Paper, specifically in relation to the mushroom sector.
answer_text: The deputy First Minister and Junior Minister Reilly attended an Inter-Ministerial Group meeting where the immigration white paper was discussed. They raised key local issues and highlighted local needs, including the impact on agrifood sector here.Ministers also wrote to Home Office on 29 May 2025 to highlight the issue and specifically reflected the issues and potential local mitigations for the Mushroom sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 25257/22-27, to confirm that there are currently 14 disabled parking spaces available at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children.
answer_text: There are currently 14 disabled spaces in the surrounding perimeter of the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC), with a further 5 disabled spaces in the secured barrier car park for RBHSC.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what engagement has their Department had to date with (i) the Home Office; and (ii) the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs , on the UK Government’s upcoming Immigration White Paper, specifically in relation to the mushroom sector.
answer_text: Immigration policy is not devolved.The specific issue of the mushroom growers was raised by Ministers at the Inter Ministerial meeting on Wednesday 21 May 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what engagement has he had with the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs to date on its agri-food strategy.
answer_text: My officials are represented at Defra led meetings to develop a high-level outcomes framework for the UK food system. The approach will consider the food system as a whole, while recognising that large parts of food policy are devolved. Initial meetings have taken place of a DEFRA led Senior Officials Group which brings together senior officials from departments with an interest in food policy and a Food Strategy Advisory Board made up of senior leaders from across the UK food system.Progress on the development of the food strategy is also discussed as an agenda item  at the Inter-Ministerial Group for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs which I attend.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) what the high-risk occupations are for blood cancers, based on research; and (ii) what steps are being taken to improve early diagnosis, monitoring, and workplace protections for these groups.
answer_text: There is limited research specific to the prevalence of blood cancers associated with specific occupations.   However, some high-risk occupations with increased risk of blood cancer, particularly leukaemia, include those with exposure to certain chemicals, solvents and radiation such as benzene and ionising radiation.  The list below outlines occupations which are susceptible to a higher risk of developing blood cancers.  Petrochemical Industry Workers (Limited population in NI);Rubber and Plastics Manufacturing Workers;Agricultural Workers and Farmers (where exposed to certain pesticides);Painters and Industrial Coaters;Shoemakers and Leather Workers (limited population in NI);Radiologic Technologists and Nuclear Workers (mostly in healthcare where highly regulated);Dry Cleaning Workers;Firefighters;Hairdressers and Barbers and;Printing and Press WorkersWhilst some of these occupations are very specific it should be noted that not everyone employed within these sectors will be exposed to high-risk chemicals associated with blood cancers. In 2015, the World Health Organisation’s International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) co-ordinated a study on the International Nuclear Workers Study (INWORKS).  This study provided the strongest evidence yet that protracted exposure to low doses of ionizing radiation can cause leukaemia and that the risk of death from leukaemia increases linearly with the radiation dose.  In terms of the steps being taken to improve early diagnosis, monitoring and workplace protection for these groups, the Health and Safety Executive Northern Ireland (HSENI) is currently running an Occupational Health Campaign involving inspection, advisory visits, information promotion and education across all work sectors. A key component of this campaign is to raise the awareness of employers and employees of the need to identify and manage specific risks encountered in their workplace including occupational cancers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6966/22-27, to detail (i) DfI Roads' (a) plans; and (b) schedule regarding The Mills, Coalisland; and (ii) the timeframe to appoint a contractor.
answer_text: DfI Roads hold a joint bond with NI Water for this site. In order to progress towards adoption of the Mills, my Private Streets team have contacted NI Water to request confirmation on the extent of the works necessary to bring the water infrastructure up to an adoptable standard. Once we have this information the Department can then commence enforcement action, using the bond to appoint a contractor and complete the outstanding works to allow adoption.My officials will be happy to provide a timeframe for adoption following receipt of the necessary information from NI Water.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether all recommendations regarding procurement and governance in NI Water, contained in the Public Accounts Committee report entitled Measuring the Performance of NI Water and Procurement and Governance in NI Water 2011, have been fully implemented.
answer_text: I have been advised that all recommendations regarding procurement and governance in NI Water, contained in the Public Accounts Committee report entitled Measuring the Performance of NI Water and Procurement and Governance in NI Water 2011, have been fully implemented as appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any measures being taken to deliver 24-hour access to thrombectomy services.
answer_text: I can confirm that expanding the regional thrombectomy service to 24/7 remains a key priority for Northern Ireland. However, there are significant challenges that need to be addressed to achieve this goal. These include the need for substantial investment and the recruitment of specialist staff within a limited workforce pool. Despite these challenges, my department is committed to ensuring that as many people as possible benefit from the current thrombectomy services within the existing operational hours.Additionally, my department has published the Reshaping Stroke Care Stroke Action Plan, which outlines priority areas to improve stroke services. This includes the development of a costed model to expand thrombectomy services to 24/7 and a revised thrombectomy repatriation protocol. Artificial Intelligence software has also been rolled out in six hospitals to support clinical decision-making, which is a step towards enhancing the efficiency and reach of stroke care services.While we are not yet in a position to provide a 24/7 thrombectomy service due to budget constraints, these measures are part of our ongoing efforts to improve and eventually expand the service availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline what measures he is implementing to prevent wildfires in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current number of individuals awaiting a medical driving licence assessment; and (ii) the number of medical assessors currently employed to carry out these assessments.
answer_text: 3,808 drivers have had an initial assessment completed by our medical officers and for whom we are awaiting further medical information in order to progress their applications. This may include information from their GPs or consultants, driving assessment reports or reports from our contracted specialist medical advisory service. There are currently 7 medical assessors employed to carry out these assessments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of individuals currently awaiting a medical driving test assessment; and (ii) the number of qualified assessors available to carry out these assessments.
answer_text: There are currently 1,041 drivers awaiting a medical driving assessment in Northern Ireland. The Northern Ireland Mobility Centre (NIMC) is an accredited centre of Driving Mobility and currently employs 4 part time assessors to conduct driving assessments following referral from DVA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps he is taking to ensure adequate funding for hospices to support the continued delivery of high-quality palliative care; and (ii) how this funding will be sustained to meet the growing demand for such services.
answer_text: The majority of palliative and end of life care is delivered by generalist health care professionals, including the patient’s GP, District Nurse and other healthcare professionals in hospitals and care homes. Hospices and Trusts play an important role in supporting individuals, and families with more complex physical, psychological and social needs which require specialist intervention.  I recognise that the current funding arrangements for adult hospice services have been in place for some time, to date, it has not been possible to progress a planned regional review of adult specialist care services given the ongoing and challenging financial pressures facing my Department.My Department officials have ongoing engagement with the hospice sector as part of the arrangements in place with them for the delivery of contracted specialist palliative care services. In 2024/25, £9.73million was provided to hospices for contracted adult services which included an uplift of 5.09% to support increases in National Living Wage, non-pay inflationary pressures and other pay increases. For 2025/26, an interim uplift to contracts of 2.98% has already been advised to hospices and this will be updated when possible in respect of any 2025/26 pay award.Given the complexities of palliative and end of life service provision, my officials are beginning a baseline scoping exercise to establish the totality of palliative care services (both generalist and specialist) which are currently available across the system. This exercise will include how services align to the populations they serve and a needs assessment to consider the increasing palliative and end of life care needs across Northern Ireland.The outcome will inform decisions about the future planning and commissioning of adult palliative care services across the region, including specialist palliative care services provided by hospices.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any steps his Department is taking to implement the UK Pesticides National Action Plan 2025 in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how his Department is supporting local farmers in transitioning to alternative pest management practices while ensuring agricultural productivity and environmental sustainability.
answer_text: (i) My Department has published guidance on Integrated Pest Management practices (IPM), a sustainable approach to managing pests, weeds and diseases. In collaboration with other UK administrations my officials are working to develop a range of further measures to support the implementation of the UK Pesticides National Action Plan 2025. At a UK level, work has commenced on the potential use of Drone technology to aid precision application of pesticides, and on the development of an Horizon Scanning project to identify and address future gaps in pest control methods. 		(ii) The new guidance on IPM practices supports local farmers in adopting alternative pest management practices. It provides clear and practical information about IPM and online tools are available to support planning and decision making. The guidance can be found on the DAERA website at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/integrated-pest-management 		DAERA will continue to collaborate with stakeholders to make sure pesticides are used sustainably to minimise their impact on the environment and human health.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any actions being taken to improve mobile phone service.
answer_text: As you are aware, Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter with the Department for Science, Innovation & Technology (DSIT) holding the policy responsibility on improvements to mobile connectivity. The Department for the Economy (DfE) maintains regular contact with DSIT ensuring that all local issues are given appropriate consideration for any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of telecommunications here. Mobile infrastructure is currently provided on a wholly commercial basis by Mobile Network Operators (MNOs), however, my officials are involved in a number of initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity here. These include the Mobile Action Plan and the Barrier Busting Taskforce which aims to identify the main issues that impact on the roll-out of mobile technologies and provide solutions to remove barriers that prevent the fast and efficient deployment of mobile connectivity here. My officials also support the digital infrastructure elements of the City and Growth deals which have mobile connectivity proposals designed to improve roll out. They are also investigating potential alternative funding streams to establish their viability for extending/ improving mobile connectivity in the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that music education is accessible and well-supported in schools, particularly in light of recent challenges to arts funding.
answer_text: Music education is an important aspect of school life for our children and young people.  It is covered under ‘The Arts’ area of learning and is a compulsory element of the Northern Ireland Curriculum at both primary and post-primary levels.To support schools in the delivery of music education, the Education Authority (EA), operates a Music Service which enables learners from all backgrounds to receive musical education of the highest quality.  The EA’s Music Service offers access to music education in schools through both school-based tuition and larger performance opportunities in orchestras.  Access to quality music education, through an inclusive approach to service provision, is fundamental to the operation of the Music Service, based on the principle that every young person deserves the opportunity to experience the transformative power of music.In terms of funding issues more generally, education has been and remains significantly underfunded here in Northern Ireland.  This is independently evidenced by not only the Institute for Fiscal Studies in its last two comparisons papers on school spending per pupil across the United Kingdom, but more recently the Independent Review of Education’s report, Investing in a Better Future.  Education is key to the future economic stability of our society, and we cannot afford to continue to neglect it through years of underfunding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the steps his Department is taking to ensure that schools have adequate mental health support services in place for both primary and post-primary pupils; and (ii) any plans to increase access to these services in the 2025-26 academic year.
answer_text: I am pleased that I have been able to protect the current level of funding that my department contributes to the joint investment, with the Department of Health, to a combined total of around £5m per annum to support programmes identified under the joint Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework, in what is a significantly challenging budget settlement for education. It is also important to note that schools continue to have flexibility to use their individual budgets to best meet the identified needs of their pupils and in some circumstances, they may employ the services of a counsellor or therapist from existing funds.Any immediate, and appropriately assessed, need for counselling or therapy remains available through health services, including CAMHS and associated clinical pathways to support services.While our schools are encouraged to provide a supportive and nurturing environment, the provision of mental health and therapeutic services for children and young people is ultimately within the remit of the health service and should be delivered by appropriately qualified and registered health professionals (i.e. counsellors and therapists).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any actions he is taking to address the rising suicide rates in Northern Ireland; and (ii) how his Department is working to improve mental health services and support for individuals at risk.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the devastating impact of each and every suicide, not only in the lost life but also the impact on loved ones and communities. I believe every suicide is preventable up to the point of completion, so while we may set realistic targets, in reality the only acceptable number is zero. The NISRA statistics caution against inferences being drawn on one year changes. The 3 year rolling average of suicide deaths has remained relatively stable since 2017. It should also be noted that suicide statistics relate to year of registration by the coroner and not the number of deaths within that calendar year.The Department has worked with stakeholders and other Departments to update the new Protect Life Action Plan and Implementation Plan. The Executive Working Group on Mental Wellbeing, Resilience and Suicide Prevention has been re-established and its first meeting was held on 3 April. This group will provide a renewed focus for all Ministers on suicide and self-harm prevention. The new Protect Life Action Plan and Implementation Plan were discussed at the Executive Working Group on 3 April and will be published very shortly. The new Action Plan will commit to a range of actions across all Departments across five key themes of: A Whole of Government and Society Approach; Awareness and Prevention; Supporting Compassionate Responses to Suicide; Supporting Compassionate Responses to Self Harm; and finally, Research, Evidence and Planning. The actions underpinning these themes will contain a wide range of deliverable actions over the coming years. The Mental Health Strategy 2021 – 2031 sets out the vision and future direction for mental health services in Northern Ireland over the next decade. A broad intention of the Mental Health Strategy is to put the individual’s needs at the centre, rather than their diagnosis. The Strategy seeks to ensure a “no wrong door approach” and aims to ensure that all staff are trained to identify and address the specific needs of particular at-risk groups.The Strategy lists 35 actions that set out a clear direction of travel to support and promote good mental health across our region and reduce the stigma around it.  Implementation of a range of Strategy actions is being progressed through annual delivery plans and subject to budget availability.Delivering a new Regional Mental Health Crisis Service is a commitment in the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, under Actions 12 and 27. The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group and the Public Health Agency are working in partnership to deliver on both of these actions, to ensure there is an effective crisis service established for all citizens of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on any measures his Department is taking to support farmers in adapting to the challenges posed by climate change; and (ii) how his Department will ensure these measures align with (i) environmental sustainability; and (ii) economic viability in the agriculture sector.
answer_text: To meet DAERA’s obligation under the Climate Change Act, an evidence-led and co-design approach to policy development has been adopted to ensure that policies and proposals to meet Climate Change targets, contribute to the stronger resilience, improved environmental sustainability and enhanced productivity of our agriculture sector.The Schemes being introduced as part of the Sustainable Agriculture Programme will be essential levers in contributing to Northern Ireland’s statutory obligations under the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022.I am also committed to delivering a ‘Just Transition’ for Agriculture and I have been successful in securing a budget of £12.3m for 2025/26 to take forward initial work in this area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail the number of disabled parking spaces currently available at the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children; (ii) how many of these spaces are assigned to disabled staff; and (iii) how many disabled parking spaces have been removed to facilitate the construction of the new Children’s Hospital.
answer_text: There are 14 disabled parking spaces available around the perimeter of Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children. None of these spaces are assigned to disabled staff.To accommodate the construction of the New Children's Hospital, four disabled parking spaces at the Falls Road entrance to the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC) have been removed. These spaces were previously decommissioned for the demolition of Bostock House and were reinstated in the summer of 2024.The Belfast Trust recognises the scale of this project, and the necessary support works will cause significant disruption across the Royal site. The Trust sincerely appreciate the public's patience as they progress with this important project and are committed to minimising the impact wherever possible.
