answer_text: The Terms of Reference for the forensic accountant investigation into NI Water is in the process of being finalised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the total expenditure on medicines in (i) primary care; and (ii) secondary care, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Primary care medicines expenditure includes the cost of medicines in primary care services namely, GP's, Pharmacists, Dentists and Optometrists, however this will primarily represent the reimbursement of prescribed medicines issued by Community Pharmacies.  Secondary care medicines expenditure is the cost of medicines reported by the six Health and Social Care Trusts.The total expenditure on medicines in primary care and secondary care in each of the last ten years is set out in the table below. Primary Care£mSecondary Care£2014/15 425.8  166.9 2015/16 441.1  167.2 2016/17 439.5  183.8 2017/18 436.3  192.3 2018/19 421.3  211.6 2019/20 444.1  224.4 2020/21 454.3  231.2 2021/22 456.2  285.1 2022/23 486.9  296.8 2023/24 501.3  326.2 Notes:Secondary Care: may include a small element relating medicines issued outside an acute care setting, e.g. GP Out of Hours, which cannot be identified separately.The values in the above table exclude any cost of medicines where treatment is delivered outside of NI, as the cost of medicines is not separately defined within this expenditure.Expenditure excludes VAT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the funding support or mitigations that will be provided to community pharmacy, GPs, dentists and opticians for the increases in National Insurance contributions and the National Minimum Wage from April 2025.
answer_text: I recognise that the increase in employer’s National Insurance contributions announced in the Autumn Budget as well as the increase to the National Living wage will significantly increase the cost-base for Family Health Service providers.  I have written to the Secretary of State for Health and Social Care on the issue of National Insurance, to urge him to work to ensure the treatment of these providers faced with these cost pressures is equal to our other providers of health and social care.I have also written to the former Finance Minister asking that she also bring her influence to bear on HM Treasury in this regard and I am aware that she has written to the Chief Secretary to the Treasury in similar terms.Despite my efforts and that of the former Finance Minister I am not anticipating additional funding will be provided to cover these additional costs.Whilst a final Budget allocation has not been agreed, following the announcement of the 2025-26 Draft Budget allocation my department has assessed that we will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025-26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.In recognition of the valuable role played by those providers affected, I will be considering these funding pressures alongside other priorities before I make any final funding decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when negotiations for new contractual and funding arrangements with community pharmacy representatives will commence.
answer_text: A contractual framework has been in place with Community Pharmacy since 2017 which was negotiated between Health and Social Care Board, DoH and Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI). My Department continues to liaise with CPNI, through the Community Pharmacy Commissioning Oversight Group, to oversee the delivery of commissioned community pharmacy services in line with the financial envelope and against the commissioning plan in order to ensure the provision of best possible services to patients and the public. My officials are working closely with CPNI regarding the development of a new 3-year commissioning plan 2025-2028 to support implementation of the Community Pharmacy Strategy 2030. A workshop was held with CPNI on 4 February 2025 to review achievements of the previous 3 years, and to a develop the new plan which has since been shared with CPNI.  A funding update for 2024/25 & 2025/26 was issued to community pharmacy contractors on 14th March 2025. This provided an increase to the opening position of 2024/25 of £7.7m as follows:Opening position 2024/25£147mNon-Global sum uplift 2024/25£2.4mMargin Threshold£2.5mZero Discount Appliances£2.8m (estimate)Total £154.7mIncrease£7.7mFollowing the announcement of the 2025/26 Draft Budget allocation the Department has assessed that it will be facing a significant funding gap in 2025/26 as a result of both inflationary pressures and increased demand.In recognition of the valuable role played by community pharmacies, these funding pressures are being considered alongside the other priorities of the Department and we are unable to make any commitments at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessments of impact and consultation he is carrying out, or has planned to carry out, with disability groups and stakeholders in relation to the future of Personal Independence Payment.
answer_text: I refer you to my answer provided for AQW 24701/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the estimated financial loss to Northern Ireland claimants as a result of the proposals in the UK Government's Pathways to Work: Reforming Benefits and Support to Get Britain Working Green Paper.
To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on roof repairs to Parliament Buildings.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 24017/22-27, to detail how many of the 40 cases referred to NI Water were downloaded from the whistleblower’s SharePoint and had a full technical investigation, including application, payments and site validation processes, by Deloitte for their recent report into alleged fraud within NI Water.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information and NI Water has advised that all cases were downloaded from the whistleblower’s SharePoint site and reviewed by Deloitte as appropriate as part of the investigation.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide clarity on the future of Personal Independence Payment.
answer_text: I do not know the full impact of the welfare changes announced on 18 March. My officials are continuing to work closely with the Department for Work and Pensions to understand the full implications for Northern Ireland.  Calculations on the UK wide impact of the Green Paper were published along with the Chancellor’s Spring Statement on 26 March. It will take time to understand the Northern Ireland impacts thereafter. I want to ensure that Northern Ireland has a welfare system that is fair for claimants and taxpayers and protects the most vulnerable. I will continue to engage with the UK Government to ensure that this happens, and also with Executive colleagues as Northern Ireland does not have the resources to mitigate against these changes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans he has in place to provide financial support or alternative assistance to those who may be adversely impacted by any changes to Personal Independence Payment.
answer_text: I refer you to my answer provided for AQW 24701/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his long-term vision for Personal Independent Payment; and (ii) how his Department will ensure that it remains responsive to the needs of people with disabilities and long-term health conditions.
answer_text: I refer you to my answer provided for AQW 24701/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 23592/22-27, to detail the number of cases of alleged fraud within NI Water that have investigated by Internal Audit in each of the last five years; and, of those cases, to detail (i) the number of disciplinary actions that have been taken; and (ii) the number of cases that have been referred for further investigation, including to whom they have been referred.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. Pursuant to AQW 23592/22-27, NI Water has advised that there have been 5 cases of alleged fraud in the last 5 years, which have been specifically investigated by Internal Audit as follows:Year Number of cases Number of Disciplinary actions that have been taken Number of cases referred for further investigation2019/20 1002020/21 2002021/22 2002022/23 0002023/24 000Total 500Please note that suspected water fraud cases, including unauthorised connections to NI Water’s infrastructure, meter tampering and other suspected breaches of legislation, are dealt with by the NI Water Compliance Investigation Team (CIT) and not Internal Audit.  For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures that will be introduced by her Department to ensure the safety of children alighting from and boarding school buses.
answer_text: I am committed to considering any initiatives that can improve road safety, which is a priority for me as Minister.  With regard to school buses, I have asked my officials to look at all the options that may be available to improve safety for school children and to report back to me as a matter of urgency.I have also asked my officials to reinforce the messaging to help keep all road users safe, especially the vulnerable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of comments made on 12 March 2025 by the Minister of State for the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs in relation to halting subsides for farmers.
answer_text: Comments made by DEFRA Secretary of State relate to Defra’s Sustainable Farming Incentive Scheme in England. Agri-Environment support is devolved in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of whether the use of unexplained wealth orders in the last three years has been a sufficient deterrent for organised crime gangs.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of inpatient beds planned for the inpatient oncology and haematology ward at the new Children’s Hospital in Belfast; (ii) the number of individual bedrooms with en suite facilities; (iii) the number of bedrooms classed as isolation rooms; (iv) the number of treatment rooms; (v) the areas available for parents to prepare and store meals within the oncology and haematology ward.
answer_text: Haematology and oncology will be a completely distinct department from other services within the New Children's Hospital.  This will include inpatients, outpatients and a Programmed Treatment Unit for outpatients to receive treatment.The New Children’s Hospital will have physical space for 13 single oncology/haematology beds. At present, Belfast HSC Trust are commissioned for 10 beds, additional investment would be required to increase bed numbers from the current commissioned levels. Without additional investment the new hospital will transfer "as is" with ten funded beds.All single rooms will have en suite facilities. Two of these rooms are planned as positive pressure isolation rooms.The Programmed Treatment Unit will have a bay area for six patients to receive treatment in addition to two bedrooms with en suite facilities.  Outpatients will consist of four consultation rooms and one treatment room.Throughout the floors of the New Children’s Hospital, there are break out areas where families can prepare food. Parent coffee points with kitchenettes are located on each ward of the New Children’s Hospital in a central location.  Whilst parents will have an area to make tea/coffee, the storing and preparation of food within the New Children's Hospital will be restricted to ensure compliance with food hygiene and safety requirements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount paid by NI Water to Deloitte in each of the last five years.
answer_text: (i) what targeted support services are available or being developed to address the needs of women living with ADHD post-diagnosis;There is currently no commissioned service for Adult ADHD.All Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of adults with ADHD within Trust areas. The Department is commissioning a research exercise into the current and projected demand with a view to bringing forward recommendations to inform a commissioned ADHD service, subject to future budget availability. The exercise will (amongst other things) seek to establish the prevalence of ADHD (for both children and adults) in Northern Ireland, examine best practice from other jurisdictions and identify challenges and barriers in relation to ADHD diagnosis and treatment. Importantly, the research exercise will be expected to make recommendations on what any future commissioned service might look like in terms of, for example, costs, staffing profiles and recommended care pathways.A research lead has now been identified and work on the research has commenced, with an anticipated completion date of end June 2025.Any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model.(ii) what plans his Department has to improve training for healthcare providers to better recognise and diagnose ADHD in women.As indicated above, any future commissioned service will be subject to both budget availability and evidenced demand, but the research, once completed, will help provide a firm evidence base to inform a future model which will include training provision required for healthcare provider to better recognise and diagnose ADHD in women.I hope this information is helpful and provides some clarity to you on this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount paid by NI Water to Deloitte for its recent report on its investigation into alleged fraud at NI Water.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised the amount that will be paid is £144,000. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the terms of reference for the recent report by Deloitte into alleged fraud at NI Water will be made public.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water have advised Deloitte were commissioned to carry out an investigation to assess if there was any evidence of fraudulent activity and to highlight recommended actions to strengthen internal controls. The terms of reference for the completed investigated covered 4 allegation areas as follows:Contracted work paid for by NI Water but works not carried out by contractor(s); Direct award of contracts over £5,000 to contractors without following NI Water’s policy; Unregistered subcontractors working on remedial repairs under contract; Recently commissioned or refurbished sites, by external contractors, from the capital/asset side of the business were not “defect free” on completion”.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com . Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the budget that has been allocated for the office of each language commissioner.
answer_text: The business case provides for the establishment of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression (OICE), the Irish Language Commissioner, and the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition and the initial operating costs of each new body. In respect of both Commissioner’s offices, it provides that, following establishment, initial operating costs will amount to £1m per year, for the first two years.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the timescale for the appointment of the language commissioners.
answer_text: We were delighted to announce that competitions to recruit: the Director and other members of the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;  the Irish Language Commissioner; and the  Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition launched 10th March 2025.We are committed to making appointments as soon as possible.The competitions will be run in line with the principles of the Code of Practice for Ministerial Public Appointments, which means that there are additional steps that need to be taken into consideration in terms of governance and accountability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many cases of alleged fraud within NI Water have been reported for each of the last five years.
answer_text: The issue you have raised is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the required information. NI Water has advised that the number of cases of alleged fraud which have been reported for each of the last five years is as follows:-YearNumber of cases2019/20 144 2020/21 127 2021/22 125 2022/23 118 2023/24 80 Total 594For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the key principles proposed within the new framework for reforming North-South bodies.
answer_text: ANSWERThe new proposed funding framework for North-South bodies, whilst agreed by the previous Finance Minister, Dr Caoimhe Archibald, has not yet received Executive approval.It is a cross-cutting issue and a paper was circulated to the Executive on 15 October 2024 and has been requested for inclusion at a number of subsequent Executive meetings.Once agreed, the Assembly will be advised of the new arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail what has been done by his Department to match the extra funding required for Foras na Gaeilge.
answer_text: ANSWERIt is the responsibility of sponsor departments, both North and South, to jointly develop and agree budgets for Foras na Gaeilge. The agreed provision for the relevant contributions North and South respectively will be funded from the budget of the Sponsor Department in the relevant jurisdiction.  On this basis, responsibility for funding for Foras na Gaeilge is primarily a matter for the Minister for Communities.My predecessor,Finance Minister Caoimhe Archibald sought to bring forward alternative funding proposals for North South bodies, including Foras na Gaeilge. These proposals require Executive agreement.  My officials have also met with the North Sount Ministerial Council to discuss the funding of the body, and a scoping exercise is progressing to examine and address the issues being faced.  My officials and Department for Communities officials are working closely on these matters.I also met with the Minister of Public Expenditure and Reform, Jack Chambers, on 4 March where we discussed efforts being taken forward in this area.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail what has been done by their Department to match the extra funding required for Foras na Gaeilge.
answer_text: No budget has yet been set for Foras na Gaeilge.We understand that Department for Communities (DfC) officials are working with their Irish Government counterparts and with Foras na Gaeilge on the development of a Business Plan and associated budget, which will go through the normal approval processes in each jurisdiction before coming to a NSMC Language meeting for approval.  As part of investigations into the challenges faced by Foras na Gaeilge, DfC and the corresponding Irish Government department, have identified that the manner in which Foras na Gaeilge has treated pension payments is having a particular impact.Resolving this issue could go some way towards alleviating pressures on the Body. DfC and DoF are working through this with Irish Government counterparts at present. TEO Officials in the NSMC Joint Secretariat are also engaging on this to support finding a resolution.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether reform of the funding formula for North South bodies will be a priority on the agenda for the next scheduled meeting of the North South Ministerial Council.
answer_text: There is no agreement on reform of the funding formula for North South Bodies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any guidelines that will be introduced to recognise the community of Irish speakers by facilitating their inclusion in our legal services and spaces.
answer_text: I very much welcome the long overdue repeal of the Administration of Justice (Language) Act (Ireland) 1737 as a significant recognition of the importance of Irish to many in our community. Any application to use Irish in legal proceedings is a matter for the courts and cannot, therefore be the subject of Departmental guidelines. My department already recognises the Irish-speaking community by having a code of courtesy on the use of Irish in official business, which is designed to assist staff in fulfilling the Department’s obligations under the European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any financial support available to those farmed plantations affected by ash dieback.
answer_text: Many woodlands, despite the impact of ash dieback, can continue to develop and provide the range of benefits associated with a woodland land use. On that basis my Department continues to make annual premia payments to land owners over the course of their individual forestry grant schemes. Woodlands that have been impacted to the extent that they need to be felled are eligible for the Woodland Investment Grant (Restocking). This supports replanting and details of the grant are available via the Department’s website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when pensioners no longer eligible for the Winter Fuel Payment will receive the one-off fuel support payment of £100.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer provided to AQW 21433/22-27

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) the standards of customer service required under Project Stratum; and (ii) the oversight mechanisms in place at her Department to ensure delivery of those standards.
answer_text: The Department’s contract with Fibrus is to provide a wholesale network. Customers will interact with a Retail Service Providers (RSPs) who are using the wholesale network. Fibrus are required to provide RSPs with 99.5% network availability and resolve problems within 2 working days, following the relevant industry standards. Where these requirements are not met, an RSP is entitled to compensation under a scheme that must follow Good Industry Practice and any requirements from the communications regulator (Ofcom).The provision of these services is monitored by the Department by way of quarterly reports required by the contract, which includes information on compliance with the service level, which must include: network availability; trouble to resolve time; provisioning order installation timescale; and service failure compensation (number and value of compensation paid for failed time to resolve).

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: Question 14 AQO 1598/22-27 grouped with Question 15 AQO 1599/22-27Since my predecessor Conor Murphy set out a new economic vision for the North, my Department and its delivery partners have taken forward a wide range of initiatives to help create more good jobs.The Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill public consultation focused on four key aspects of a Good Job – terms of employment; pay and benefits; voice and representation; and work-life balance. Detailed analysis of responses is almost complete. This analysis, along with other policy considerations, will inform my decisions about the way ahead. I plan to bring Bill proposals to the Executive for agreement in the months ahead. A Good Work Charter consultation will be launched in the coming months. I look forward to working alongside the LRA, business and trade union leaders in co-designing a Charter that will support businesses here to maintain and create good jobs for all.We want to stimulate high-value-added growth by promoting industries and sectors with the potential to create high-quality jobs with good pay, conditions, security and opportunities. Last year we published action plans for seven high-potential industrial sectors, a Sub-Regional Economic Plan, and a Social Enterprise Action Plan.Continued investment in education and skills is another crucial component of the good jobs agenda. Despite the continuing budget constraints we face, we prioritised up to £12m this year for a Skills Fund that offers reskilling and upskilling opportunities for both those in and seeking employment.We also provided extra funding to grow student numbers in our universities and have taken a range of measures to widen access and participation for people regardless of their background, including a 20% increase in the maximum maintenance loan available for higher education students. Thanks to the Magee Taskforce we now have a clear action plan to expand university provision in the North-West.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of fatalities that are attributable to the use of generators during the power outage caused by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: NISRA collates data on death registrations as collected by the General Register Office. The information requested is not available as there is no data gathered via the death registration process to identify deaths attributable to specific circumstances such as due to the use of generators during a power outage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when resurfacing measures will be carried out to Main Street, Toomebridge.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition, due to over a decade of underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including Main Street Toomebridge, due to limited budget resources resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.  Details of the planned list of upcoming resurfacing schemes will be included within the Department’s Annual Report to Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a timeline for the bovine TB action plan.
answer_text: The first meeting of the TB Partnership Steering Group took place at CAFRE’s Greenmount campus on Wednesday 29 January 2025.  The second meeting of the Group is scheduled for Monday 17 February 2025 where further work on the development of a bTB delivery plan will take place.   The group will firstly focus on identifying the immediate priorities for action and the steps needed to put them in place. It will then work through the remaining proposals and report to me by the end of March.It is my intention that this plan, which will draw upon the CVO’s proposals alongside actions within the 2022 bTB Eradication Strategy for Northern Ireland, will clearly set out our priorities to be taken forward in the time ahead to tackle the unacceptable levels of bTB in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for a timeline for the introduction of an independent environment protection agency.
answer_text: I am keen to bring forward proposals to enhance environmental governance in Northern Ireland, for consideration by the Executive.There is however a need to fully scope out this issue and review the current and changed landscape post EU exit to ensure the best possible outcomes and public confidence going forward.On 19 November 2024 I announced the panel that will undertake their independent review of environmental governance and report back with their recommendations by summer 2025, with an interim report due in spring.After considering the panel’s recommendations I intend to identify a preferred way forward, which I will take to the Executive by Autumn 2025 before presenting any proposed new arrangements to the Assembly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the actions that have been taken in Lough Neagh and the surrounding watercourses to prevent blue-green algae.
answer_text: The Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan was approved by the NI Executive and published in July 2024. The Action Plan contains science led actions to tackle the blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland. It complements and supports the actions and targets in the Environmental Improvement Plan which is key to taking a strategic approach to the issues facing Lough Neagh in the context of the wider catchment area. I am pleased to report that the implementation of the Action Plan is progressing.I would stress that there are no quick fixes to the issues and it will take many years, if not decades, to see substantial recovery, such is the depth and complexity of the problem at Lough Neagh
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of animals slaughtered due to bovine TB in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Departmental records show that compensation has been paid for cattle slaughtered as part of the bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) Programme, in each of the last 5 years, as per the table below:Year01/04/19-31/03/2001/04/20-31/03/2101/04/21-31/03/2201/04/22-31/03/2301/04/23-31/03/2401/04/24 *31/01/25Total12,86913,43316,09219,57819,01919,221* provisional figuresIn relation to non-bovines, there were three sheep slaughtered in 2021/2022, three sheep slaughtered in 2022/2023, and there was also one alpaca slaughtered in 2020/2021 as part of the bTB Programme.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a breakdown of (i) compensation; and (ii) other costs to his Department due to bovine TB, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Over the last five financial years, the following amounts have been paid to herd owners as compensation for animals slaughtered for the control of bovine Tuberculosis (bTB) in Northern Ireland:YearTotal TB Compensation Paid2019/20£19,481,5772020/21£22,271,5012021/22£28,036,0002022/23£38,008,3702023/24£36,498,440(ii) Over the last five financial years the breakdown of all the costs associated with the control of bTB in Northern Ireland is as follows:2019/20202020/20212021/20222022/20232023/2024Veterinary Service Costs£2,220,832£2,126,320£2,437,068£3,084,977£3,363,814Private Veterinary Practitioner Costs£8,404,458£8,403,761£9,016,000£9,496,168.96£12,235,093.30Compensation£19,481,577£22,271,501£28,036,000 £38,008,370.41 £36,498,440.03 Staff Costs£8,849,702.93£11,743,494.03£12,008,630 £10,635,459.08 £11,749,845.75 Haulier Costs£418,900£420,721£465,722£732,078.25£573,379Miscellaneous£94,150£161,637£64,257£27,546£3,947.54Tuberculin£683,641£805,061£826,588 £1,070,622.79 £959,484Salvage-£4,192,596-£5,114,121 -£7,147,000-£10,394,383.06 -£9,882,040.48 Condemnation Costs£143,845£131,990£156,720£174,820£184,802.45Total Expenditure£36,104,509.93£40,950,364.03£45,863,985£52,835,659.43 £55,686,765.59

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any urgent actions that have been taken with internet providers to repair and reinstate services to customers.
answer_text: Telecommunications is the responsibility of the British Government, with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) holding policy responsibility.		The restoration of connections on the telecommunications network is being led by the private-sector telecoms companies. The Department for the Economy has no contractual relationship with any telecoms company apart from Fibrus Networks. My officials have, however, been working closely with DSIT’s Electronic Communications Resilience and Response Group (EC-RRG) and with the Civil Contingencies Division in the Executive Office, which have been engaging with the telecom companies regarding restoration of the networks. The telecoms companies are working closely with NIE, and services are being restored, where it is safe to do so.	My officials will participate in a post incident review of Storm Éowyn, led by DSIT, which is scheduled to take place next week, and will assess the impacts, lessons learned and future actions for the telecommunications industry and other stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the current number of school crossing patrol officers; and (ii) how this compares to (a) five years ago; and (b) ten years ago.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it does not hold figures on School Crossing Patrols (SCP) prior to 2019/20.  The EA has provided the table below detailing the amount spent on SCPs and the number of SCPs in each of the last five years.Financial Year2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24Annual Cost1£2.862m£2.822m£2.757m£2.759m£2.955mAverage number of SCPs2457449431388365These figures include annual pay awards which have varied from 2.3% in 2021/22 to 12.28% in 2023/24. The number of SCPs in any given year varies and therefore the EA has provided the average for each year. SCPs are automatically replaced within a 2 year period, i.e. someone vacates the post, however if the period is longer a new assessment must be completed to consider any relevant changes within that time. Assessments  are based on the criteria as set out in the Road Safety GB’s ‘School Crossing Patrol Service Guidelines.’

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the adequacy of support for young adults with special educational needs after transitioning from children’s services to adult services.
answer_text: On 13 January 2025, I set out my vision for reform of the provision of support for children with Special Educational Needs (SEN) in the Northern Ireland Assembly.  This included a focus on transitions planning and pathways for young people with SEN leaving education throughout the lifetime of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan.It is clear for young people with SEN that consideration of their future needs will require a collaborative approach across departments to the design and delivery of post-16 and post-19 services, alongside an investment to address the increasing numbers and changing profile of needs.Officials from across the departments of Health (DoH, Education (DE), Communities (DfC) and Economy (DfE) will continue to work together with young people and families to identify improvements, create better ways of working and to share information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the adequacy of support for young adults with special educational needs after transitioning from children’s services to adult services.
answer_text: There is currently no legislation in NI to provide special educational needs pupils with an Education and Health Care plan (EHC) after the age of 19. My officials are engaged with cross-departmental partners to explore potential legislative change. Health and Social Care will support a cohort of children with Special Educational Needs throughout their life in school and transition to adult services. Supports provided are dependent on assessed need, although it is likely that HSC services will have a continued presence in the lives of children with disabilities and their families. This may include day activity, transport, short breaks, accommodation-based support, and self-directed support. My officials have worked closely with Trusts to develop a regional protocol for Transitions to ensure that the supports we provide are regionally consistent. The next phase of this work is to commence a localised pilot ahead of regional scale up. Beyond health and social care, meaningful transition management and support can only be achieved through cross-departmental collaboration and inter-agency working across government. The Departments of Health and Education co-chair a cross-departmental Transitions working group with relevant departments to review and improve transitions for children with Special Educational Needs. To date, the group has conducted a survey, and the outputs will be used to improve the transitions process. This must form the basis of a body of work that aims to provide children with much greater choice and control on their lives after school, while recognising that Health has a pivotal role to ensure care and health needs are supported.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any actions being taken to address the 35 per cent staff vacancy rate in his Department across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: There are some 3000 people on my Departmental headcount and as of Monday 20 January 2025, there are 506 declared vacancies.  My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on my department’s ability to deliver essential services, and to fill vacant posts. NICS HR has responsibility for recruitment for the NICS.  Currently there is supply at most general service grades and we are availing of these, however we continue to experience significant gaps in supply at Professional & Technical (P&T) Specialist grades.  DfI have currently registered circa 28 P&T competitions with NICS HR which are at various levels of development and progression. The DfI People Development & Workforce Planning Directorate (PD&WPD) has been working closely with NICS HR to prioritise recruitment competitions for those grades which are having the most impact on the organisation. This work includes progressing innovative recruitment models like the recent DfI Skills Academy for Trainee Civil Engineering Assistants, and Apprenticeship and Graduate Programmes.There has been some recent improvements in recruitment to the Department, however the Department is not able to recruit at a fast enough pace to keep up with attrition rates and the legacy of historical underfunding.  All measures that we have taken to date to recruit and retain professional people into our organisation are still heavily impacted by external challenges, the most significant issue being rates of pay. As many of our team are professionally qualified, we are competing with the highly competitive private industry to attract and retain professional colleagues. NICS HR are responsible for the pay strategy and are currently leading and developing a pay strategy for NICS.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on his plans to improve road safety at schools; and (ii) whether he is committed to a further roll-out of the part-time 20mph speed limit zones at schools.
answer_text: My Department has developed a draft Active Travel Delivery Plan that includes an update of our policies and design guidance to encourage people to consider active travel for shorter everyday journeys. The work includes development of a toolkit of measures aimed at encouraging more parents and pupils to walk, wheel or cycle to school. Developing safe walking and cycling routes to schools is proposed as a key priority within the Active Travel Delivery Plan that is currently out for public consultation. Consultation on the plan runs until 28 February 2025 and I would encourage the public to take part and help shape the priorities for delivery of active travel infrastructure for the next ten years and beyond.The review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been completed and is available on the Department’s website: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools  The review report recommends that the Department should provide a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work updating our policy document is underway.  Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools will be included within budget planning considerations for next financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the number of service users who have been waiting for domiciliary care packages in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust for (i) one month; (ii) six months; and (iii) more than six months.
answer_text: This information is not collated and therefore cannot be provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20346/ 22-27, to detail the vacant ward capacity that exists across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: There is no vacant ward capacity across all HSC Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what engagement she has had with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs in relation to providing funding to address blue-green algae in Lough Neagh.

To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 12373/22-27, to detail any cooperation that has taken place with (i) criminal justice organisations; and (ii) organised crime task forces internationally, to identify (a) opportunities; and (b) risks with Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: (i)(a) The new Themis contract in NI Courts and Tribunals Service is one example of where co-operation is underway to consider if AI might be used to increase automation of processes and validation of data between Criminal Justice Organisations as part of a range of digital opportunities. Also, in the current discussions around the procurement of a replacement Causeway contract, consideration is being given to how AI might aid the analysis of data between the CJO's in the future.(b) The Department has adopted a NICS policy on the use of AI, this follows an internal approval process to ensure that the risks are fully considered before AI is implemented and that if required appropriate safeguards are in place.   (ii)  There has been no engagement through the Organised Crime Task Force on AI internationally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 12280/22-27, for an update on the development of an Artificial Intelligence policy at his Department.
answer_text: The DAERA Artificial Intelligence policy, entitled "Securely Maximising the Benefits of Artificial Intelligence", was approved on 5th November 2024 and published via the DAERA Intranet. The policy outlines high-level principles and practical guidance for civil servants when considering the use and development of AI products.  It aims to ensure the responsible and ethical use of AI within DAERA and enable effective and secure exploitation of opportunities in the delivery of public services.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he is aware of Universal Credit claims leading to overpayments of Tax Credits due to delays in the Universal Credit claim notification process by his Department to HMRC.
answer_text: HMRC have not advised my Department of any issues relating to Tax Credit overpayments resulting from delays in the Universal Credit claim notification process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what use is being made of vacant ward capacity for geriatric convalescent care in order to free up acute hospital beds.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts have confirmed that they have no vacant ward capacity for geriatric convalescent patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 12283/22-27, to detail the progress to date by the Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre.
answer_text: The Artificial Intelligence Collaboration Centre (AICC) was launched in March 2024. It has since recruited a Director who commenced in August 2024. Its industry led Advisory Board, representing the views of industry stakeholders, has been established, and met formally in December 2024 as has its Technology and Skills Advisory Board which also features industry representatives. AICC has recruited 10 new staff since October 2024 and a new branding and website launched in December 2024.Since March, AICC has held 15 regional events and 160 industry meetings and engagements. The AICC’s Business Transformer Programme, offering up to 20 days of fully funded AI, machine learning, and data science consultancy, has engaged its first 10 businesses with work in progress, and a further 7 are at engagement contract and statement of work negotiation phase. AICC has just opened its regional AI Acceleration Tour series for registrations, empowering SMEs and professionals to get ready to embrace AI innovation in their business, through expert led modules and interactive live sessions. There are currently 6 workshops live for registration on the AICC website, with each workshop serving up to 30 participants. A total of 142 students have matriculated on AICC Post Graduate scholarships across Ulster University and the Queen’s University.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 12375/22-27, to detail any actions that have been taken place with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology and the National Cyber Security Centre to explore the opportunities of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: My department has well-established relationships with both the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) and the National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) which ensures we are well-positioned to understand the opportunities and risks posed by Artificial Intelligence (AI). My officials regularly engage with the Central Digital & Data Office (CDDO), part of DSIT, on AI developments and have used shared learning and code to develop pilot projects within my department. In October 2024, my department arranged for the CDDO to present and host a panel on AI at the recent Technology Forum in Belfast.Similarly, my officials work closely with the NCSC which helps to identify interventions to mitigate cyber security risks in AI and explore opportunities to enhance our cyber resilience using AI. In June 2024, my department updated the security schedules within the terms and conditions of NICS contracts to include supplier compliance with the AI Cyber Security Code of Practice in line with DSIT specifications.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of buildings his Department owns that have renewable energy systems installed, including (i) the name of the building; and (ii) the percentage this is of all buildings owned by his Department.
answer_text: A DfI Accommodation Group has been established and energy management is a standing item on the agenda. The group actively looks at ways to reduce our carbon footprint however not all buildings are currently suited to the introduction of renewable energy. My Department has installed renewable energy schemes in the following buildings which it owns:Lisburn Driver Vehicle Agency (DVA) Test Centre LisburnOmagh DVA Test Centre Downpatrick DVA Test CentreHydebank DVA Test CentreHydebank House DVA HeadquartersRoads Laboratory - Carn Roads DepotOperations & Maintenance Building - Carn Roads DepotThe percentage of buildings owned by the Department with renewable energy sources is 4%.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the number of buildings his Department owns that have renewable energy systems installed, including (i) the name of the building; and (ii) the percentage this is of all buildings owned by his Department.
answer_text: The Department for the Economy does not own any buildings, and the DfE Office Estate is based in Department of Finance owned or leased properties.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline what steps his Department is taking to address job vacancies caused by a skills shortage.

To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of buildings her Department owns that have renewable energy systems installed, including (i) the name of the building; and (ii) the percentage this is of all buildings owned by her Department.
answer_text: Ten of the seventy buildings my Department owns (14%) have renewable energy systems installed. The buildings are: Academy House Castle Cottages and StablesClare House Colby House Glenree House Inishkeen House Jubilee House (Ballykelly)83 Ladas Drive, BelfastLoughryOrchard House As part of my Department’s Office Estate Strategy we are committed to improving energy efficiency across our portfolio.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, pursuant to AQW 12376/22-27, to detail any actions that have been taken place with the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology and the National Cyber Security Centre to explore the opportunities of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: The office of the Chief Scientific and Technology Adviser (CSTA) has regular engagements with DSIT covering a range of topics, including the future application of AI to improve the efficiency and quality of services across government, the public sector and private enterprise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures being taken to address staff shortages in DfI Roads Western Division.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government. This has had a significant impact on my Department’s ability to deliver essential services, and to fill vacant posts.While NICS HR has overall responsibility for NICS recruitment, DfI supports this to ensure that, as far as is possible, the right people with the right skills are in the right posts within the Department. My Department has taken a number of steps to address the high vacancy rate including working with NICS HR to progress a range of recruitment competitions; the establishment of a People Development & Workforce Planning Directorate and agreement of a Workforce Plan to focus efforts on recruitment to fill critical vacancies. Over the last year the Department has taken forward recruitment campaigns to provide career pathways into DfI through Graduate, Apprenticeships and the pilot DfI Professional & Technical Skills Academy which, has been successful in placing suitable candidates in key areas such as road and private streets inspections.  Further external recruitment campaigns for the Principal Professional and Technical Officer (PPTO) and Higher Professional and Technical Officer (HPTO) have recently been launched, and these competitions will be used to fill further vacancies within the organisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans his Department has to address the lack of electric vehicle charge points between Omagh and Cookstown.
answer_text: The development of publicly accessible Electric Vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity, the provision of which rests solely with Charge Point Operators (CPOs). Individual CPOs are best placed to advise if they have plans to install further charge points within their wider networks. My officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters. There has been significant investment from CPOs in EV infrastructure over the last 18 months. The commercial publicly accessible charge point network has over 680 chargers now in place here, this represents a growth in EV charging provision of 75% since April 2023. The Rapid/Ultra Rapid charging public network, which offers the fastest, most convenient method of charging has grown by over 300% here in the same period.   Confidence in the market is very encouraging, with nearly 30,000 EVs on our roads and more than 20 CPOs providing key services to EV drivers at multiple sites across the north including shopping centres, hotels and on the strategic road networkMy Department has committed over £470,000 match funding to support applications from Councils to the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme of which just over £133,500 has yet to be drawn down by Councils. This scheme led by Derry City and Strabane District Council on behalf of nine Local Councils aims to provide a further 127 charge points across those Council areas. Individual Councils are best placed to advise on the number of additional charge points this will provide in their area.My officials continue to engage with officials from the British Government’s Office for Zero Emission Vehicles on grants and funding available for EV Infrastructure here. I am pleased to confirm that the north has now had it’s 1,000th workplace charge point installed through the Workplace Charging Scheme and a further 109 charge points have also been installed in ‘Staff and Fleet Carparks’ under the Electric Vehicle Charge Point Grant Scheme. These charge points provide additional options for charging to EV owners and further compliment the growing publicly accessible charge point network.Through the Shared Island Fund we will soon have additional charging facilities at nearly 50 sports clubs across the north, 7 of which are in County Tyrone bringing new access to charging to the heart of our communities.  Further details on this scheme can be found at: Shared Island Sports Club EV Charging Scheme - Pobal

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any representations he has made to the UK's Department for International Trade in relation to the interests of the north’s agricultural sector in future trade negotiations.
answer_text: While the Department for the Economy (DfE) is responsible for liaising with the Department for Business and Trade (DBT) in respect of international trade agreements, the main point of UK Government contact for the agricultural aspects of the trade negotiations is the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra).  I can confirm that the Northern Ireland position on agri-food matters has been made clear to the UK Government, namely that trade agreements must not see any change in UK SPS regulations or lowering of standards required of imports and tariff protection retained for sensitive agricultural products, particularly beef, sheep, dairy, pigs and poultry.I will where appropriate, liaise with the Minister for the Economy and make representations to the UK Government to ensure that the interests of the Northern Ireland agricultural sector are considered in UK Government trade negotiations.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any measures his Department has in place to support the mental health of isolated workers in rural communities.
answer_text: My Department’s Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework provides a mechanism to fund initiatives which address poverty, isolation and loneliness and improve the physical and mental health and well-being of isolated workers in rural communities. The Farm Families Health Check Programme (FFHCP) in conjunction with the Public Health Agency and the Northern Health and Social Care Trust provides a comprehensive physical and mental health screening and advice service to rural people who attend a range of community events, and farmers who visit livestock markets, that the screening vehicle attends across Northern Ireland. Where necessary referrals are then made to General Practitioners or appropriate support services. Also, the Rural Support charity, provide a range of programmes and services for farmers and farm family members in support of their personal wellbeing. Their objective is to reduce emotional distress and help alleviate poor mental health triggers.My Department also has a long-standing funding relationship with the Young Farmers’ Clubs of Ulster (YFCU), through which they promote good health and wellbeing for young people in rural areas of Northern Ireland. A key element is to facilitate good health and wellbeing among the young people, and this is brought about through the YFCU’s engagement with the Agri-Rural Health Forum and also the training of Young Farmers Ambassadors to raise awareness of mental and emotional heath.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how his Department (i) monitors; and (ii) recognises the work carried out by (a) farmers; and (b) other land managers undertaking measures to reduce their carbon output.
answer_text: My Department is seeking to ensure that as many of the new policy interventions as possible being introduced through the Farm Support and Development Programme (FSDP) help to drive down the carbon footprint of the agricultural sector. Within the FSDP, the Carbon Footprinting Project will provide a baseline whole farm carbon footprint to all farm businesses in Northern Ireland in a standardised and consistent way. Training will also be provided to farm businesses to help them to understand their carbon footprint reports and identify actions they can take to reduce emissions. Monitoring of Project participants will be undertaken to assess their increased awareness of managing their net carbon emissions to achieve reductions. In addition, the FSDP’s Monitoring and Evaluation Framework will provide evidence on an annual basis on progress of achievement of performance indicators in relation to reduction in net greenhouse gas emissions from NI Agriculture and Land Use, Land Use Change and Forestry.The FSDP will promote more efficient, lower carbon and sustainable farming practices through a mix of incentivisation, innovation, and enhanced farmer knowledge and training, with a firm focus on data to help with decision making.It is planned that participation in the Carbon Footprinting Project will be a one of the conditionalities for receipt of the new Farm Sustainability Payment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration she is giving to addressing agricultural equipment theft by introducing legislation similar to the Equipment Theft (Prevention) Act 2023 in England and Wales.
answer_text: A partnership approach is central to addressing crime, including agricultural crime. My Department chairs the Rural Crime Partnership with members including the Police Service of Northern Ireland, the Ulster Farmers’ Union, NFU Mutual, the Young Farmers’ Club of Ulster, the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs and Policing and Community Safety Partnerships. The partnership shares information on emerging or reoccurring crimes, and will continue to do so, to support a coordinated response and preventative approach to addressing issues such as agricultural equipment theft. The Equipment Theft (Prevention) Act 2023 in England and Wales primarily aims to prevent the theft of commercial or agricultural equipment by requiring specified equipment to be fitted with an immobiliser or a visible marking, and a power to make regulations on the recording of sales of such equipment.Following devolution of justice matters in 2010, authority for the development and introduction of criminal and civil offences and penalties was devolved to each Northern Ireland Assembly Department. It, therefore, falls to the Department owning the overarching policy area to consider whether there are gaps in the law or amendments required to existing legislation and, where that is the case, responsibility for any subsequent legislative changes remains the responsibility of that Department. It follows that it would be open to the Department for the Economy to provide details of any specific policies that have been established to support the sale/resale of equipment, regardless of whether it is for agricultural or commercial purposes.In that context, as Minister of Justice, I do not have any plans to introduce similar legislation to that identified; however, my officials would, of course, be willing to assist any other Executive department with regards the appropriateness of any penalties attached to such offences as they may wish to create.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how he is encouraging young people to (i) stay in the farming sector; and (ii) see farming and agriculture as a potential career.
answer_text: The College of Agriculture Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), through its delivery of education, knowledge transfer and innovation programmes plays a key role in educating people entering or currently working within the farming industry. Currently there is 1160 learners enrolled on agriculture education programmes at CAFRE, with knowledge transfer and innovation programmes delivered to over 13,000 people working within the NI agri-food industry on an annual basis.During October 2024 I launched the Farming for the Generations pilot Scheme, which aims to raise awareness of the need for succession planning on farms to help ensure the long-term sustainability of the farm business and bring the next generation into the farm business. Each year to highlight the wide range of job roles available within the agriculture industry, my officials at CAFRE host a careers event attended by agri-food businesses which promotes the wide range of opportunities open to CAFRE students enrolled on agriculture education programmes.In additional CAFRE's Student Engagement Team also promote opportunities in agriculture to school pupils, careers teachers and subject specific teachers in many of Northern Ireland's 190 post primary schools. CAFRE is also a support partner of the ABP Angus Youth Challenge programme which builds young people’s knowledge of agri-food production. Each year, CAFRE staff provide mentoring and curriculum support to finalist school teams.To encourage new entrants into the industry open days are hosted at CAFRE twice per year, at which hundreds of prospective students and their families find out more about the opportunities to study programmes leading to careers in the agriculture sector.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action he is taking to support farmers who are affected by accumulative heavy rainfall, which is reportedly damaging crops and impacting the lambing season.
answer_text: Technical advice and support to farmers is provided by the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), through a range of different forums including press articles, online and face to face meetings and through the CAFRE website. Farmers and growers requiring advice on managing the impacts of difficult weather conditions should contact their local CAFRE Adviser.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of hectares of agricultural land that have been taken out of use for farming in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs does not collect data on agricultural land that has been taken out of use for farming.  The table below shows the total area farmed, as reported in the annual farm census in each of the last ten years. Annual Farm CensusTotal Area Farmed (Ha)2015997,74820161,013,24820171,019,73620181,022,39520191,023,16320201,029,82220211,035,64220221,043,27020231,042,31820241,040,392Total area farmed has increased by 4.3% in the ten years from 2015 to 2024.  New methodologies were adopted to create the farm sample and improve the quality of estimates in 2020. The change in total area farmed of 0.7% between 2019 and 2020 suggests changes had negligible effect on the land area variables.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether dairy produce from cows fed with Bovaer is safe for human consumption.
answer_text: Milk from cows given Bovaer, a feed additive used to reduce methane emissions, is safe to drink. The additive is metabolised by the cows so does not pass into the milk.All feed additives that are authorised for use within the EU, including Northern Ireland, are subject to a scientific assessment by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) on the safety and efficacy of that additive specific to the proposed terms of use. In its report, EFSA concluded that use of the Bovaer additive in the feed of dairy cows poses no concern for the safety of consumers of dairy products.Bovaer has also undergone rigorous safety assessments as part of the Food Standard Agency’s market authorisation process and is approved for use in Great Britain. The Food Standards Agency and Food Standards Scotland safety assessment concluded there are no safety concerns when Bovaer is used at the approved dose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any analysis that has been carried out into food from animals that have been fed the Bovaer additive.
answer_text: All authorised feed additives undergo rigorous safety assessment by the Food Standards Agency (FSA) and Food Standards Scotland (FSS). This ensures products are safe and appropriate for the UK market. Bovaer has been authorised for use in the UK for all milk producing and breeding ruminant animals by Ministers across the nations based on the FSA/FSS recommendations and supporting evidence. It is also authorised for use in the EU for dairy and breeding cows, following a similar risk assessment by the European Food Safety Authority. Experts in the UK and EU agree that its use poses no safety concerns to consumers, animals or the environment when used as instructed.In summary, FSA has concluded that milk from cows given Bovaer, a feed additive used to reduce methane emissions, is safe to drink and note that the additive is metabolised by the cows so does not pass into the milk.  Further information is provided by the FSA in the link below:https://food.blog.gov.uk/2024/12/05/bovaer-cow-feed-additive-explained/
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 18567/22-27, to detail the number of occasions that the (i) ozone demand was not met; and (ii) ozone was overdosed at the Moyola Water Treatment Works in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The information is not held by my department. Although the Drinking Water Inspectorate consider it good practice, there is no regulatory requirement for NI Water to measure ozone demand, and subsequently no requirement to provide data to the Inspectorate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Executive colleagues have made him aware of any capital budget commitments which will be impacted by issues with the water and wastewater infrastructure.
answer_text: I frequently discuss wastewater challenges with my Executive colleagues. This year, I have provided NI Water with resource and capital budget allocation of c.0.5bn and I want to reinforce that it is the responsibility of the Board of NI Water to ensure that the funding that has been made available, is suitably prioritised to maximise investment.I acknowledge the significant financial pressures NI Water is operating under; however, it is imperative that NI Water works with this funding, prioritises accordingly, and not approach issues with a blanket response that finances are always to blame.NI Water has advised that approximately 18,500 properties can be offered connections if the developer self-funds action to offset stormwater entering the wastewater system. In addition, a fully funded PC21 investment plan would enable a further 4,500 new properties to connect to the wastewater infrastructure.I am fully committed to resolving the current wastewater capacity challenges and am taking a three-pronged approach to this matter.  Firstly, I continue to work with my Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I am bringing forward new legislation in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which will help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, I have asked my officials to examine the legislative change required to bring forward developer contributions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any progress that has been made to reduce Corporation Tax in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department does not have the ability to reduce the rate of Corporation Tax in the North. These powers have not been devolved to the Executive.My position remains that the devolution and reduction of Corporation Tax can only occur if it is affordable and furthers economic development, and will only be worthwhile if it ensures that ordinary citizens benefit through the creation of sustainable, well-paying jobs. I negotiated the Interim Fiscal Framework earlier this year which committed the British Government to further consideration of fiscal devolution. However, my department is currently prioritising those elements of the Interim Fiscal Framework in relation to our funding arrangements which critically need to be progressed in the near-term. Once these elements are sufficiently advanced, I will bring my proposals to the Executive on the additional fiscal powers we might seek and negotiate for this with the British Government. These proposals will be informed by the recommendations of the independent Fiscal Commission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the provision of a new centre for mental health at Antrim Area Hospital.
answer_text: The provision of a new Mental Health Centre at the Antrim Area Hospital Site is currently at the conclusion of RIBA Design Stage 3, awaiting a positive AEDET review, prior to final sign off by the Northern Health & Social Care Trust and Health Estates. The Department of Health is currently in receipt of an OBC addendum which, when approved, will facilitate progression to RIBA Stage 4 and subsequent tender.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any action taken to assess increased water flow to the River Moyola as a result of major road schemes in the area.
answer_text: The A6 Randalstown Castledawson Dualling is a major road project which crosses the Moyola River, constructed between 2017 and 2021.  During the early scheme assessment stages, the potential impact on the water environment within the vicinity of the proposed road upgrade was assessed in detail.  A computer-based highway drainage design model was developed and my department also implemented the highway drainage design philosophy principles of Sustainable Urban Drainage Systems as set out in the Sustainable Urban Drainage Design Manual for Scotland and Northern Ireland (SUDS Manual).  The drainage infrastructure associated with the A6 upgrade works has resulted in no increased water flow from the road infrastructure to the River Moyola.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the steps his Department will take to prevent future flooding on Kings Row, Coalisland; and (ii) any compensation that will be made available to residents who had recent flood damage to their property.
answer_text: Following a period of very heavy rainfall associated with Storm Bert, the river drainage infrastructure on the Mousestown Stream, Coalisland became overwhelmed. The open watercourse enters a culvert via an inlet screen near the junction of Annagher Road and Stewartstown Road. The river surcharged at the mouth of this culvert and it overflowed. I am advised that this overflowing of the Mousetown Drain contributed to the flooding suffered by residents along Kings Row.My Department was proactive across the weekend, responding to over 600 calls for assistance across a wide geographical area. Preventative screen maintenance was undertaken across the North in the days prior to this event to help alleviate the impacts of flooding.  This preventative work contributed to hundreds of homes being protected.  Unfortunately, a number of residents across the North experienced flooding inside their homes including those at Kings Row, Coalisland. My Department’s Rivers Directorate will continue its inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure within the Coalisland area. If an inspection determines that there is a blockage significantly impeding the free flow of water within the watercourse or that drainage infrastructure is in an unsatisfactory condition, then works will be programmed.I can confirm that I have instructed officials to review my Department's response to this event, and the feasibility of any viable flood alleviation measures at this location will also be reviewed.The Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance was introduced by the Executive in 2007. Its purpose is to provide immediate financial support to households who have suffered from flooding. It is not a compensation scheme and does not purport to be one; nor is it intended to replace or displace the assistance that households should receive from their insurers.The Minister for Communities activated the Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance from 22 November 2024 to 21 December 2024. An immediate payment of £1,000 is made via district councils to provide eligible householders with assistance to make their homes habitable as quickly as possible..One other practicable option for domestic property owners is to avail of the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme run by my Department. Successful applicants receive 90% of the costs (up to £10,000) to modify their properties to make them more resistant to flooding. Details on how homeowners can apply are available on my Department’s website at: Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme | Department for Infrastructure

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any consideration that has been given to establishing a cross-departmental body to co-ordinate action on the governance of Lough Neagh.
answer_text: I recognise the need for coordinated cross-departmental action and collaboration to achieve successful delivery of the actions in the Lough Neagh Action Plan and my officials have established a Lough Neagh Programme Board to oversee delivery and governance.In addition, I recognise the necessity of wider collaboration to build consensus with the public and private sectors, including local government, and the community. To assist with this, I have established and will be chairing the first quarterly Lough Neagh Stakeholder Forum on 12 December 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

To ask the Assembly Commission whether it plans to extend or enhance the work of the Education Service to encourage more participation by young people in democracy education.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Northern Ireland Environment Agency 2023 investigation into the high level of consumer contacts on the taste and odour of the mains water supply and contraventions of the taste and odour parameters in the final water from Moyola Wastewater Treatment Works is still ongoing.
answer_text: The Drinking Water Inspectorate (DWI) investigated this water quality event through its normal procedures and can confirm this investigation is now complete. The outcome of this investigation was provided in AQW 18567/22-27.
