question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail any rural needs assessment or rural proofing that was carried out on the draft Programme for Government 2024-2027 before it was agreed by the Executive.
answer_text: A Rural Needs Impact Assessment (RNIA) was completed on the draft Programme for Government and published alongside the draft PfG.  It is currently out for a 12-week consultation alongside the Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) and Child Rights Impact Assessment (CRIA).  This consultation closes on 2nd December. The Public Consultation on the draft PfG and the associated draft impact assessments can be found here - http://www.northernireland.gov.uk/PfG . The Rural Needs Impact Assessment will be updated if required following the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the outcomes of the Drinking Water Inspectorate’s audits of (i) the controls in place to manage the blue-green algae risk in October 2023; and (ii) the Moyola Wastewater Treatment Works in January 2024.
answer_text: Following the audit of the controls in place to manage the blue-green algae risk in October 2023, the Drinking Water Inspectorate made six recommendations to Northern Ireland Water as follows:Identification of other hazards, or hazardous events, relating to algae should be considered; Additional monitoring of raw water quality should be put in place to improve assessment of risk in relation to algae; Additional risk control measures should be considered to lower the likelihood of hazardous events and/or the severity of consequences of the hazards in relation to algae; Sampling of raw water should be extended to periods outside of the algae season; Frequency of operational monitoring of final water for algae counts, Geosmin, MIB, and Microcystin-LR should be increased and be more consistent; andTurnaround time for Microcystin-LR analysis should be improved. All six of these recommendations were accepted by, and have been addressed by, NI Water. The audit of Moyola WTW in January 2024 did not identify any significant issues with the treatment process. However, the following one relevant recommendation was made by DWI Consideration should be given to verifying the ozone demand has been met and ensuring ozone is not overdosed. Ozone is important in oxidising Microcystin-LR which is a toxin produced by cyanobacteria (blue/green algae). This recommendation has been accepted by NI Water.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any wastewater treatment works facilities that are not capable of delivering the rate of flow they were designed for (i) within the Lough Neagh catchment area; and (ii) across Northern Ireland as a whole.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:	The measurement of flow is not currently part of NI Water’s regulatory compliance regime and, therefore, NI Water does not have flow monitoring equipment installed widely across its wastewater infrastructure. 		However, the Public Private Partnership (PPP) operated wastewater sites have the necessary flow monitoring equipment installed, and NI Water has confirmed that all PPP operated sites can deliver and treat the prescribed rates of flow, except in conditions of mechanical or electrical failure, as permitted within the respective Water Order Consents, issued by the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health why eligibility for health service-funded IVF, and related treatments, is restricted to instances where the woman has a body mass index of at least 19.0kg/m2 and up to and including 30.0kg/m2.
answer_text: The BMI criterion for the provision of publicly-funded IVF treatment in Northern Ireland states that a woman should have a BMI of at least 19.0kg/m2 and up to and including 30.0kg/m2. This reflects the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Guideline CG156, Fertility problems: assessment and treatment, which was endorsed by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of people that have (i) applied; (ii) been interviewed; and (iii) been accepted for positions as Soil Sampling Technicians in the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme during the current recruitment process.
answer_text: The Soil sample collection contractor RPS Tetratech Group have confirmed that this year:  1,374 have applied for Soil Sampling Technician positions;894 have been interviewed; and222 have accepted positions with RPS Tetratech Group.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she will use the Barnett consequential of the 40 per cent rates relief scheme for independent retailers and other high street businesses in England to provide appropriate rates relief for high street businesses in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 17794/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to introduce legislation to provide improved labelling of country of origin on jars of honey.
answer_text: The Food Standards Agency (FSA) in Northern Ireland holds policy responsibility for food compositional standards and labelling. Current rules require that all food information and labelling, including country-of-origin information, shall not mislead the consumer.There are specific rules covering country-of-origin labelling for honey. A person may not trade in honey unless the country-of-origin where the honey has been harvested is indicated on the label. Currently, blends of honey from more than one country may be labelled as ‘blend of EC honeys’, ‘blend of non-EC honeys’, or ‘blend of EC and non-EC honeys.’ The EU has published a Directive introducing new rules on country-of-origin labelling for honey blends, which will apply to honey producers from 14 June 2026. The FSA is working towards transposing the new requirements by 14 December 2025, subject to the Northern Ireland legislative process. The main mandatory change relates to increased transparency in origin labelling of honey blends. The new rules require food businesses to list all countries-of-origin, in descending order of weight including the percentage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure that honey sold here is genuine and not imported, adulterated honey.
answer_text: The Food Standards Agency (FSA) in Northern Ireland has policy responsibility for the Honey Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015, with the enforcement undertaken by district councils. The Food Safety (Northern Ireland) Order 1991, which is also enforced by district councils, stipulates that it is an offence to sell any food ‘which is not of the nature or substance or quality demanded by the purchaser.’The FSA gathers intelligence relating to food fraud and where there is credible evidence of food crime, works with district councils and other enforcement agencies to investigate the matter and take appropriate action in relation to the safety and authenticity of food.The UK has a wide-ranging programme of activities on honey authenticity and associated methodology and the programme works in close collaboration with industry and academic experts in honey, the outputs of which are published and freely accessible.The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs is responsible for conducting import controls on Products of Animal Origin, including honey, on entry to Northern Ireland in line with Regulation (EU) No. 2019/2129.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the comments made by the Tánaiste at the British Irish Association conference in September 2024 that a root and branch reform of the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information Retrieval is needed.
answer_text: As the Member will be aware, I have previously made clear my concerns about the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023, which marked a significant departure from the Stormont House Agreement, blocked access to justice for many people, and caused distress and anxiety for many people.It remains unclear what the UK Government’s commitment to repeal and replace the Act will mean in practice. I note the Secretary of State is currently engaging with a range of stakeholders and I would urge him to listen closely and carefully to victims, survivors and their families before determining next steps on the Independent Commission for Reconciliation and Information Recovery.I am due to meet the Secretary of State on 28 November to discuss legacy and I will take that opportunity to seek clarity and to reiterate my concerns about the Act to him. I remain unconvinced that any reform can make the ICRIR acceptable to families of victims.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will implement the 5.5 per cent pay increase for health workers as recommended by the Pay Review Body, that is being paid in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: I am already on record as being supportive, in principle, of the recommendations set out in the report. However, given the severe financial constraints placed upon my Department, it is not possible, at the current time, to make a final decision on the acceptance of the review body recommendations for this year.As I have stated publicly, our health service would be nothing without our hard-working workforce and I am committed to doing everything I can do to support them.  I will continue to make the case, to Executive colleagues, for additional financial allocations that would allow me to implement a pay award in line with the recommendations of the NHSPRB for 24/25.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, as part of their work on international relations, in relation to securing investment, trade and tourism, whether they are aware of the use of Belfast International Airport runway by United States Military aircraft.
answer_text: We do not hold any information in relation to this matter.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, as part of their work on international relations, in relation to securing investment, trade and tourism, whether they are aware of the use of Belfast International Airport runway by United States Military aircraft to transport material and military personnel to Israel and the South-West Asia and North Africa region.
answer_text: We do not hold any information in relation to this matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail what consideration has been given to the Council of Europe Committee of Experts Report on the implementation of the European Charter for Regional Minority Languages, published 19th September 2024, which recommends the development and implementation of a strategy for the recruitment of teachers in co-operation with speakers, including the training of teachers for children with special needs within the Irish medium education sector; and (ii) how does he intend to take these actions forward.
answer_text: I acknowledge the findings of the report, and I am aware these issues have been prevalent in the Irish-medium Education sector for some time. Given the ongoing shortage of subject-specialist teachers, particularly in Irish-medium post-primary schools, My Department has requested that Irish-medium Initial Teacher Education course providers should consider how their Irish-medium places might be prioritised towards students who have expressed a preference to teach in Irish-medium post-primary settings within Northern Ireland.In June 2024, officials from the Department’s Teacher Education and Professional Learning team conducted a survey of schools across Northern Ireland to assess the challenges faced in recruiting and retaining teachers. Of the 287 schools that responded, only two were Irish-medium primary schools, and no Irish-medium post-primary schools participated. The survey findings did not reveal any new insights into the sector’s challenges in securing qualified Irish-medium teachers.To address workforce planning needs, the Education Authority, in collaboration with DE and other education partners, is hosting an Education Workforce Planning Conference on 4 December 2024. The event will engage key stakeholders in discussions about the overall needs of the education sector. It is anticipated that these discussions will include the challenges faced by the Irish-medium Education sector.I am aware of the gaps in Special Education Needs (SEN) support services and special education provision in the Irish-medium. My Department is working with the Education Authority to identify solutions to the specific challenges faced by the sector. A working group has been established to make progress on this issue. Furthermore, officials from my Department have engaged with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) and the Irishmedium Education sector through the End-to-End Review of SEN. The Review is in the closing stage and proposals have been drafted which include addressing the specific support needs of children and young people with SEN in Irish-medium Education.Officials in my Department are in the early stages of commencing an Irish-medium Strategy, which will hope to address the concerns of this report and those of the wider sector.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) what consideration has been given to the Council of Europe Committee of Experts Report on the implementation of the European Charter for Regional Minority Languages, published 19th September 2024, which recommends the full implementation of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 and the Irish language strategy; and (ii) whether these legal duties will be included in the Programme for Government.
answer_text: TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act) for which it is responsible, namely the establishment of the new bodies namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.The Act also places a duty on the Department of Education (DE) and repeals The Administration of Justice (Language) Act (Ireland) 1737, which is a matter for the Department of Justice. These provisions have not yet been commenced by the UK Government.The Department for Communities (DfC) remains policy lead for language policy, including the development of the Ulster Scots, Language, Heritage and Culture strategy and the Irish Language strategy.  Consultation on the draft Programme for Government closed on Monday, 4th November. Work is ongoing to analyse all consultation responses and to provide the Executive with a robust consultation report. Further decisions on new policies and projects being included within the Programme for Government will be taken once the report is complete, and Ministers have had the opportunity to consider the findings of the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any specific consideration that has been given to the safety concerns raised by GPs in relation to the dispersal of patients from Fairhill GP practice, Magherafelt; and (ii) any assessment his Department has made of its own exposure to liability in the event that a patient comes to harm due to the pressures placed on receiving practices during this process.
answer_text: All avenues to secure an alternative contractor for Fairhill have now been explored and exhausted and the position is that the practice list of Fairhill Health Centre will be allocated across the neighbouring practices in order to ensure that patients continue to have access to a GP.  From a safe practice perspective, allocating the patient list for one single-handed practice across 11 practices significantly reduces any potential impact on the receiving practices. The redistribution of the patient list has been managed to ensure that the highest level of increase in patient numbers will be circa 3.2%. Furthermore, to reduce additional workload for these practices, during the first three months of the new arrangement SPPG will put in place a ‘freeze’ on other patients moving practices within the local area. The challenges being faced by single-handed GP practices in securing locum cover when the single partner is unwell or on leave are of concern across the UK and the managed integration of this patient list into multi-partner practices will assist in addressing that concern.Throughout this process my Department has considered the continuation of care of registered patients and have recommended the best approach to limit the impact on their ongoing needs for access to GP services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the imminent dispersal of 2,330 patients from Fairhill GP Practice, Magherafelt, to surrounding GP practices in a short time frame, (i) how his Department plans to ensure that adequate financial and workforce resources are provided to receiving practices to safely manage the increased demand; and (ii) given the existing pressures on primary care services and concerns about patient safety, whether additional funding per patient will be allocated to these practices to ensure the safe and sustainable delivery of care.
answer_text: The Department of Health’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) will redistribute the financial resources from the Fairhill Health Centre contract across the 11 receiving practices in proportion to their list increase and in line with General Medical Services regulations. In addition, SPPG will allocate extra funding to the 11 practices to support the administrative tasks involved in integrating patient records. All new funding will facilitate practices in recruiting and retaining additional clinical, managerial and administrative staff to assist them in this process. The redistribution of the patient list has been managed to ensure that the highest level of increase in patient numbers will be circa 3.2%. Furthermore, to reduce additional workload for these practices, during the first three months of the new arrangement SPPG will put in place a ‘freeze’ on other patients moving practices within the local area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any conflicts have been identified between (i) the October 2024 consultation Hospitals – Creating a Network for Better Outcomes; and (ii) the Northern Health and Social Care Trust Corporate Plan 2024-25 - 2027-28.
answer_text: ‘Hospitals – Creating a Network for Better Outcomes’ is intended as a high-level strategic framework within which decisions on specific services can be made by individual Health and Social Care Trusts.  The Health and Social Care Trusts were instrumental in developing the consultation document, indeed the content was co-produced with the Trusts and overseen by a task & finish group which involved the Trust Chief Executives.I do not believe that there are any conflicts between ‘Hospitals – Creating a Network for Better Outcomes’ and the Northern Health and Social Care Trust Corporate Plan 2024-25 – 2027-28. For example, both documents are clear in setting out the ambition to make Causeway Hospital an elective and diagnostic hub and to expand same day emergency care. Both documents are also consistent in reaffirming that a 24/7 Emergency Department is to be maintained at Causeway Hospital.I encourage any feedback in response to the consultation, including where there is any perceived conflict with other documents.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 9039/22-27, to detail the most recent figures for the number of applicants that have been on the housing waiting list for (i) 1-2 years; (ii) 2-3 years; (iii) 3-4 years; (iv) 4-5 years; and (v) more than 5 years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 16991/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when he will publish the outcome of his scoping exercise on strengthening environmental governance.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the measures being taken to ensure the safety and care of patients from Fairhill Health Centre, Magherafelt, following its closure on 1 November 2024; and (ii) whether he will ensure that any solution implemented does not affect the remaining GP practices or compromise the overall stability of primary care services in the region.
answer_text: Following an extensive process to identify a solution to allow Fairhill Health Centre to remain open, no viable solution has been reached.  The only option now is to disperse the patient list across 13 other GP practices in the Mid-Ulster area.  The patients will be allocated to one of the 13 practices ensuring the continuation of care for each patient on the list and health care records will be transferred to the receiving practice. Given the patient list size and the fact that it will be dispersed over 13 practices the number of patients to be received by each practice will not be excessive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when a new Commissioner for Victims and Survivors will be appointed.
answer_text: We have instructed our officials to formally proceed with the recruitment process for a new Commissioner for Victims and Survivors.The competition was launched on 11 November and it is hoped that the new Commissioner will be appointed in early 2025.The Commission for Victims and Survivors is regulated by the Commissioner for Public Appointments (CPANI) and, as such, the appointment process must comply with the Code of Practice for Ministerial Public Appointments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs has an assessment been made of whether Mr David Campbell’s recent representations as Chair of the Loyalist Communities Council are compliant with the Agri-Food and BioSciences Institute Board Member Code of Conduct and the Seven Principles Underpinning Public Life.
answer_text: All publicly appointed board members, including those to AFBI, are bound by the terms and conditions of their appointment and once appointed, all AFBI board members must confirm, in writing, their acceptance of the AFBI Board Member Code of Conduct.As the Member will appreciate, it would not be appropriate for me to comment on individual cases but I can offer assurance that my Department will not hesitate to take action where there is evidence of non-compliance with the relevant Code of Conduct.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs which of the recommendations contained in the 2020 Report on the Review of the Implementation of the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 have been implemented by (i) his Department; (ii) the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute; and (iii) all public authorities.
answer_text: My Department undertook a review of the implementation of the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 (the Act) in 2019. A report of the findings of the Rural Needs Working Group contained 18 recommendations for DAERA, one for Agri-food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) and six for all public authorities. Of the recommendations assigned to DAERA, 16 have been fully implemented. These are recommendations 1-4, 6, 8, 9, 11, 15, 17-19 and 22-25. Recommendation 12 has been partially implemented but further research will be taken forward to implement the recommendation in full. Recommendation 21 has not been implemented yet, but it will be considered as part of future plans to complete a full review of the Act.The recommendations which related to all public authorities (recommendations 5,7,10,13,14 and 20) have been fully implemented by DAERA.  DAERA also shared the recommendations with all other public authorities listed in the Schedule to the Act. AFBI has implemented recommendation 16. I recognise that there is still scope for more to be done to help ensure that the Rural Needs Act (NI) 2016 delivers better outcomes for rural communities and I am keen that the Act is more widely reviewed now that it has been in operation for seven years. I have asked my officials to consider this in the development of new evidence-based rural policy proposals, which will be subject to public consultation next year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any research his Department has (i) undertaken; and (ii) funded, into the impact of the Covid-19 lockdown on the mental health of the population, broken down by age group.
answer_text: The challenges faced by the population as result of the Covid-19 pandemic and the restrictions on everyday life are well documented and have significantly impacted mental health. It created very specific challenges to the psychological wellbeing and mental health of the whole population. Various reviews/studies have been commissioned or supported by my Department and its arms-length bodies.In acknowledgment of the extra pressure placed on the population’s mental health during the pandemic, the Mental Health Action Plan – published by my Department on 19 May 2020 – included a dedicated Covid-19 Mental Health Response Plan. The Plan can be viewed at www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/mental-health-action-plan. A key commitment made in this plan was to the development of the Mental Health Strategy 2021-2031, which was published in June 2021.In 2020, the Mental Health and Emotional Wellbeing Surge Cell convened by the then Health and Social Care Board in order to provide evidence to the Department of Health, prepared a review paper to consolidate the research, knowledge and evidence on the impact of Covid-19 on key areas of mental health and emotional wellbeing and the likelihood of new inceptions of mental illness. It includes recommendations for ameliorating these – i.e. prevention, early intervention and recovery – and details priorities for further research. It examines the evidence relating to the whole population and includes sections relating to children and adolescents and older people, as well as other vulnerable groups. The report is available online at  www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/mh-impact-covid-pandemic.pdf. In addition to the above review, the Department worked with the Mental Health Foundation and Queen’s University Belfast to produce the International Policy Guidance and Responses to Covid-19 Mental Health Recovery Rapid Review. This is also available online: www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/international-policy-covid19.pdf. The main aim was to identify, analyse and present evidence to inform the response to mental health needs arising from, and/or being exacerbated by, the pandemic. While not undertaken by the Department, the Public Health Agency (PHA) Research and Development (R&D) Division commissioned a call for rapid response research on Covid-19; a summary of papers and links to their reports is available online at https://research.hscni.net/covid-19-rapid-response-funding-call. A number of these had a mental health focus. All of the mental health and wellbeing papers explore the impact of Covid-19 on adults, but a number – especially related to self-harm/suicidal ideation – also include people aged 10 and over. Analyses of self-harm presentations (for ages 10+) can also be found as part of the PHA’s Self-Harm Registry for the relevant periods atwww.publichealth.hscni.net/publications/northern-ireland-registry-self-harm-regional-report-202021-and-202122. PHA R&D

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline when he will consult on the regulations to establish a Just Transition Commission.
answer_text: I envisage that proposals around the establishment of the Just Transition Commission will be published for an 8-week consultation in October 2024. Draft regulations that will establish the Commission will form part of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps he has taken to ensure that any provisions in the proposed new public health bill are compliant with human rights legislation.
answer_text: My officials have engaged with and will continue to engage with the Northern Ireland Human Rights Commission and the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland. A draft Equality and Human Rights Impact Assessment has been completed and published alongside the consultation. Officials will review the impact assessment once the consultation has closed. A number of human rights and GDPR safeguards and protections will be included in the Bill which are designed to balance health protection and personal freedoms.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the basis on which NI Water has declared drinking water extracted from Lough Neagh to be safe.
answer_text: The provision of safe drinking water is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Drinking water supplied from water treatment works which source raw water from Lough Neagh are monitored daily using monitoring systems which are linked to 24 hour staffed surveillance. Throughout this event, test results from the final water leaving each of the water treatment plants is assessed against Regulatory standards within the Water Supply (Water Quality) Regulations (NI) 2017 and the recast of the EU Drinking Water Directive and a public health impact assessment is made. This is completed in liaison with the Drinking Water Inspectorate (DWI), the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the relevant Environmental Health Officers. NI water has further advised that the taste and odour emanating from water produced at Lough Neagh is as a result of Geosmin and Methyl-Isoborneol. Geosmin and Methyl-Isoborneol are naturally occurring compounds which are found in soil and food and do not pose a risk to human health.Should you have any further queries with regards this you can contact NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) toxins; and (ii) naturally occurring compounds that NI Water test for when ensuring drinking water extracted from Lough Neagh is safe to be consumed.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows: NI Water test against the parameters listed in the Water Supply (Water Quality) Regulations (NI) 2017. This is in addition to any further parameters considered to be present in raw water sources, through the Drinking Water Safety Plan for each source water and any additional parameters which are likely to be applied as a result of the Recast of the EU Drinking Water Directive.NI Water has an existing, evidence-based system of treatment that is already designed with the potential for algae to be present; however, any new and emerging risks as advised by the NIEA will continue to inform and shape NI Water’s monitoring plans.Should you have any further queries with regards this you can contact NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the additional (i) toxins; and (ii) naturally occurring compounds that NI Water plans to test for in the future to ensure drinking water extracted from Lough Neagh is safe to be consumed.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows: NI Water test against the parameters listed in the Water Supply (Water Quality) Regulations (NI) 2017. This is in addition to any further parameters considered to be present in raw water sources, through the Drinking Water Safety Plan for each source water and any additional parameters which are likely to be applied as a result of the Recast of the EU Drinking Water Directive.NI Water has an existing, evidence-based system of treatment that is already designed with the potential for algae to be present; however, any new and emerging risks as advised by the NIEA will continue to inform and shape NI Water’s monitoring plans.Should you have any further queries with regards this you can contact NI Water through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what engagement she has had with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs regarding funding for the Lough Neagh Report and action plan.
answer_text: I met the DAERA Minister as part of the process to set the 2024-25 Budget. This provided an opportunity to discuss and get an understanding of the pressures faced by the department, including the need to address the issues at Lough Neagh. Minister Muir also shared the Lough Neagh Action Plan with Executive members in July, which provided another opportunity to discuss this matter. This Plan was agreed by the Executive. I also met with Minister Muir last week as part of a series of bilateral meetings with Ministers to discuss the pressures faced by Departments in 2024-25. Once again, the Minister raised issues relating to Lough Neagh.The protection of Lough Neagh and the surrounding environment is a key priority as reflected in the draft Programme for Government. Despite significant financial challenges being faced, I was pleased the Executive agreed to allocate DAERA £1.5m Resource DEL and £1.6m capital DEL in June monitoring to support actions relating to Lough Neagh.  The process to set the upcoming 2025-26 Budget has already begun and I expect to meet with the DAERA Minister again to discuss his priorities, including around Lough Neagh, as part of that process

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will announce his decision in relation to the recommendations of the expert panel, appointed to review the Discretionary Support scheme.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the Graduated Driver Licensing programme; and (ii) to detail the timeline for the full implementation of the programme.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016 provides the statutory framework for graduated driver licensing. However further subordinate legislation, dedicated funding and significant operational planning activity are required in advance of implementation.I have asked my officials to seek legal advice which will help me determine the best way forward, once established I will make an annoument

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what recent discussions he has had with the British Government in relation to securing a new agreement with the EU on access to veterinary medicines after December 2025.
answer_text: Question 11 - Patsy McGloneTo ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what recent discussions he has had with the British Government in relation to securing a new agreement with the EU on access to veterinary medicines after December 2025.AQO 761/22-27Answer:Firstly, let me just state for clarity that the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024 came into force on Friday 12 April 2024, conferring powers upon the Secretary of State in relation to Articles 5 to 7 and Annex 2 of the Windsor Framework. Annex 2 includes Regulation (EU) 2019/6 on veterinary medicinal products. The Secretary of State has not exercised discretion with respect to this regulation and it is now under the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.As Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs I am committed to doing all within my power to ensure our continued access to veterinary medicines after December 2025. I have had significant engagement with the new UK administration and have been encouraged by their genuine recognition of the issues around veterinary medicines supply and access.I have written to the Rt Hon Hilary Benn MP, Secretary of State for Northern Ireland and Steve Reed OBE MP The Secretary of State for EFRA setting out my concerns regarding the future supply of Veterinary Medicines in Northern Ireland. I have also had recent engagement with Nick Thomas-Symonds MP, Minister for the Constitution and European Relations and Baroness Hayman of Ullock Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State at the Defra and I am hopeful that the UK Government will act in good faith to improve EU/UK relations and work towards the possibility of an SPS Veterinary Agreement that may solve many of the issues for the future supply and access to veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.I am committed to do whatever is in my power as Minister going forward to ensure a satisfactory resolution to the problem of veterinary medicine supply and look forward to working with UK Ministers, officials and stakeholders to advocate for Northern Ireland when the Veterinary Medicines Working Group is reinstated.My department officials continue to engage with the Veterinary Medicines Directorate, Defra officials and other stakeholders to address concerns around the continuing supply of veterinary medicines after the end of the grace period on 31 December 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total sum of payments made by NI Water to Deloitte in the last five years, including this financial year to date.
answer_text: The payments made to Deloitte are an operational matter for NI Water. question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any correspondence he has had with the Earl of Shaftesbury in relation to handing over the ownership of Lough Neagh to the community free gratis.
answer_text: I met the Earl of Shaftesbury on 21 February 2024 and discussed a range of issues in relation to Lough Neagh, including ownership.  Following his more recent comments on the potential to transfer ownership of the Lough into a charity or Community Trust model, I also met with the Earl on 17 July 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when the grant budget allocation for the Affordable Warmth Scheme will be finalised.
answer_text: I have allocated an opening budget of £7.175m for the Affordable Warmth Scheme (AWS) to cover £5.2m of approvals carried forward and I allocated £1.975m for new approvals. The Equality Impact Assessment consultation on the Department’s budget, launched 11 June 2024, will end on 3 September 2024 and information from this consultation will be considered before the final budget is confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the level of financial commitment (i) the Executive; and (ii) his Department has given over the next three years to the implementation of the proposals contained in the Lough Neagh Action Plan.
answer_text: The Executive has allocated £1.5 million Resource DEL and £1.6 million Capital DEL to DAERA to take forward work on Lough Neagh in 2024-25.  I have supplemented this with internal departmental allocations of £2.8 million Resource DEL and £1.6 million Capital DEL which means that £7.5 million has been allocated in this financial year towards improving water quality, addressing the challenges in Lough Neagh and tackling blue green algae.  I will continue to work with Executive colleagues to secure further funding in 2025-26 and 2026-27 as part of the Budget and Monitoring Round exercises for those years as we work to implement the 37 actions. The Resource DEL and Capital DEL that has been allocated so far is a welcome injection of funding enabling additional resources to be directed to key areas of work and take forward the actions in the plan as a priority.While some funding has been received and is being used, all the proposals in the action plan are not funded.  A significant bid has also been made to the Transformation Fund for Lough Neagh specific actions and this is currently being considered by the interim Public Sector Transformation Board.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what immediate assurances he has sought from NI Water in light of the recent research carried out by Queen’s University Belfast in relation to unprecedented harmful algal bloom in Lough Neagh associated with gastrointestinal bacteria, microcystins and anabaenopeptins presenting an environmental and public health risk.
answer_text: The quality and safety of drinking water is an operational matter for NI Water.  However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows: The processes at the water treatment works provide a multibarrier effective treatment, to ensure that drinking water supplied to customers meets strict drinking water quality standards.  Where there is a risk for algae to be present in the raw water, the drinking water treatment processes are designed to deal with this risk.  Indeed, I recently visited Northern Ireland Water’s Moyola Treatment Works facility and witnessed for myself the work being done by NI Water to ensure that people across the North have access to clean, safe drinking water.  I am reassured to see the robust testing and sampling procedures in place to ensure the continuing supply of high-quality drinking water. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 10571/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 10571 22-27 was answered on 08 July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he will answer AQW 13732/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 13732/22-27 was answered on 11 July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 13519/22-27, (i) for his assessment of the accuracy of the 2018-19 year-start investment total for community pharmacy services being £104 million in 2018/19, given that his Department’s cost findings quantified the cost of community pharmacy services to be at least £130 million in 2011-12; and (ii) to detail the funding (a) sought; and (b) secured in the June monitoring round for existing service pressures in community pharmacy.
answer_text: (i) I can confirm the accuracy of the 2018-19 opening budget allocation for Community Pharmacy Services (CPS) which was £104m.  This figure was part of a two-year deal agreed in 2018 which was based on a core financial envelope of £104m per annum for the delivery of CPS, plus additional funding for trialling and testing new services. The closing position resulted in total funding of circa. £116m and £113m in 2018/19 and 2019/20 respectively. The figure of £130m relates to a “cost of services” investigation (CoSI) of community pharmacy services relating to the period 2011/12 which was undertaken by PricewaterhouseCoopers.At the time the Department made it clear that the CoSI report would help to inform the levels of funding for community pharmacy services alongside other sources of information, including an ongoing Margins Survey. It was not intended that the CoSI report would set the level of funding required to fund community pharmacy.  (ii) It was not possible to bid for any further funding for community pharmacy in the June monitoring round. The Department of Health’s budget allocation for 2024-25 represented a 2.3% reduction in funding compared to 2023-24. The bid made by the Department at June monitoring round was to address the funding gap and focus on sustaining existing services at current funding levels. You will wish to note that community pharmacy already received a funding uplift by the previous Minister. The 2024/25 opening position of £147m was increased by £19m from the opening position of the previous year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 13519/22-27, to detail any additional financial support he will provide to implement the Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy.
answer_text: The Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan brings together four existing inter-related programmes of work led by the Department, to support the delivery of six strategic priorities up to 2030. Those four work programmes relate to: Service Development; Legislative Reform; Workforce; and e-Pharmacy.Some aspects of these programmes are in train and funded, including several initiatives to support workforce development. Also, in April community pharmacy received an increase of £19m to its opening funding position for 2024/25, bringing it to £147m, which represents an increase of 14.8% from the previous year. However, full delivery of the strategic plan is subject to securing additional funding over the next six years, and implementation will be phased. The Department of Health’s budget allocation for 2024-25 represents a 2.3% reduction in funding compared to our expenditure in 2023-24. The 2024-25 opening Budget allocation has left a funding gap of circa £340m and even after June Monitoring there is insufficient funding available to meet our needs.  Due to this position, we are not able to request additional funding for any new work areas – the focus in 2024/25 is on sustaining existing services at current funding levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether the additional criteria for Discretionary Support that was added in summer 2023 due to a limited budget is still in place; and (ii) whether that additional criteria will be removed.
answer_text: The changes to the Discretionary Support policy were introduced in July 2023 in an effort to protect and sustain the budget throughout the financial year. This was a necessary measure to ensure financial support continued to be available to help address the basic needs of some of the most vulnerable.I recognise the importance of the Discretionary Support scheme, which is why the Discretionary Support Grant Budget has been topped up to £22m for 2024-25. However, in view of the continued constrained budget situation the additional eligibility criteria introduced in July 2023 will remain in operation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) when the interdepartmental group on the anti-poverty strategy will next meet; and (ii) what Departments will be represented on the group.
answer_text: While there is no date yet set for the Cross-Departmental Working Group to meet, my officials continue to engage with colleagues across the NICS on the Anti-Poverty Strategy.  All Departments have a role and a contribution to make to this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to engage with (i) the independent expert panel who have developed recommendations for an anti-poverty strategy; and (ii) the anti-poverty strategy co-design group.
answer_text: I am currently considering the next steps to be taken in relation to the Executive Anti-Poverty Strategy. I acknowledge the input from a wide range of stakeholders to date and it will now be for the Executive to consider the way forward.		I will announce the next steps to be taken in relation to the Strategy shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to adopt the recommendations of the expert panel on Discretionary Support to improve the reach and fairness of the current system.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of the Discretionary Support Scheme in assisting vulnerable people in a crisis situation. That is why I have increased the budget for Discretionary Support grants to £22 million for the 2024/25 financial year.The recommendations for changes to Discretionary Support, made by the expert panel, are currently being considered. These recommendations will need to be assessed in the climate of financial constraint and the requirement to ensure that budgets remain sustainable over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the funding for Discretionary Support that remains available for allocation in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: I have agreed a Discretionary Support Grant budget of £22m in 2024-25, which is an increase of 10% on last year’s budget.  	For Discretionary Support Loans, which is capital funding, the budget is £10.1m which is offset by loans receipts of £9.1m for 2024-25, resulting in a total capital requirement of £1m.   	As the Discretionary Support scheme is a demand led service, my Department will continue to closely monitor scheme expenditure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to detail why his Department is the only Executive Department that has not appointed an official to the cross-departmental working group convened by the Minister for Communities to consult on the actions drafted by the Irish Language Strategy Co-Design Group; and (ii) , given that the strategy will contain actions to be taken forward by his Department, whether he will reconsider this decision.
answer_text: The absence of strategies and structures has not prevented the development of either language or culture in recent years in Northern Ireland. In the context of the severe financial challenges facing the Department of Health, any additional funding for the promotion of languages would inevitably mean that less is available for the delivery of core Health & Social Care services.In addition, fewer than 1 percent of the proposed actions in the draft Action Plans fall specifically for the Department of Health to take forward.  It is therefore not the best use of the scarce resources available for the Department of Health to make a nomination to the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage & Culture Strategy & Irish Language Strategy Cross Departmental Working Group (CDWG).  However, Department of Health officials receive copies of the papers for the CDWG meetings with representation kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the support available to register and maintain a community-owned defibrillator that is accessible for public use.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) leads on the implementation of the Community Resuscitation Strategy and is funded to provide a supporting role in advising organisations, businesses, and individuals regarding siting of defibrillators and best practice for defibrillators. Currently there are around 3203 permanently fixed community defibrillators registered with NIAS on the National Defibrillator Network, known as ‘The Circuit’.  Further information on the location of defibrillators can be found at www.defibfinder.uk or by contacting the NIAS Community Resuscitation Team at resus.admin@nias.hscni.net or telephone 028 9040 5215.Local Councils provide opportunities for community and sporting organisations to apply for funding towards defibrillators and there are a number of charities who will fully or part fund defibrillators.  These charities include but are not limited to The British Heart Foundation and Bravehearts NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether any contingency plans are in place if the procurement process to renew the operator agreement for Crumlin Road Gaol visitor attraction and conference centre is not completed by November 2024.
answer_text: The procurement for the tender to manage the Gaol Visitor Attraction and Conference Centre has commenced and is expected to be completed by November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the budgetary allocation for public realm schemes for 2024-25; and (ii) the level of expenditure on public realm schemes in each of the last ten years, broken down by location.
answer_text: The indicative capital budget allocation for urban regeneration in 2024-25 is £17.7m. This does not include any indicative budget allocated for City Deal projects or PEACE IV projects.The Department’s Retention and Disposal Policy period for financial records is seven years. The figures for investment in urban regeneration public realm projects in the last seven years, broken down by Council area, will be deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 12520/22-27, to detail the progress her Department has made in bringing forward the necessary statutory provision for wills to be validly made in Irish, as committed under New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: Meeting the New Decade New Approach commitment to ensure that wills can be validly made in Irish will require cross-government work between my department, the Department of Justice and the NIO. The necessary statutory provision for wills to be validly made in Irish is already in place. Section 4 of the Identity and Language (NI) Act 2022 removes the only current bar on a will being validly made in any language. That provision has yet to be commenced by the British Government, and will require work to ensure that the court system is adequately prepared should a will in Irish be contested in the courts.The Justice Minister and I have corresponded on this matter and we are committed to engaging our Ministerial colleagues to ensure that this important NDNA commitment is progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the Speaker's Ruling on Assembly Questions on 12 March 2024, and pursuant to AQW 12788/22-27, to detail (i) the membership of the focus group; (ii) how many times it has met; (iii) what plans it has identified that can be put in place by summer 2024; (iv) what longer-term actions it has identified; and (v) how its outcomes are to be assessed.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NI Water; however, my officials sought the requested information from NI Water and I can advise: The focus group is made up of two Directors and twelve senior managers from many key disciplines, including water supply and production engineers, wastewater engineers, drinking water quality scientists, wastewater and environmental regulation scientists, emergency planning, operational resilience, communications and stakeholder management, intelligent operations, water asset strategy engineers and customer experience managers. The group has as met a total of 13 times (up to 25 June 2024), Focused plans identified for Summer 2024 include reviewing Lough Neagh water treatment works maintenance and filter replacement, installation of barriers and screens, programming 2024 sampling and analysis, procuring algae analysis equipment and developing triggers for algal compounds, consideration of opportunities to rezone supply areas to draw less water from Lough Neagh, proactive engagement with stakeholders, customers and elected representatives, an increased understanding of its impact on the Lough environment and developing associated strategy and contingency plans.  Longer term plans identified included additional water quality and environmental drainage modelling, delivery of water and wastewater asset improvements, further filter and screen replacement and further review of Lough Neagh water dependency options.Any outcomes will be assessed based either on delivery by a planned date, or achievement of the expected effects/changes.I hope you find the above response from NI Water helpful. I would recommend for any future queries relating to operational matters, to contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what additional financial support he will provide to community pharmacies to implement the Community Pharmacy Strategic Plan 2030.
answer_text: The Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy was published on 07 May 2024. It aims to fully realise the potential of community pharmacy services to support better health outcomes from medicines and prevent illness.This important piece of work was developed collaboratively by the Department of Health and Community Pharmacy Northern Ireland (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy owners, and describes the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030.Against the backdrop of the current severe budgetary pressures and the multiple demands for additional funding across all parts of health and social care, additional investment continues to be provided to the community pharmacy sector. Funding for community pharmacies in NI has increased in recent years. The year-start investment total for community pharmacy services was £104m in 2018/19. By 2023/24 it was £128m and in April of this year it stood at £147m. There has therefore been a £43m (41%) increase in six years.The level of government funding for community pharmacies in NI compares favourably to other parts of the UK. However, more will be required to fully deliver this Strategic Plan up to 2030. I am very conscious that the Community Pharmacy sector – like all sectors across health and social care – is keen to see further investment.The Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy is available on the Department of Health website:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/community-pharmacy-strategic-plan-2030 Full delivery of the vision outlined in the plan will be subject to funding. The Department will continue to work with Community Pharmacy NI and the pharmacy profession to progress a phased approach to implementation over the coming years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the High Court Rulings [2017] NIQB 27 and [2022] NIQB 56, declaring that the Executive had failed to comply with its duty under section 28D of the Northern Ireland Act 1998, (i) what stage the Irish Language Strategy is at; and (ii) when he plans to bring it to the Executive for approval.
answer_text: The Language Strategies Cross-Departmental Working Group is considering the Co-Design Group’s proposed strategy action plan. Officials have updated me on this work. Once the work of the Cross Departmental Working Group is complete, the draft Strategy will issue to me for consideration prior to Executive consideration and public consultation. I am currently considering the next steps in the development of the Irish Language Strategy and the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy and the associated action plans. I will then engage with Executive colleagues to ask them to consider and if content agree on those next steps.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment he has made of the impact of the implementation of the Pharmacy Workforce Review 2020 on the financial viability of community pharmacies.
answer_text: The aim of the Pharmacy Workforce Review published in 2020 was to inform pharmacy workforce development needs for the period 2019- 2029 across the three main employed sectors – which includes community, hospital and general practice pharmacy. The PWR is not an assessment of financial viability.My Department has made significant investment into implementation of the Review recommendations, which is having a positive impact on the capacity and capability of the pharmacy workforce across all 3 sectors  A ‘Pharmacy Futures NI’ campaign designed to attract, recruit, and retain pharmacists has helped to increase the number of students applying to study pharmacy at the two universities here. .  The Pharmacy Workforce Review  recommended that Pharmacists undertake foundation training, progressing to independent prescribing (IP). Access to IP is a key enabler for pharmacists to move to advanced training and to enable redesign of services so that more advanced pathways are progressed, providing them with opportunities to play a bigger role in delivering clinical services in primary care and in multi-disciplinary working.In addition, new mandatory UK wide standards have been introduced to transform the Initial Education and Training (IET) of pharmacists, so that they are independent prescribers (IPs) on registration. This training will allow them to play a much greater role in providing clinical care to patients and the public from their first day on the register, including through prescribing medicines. It will also prepare them to gather clinical information to support the treatment of minor illness symptoms and to deliver self-care advice and treatment for people who present to local community pharmacies. Policy work is in development and funding is being provided to implement the education reforms and provide the necessary training so that, from September 2026, all newly qualified pharmacists will be IPs. This includes: embedding Experiential Learning (EL) and IP training into the undergraduate degree course and Foundation Training Year (FTY); increasing the grant for and number of foundation training places to 200, doubling last year’s intake; funding a number of additional posts in Trusts to support the new EL and FTY programmes; and increasing the number of post graduate IP places to 150 annually.  Further implementation of the PWR includes publication of the report on Developing the Role of Consultant Pharmacists in NI (June 2023) and development of Advanced Practice roles in NI. The necessary changes to policy and legislation are also being progressed which will introduce a regulated pharmacy technician profession in Northern Ireland by 2026. New research is also being undertaken by my Department, sponsored by the United Kingdom Research Institute Biotechnology and Biological Sciences Research Council (BBSRC), to inform the development of future policy and commissioning of advanced practice roles and commissioning in community pharmacy. The project

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister when the Junior Ministers will give evidence to the Committee for the Executive Office.
To ask the Minister of Education to outline what progress his Department has made regarding the provision of in-house expertise for special educational needs in Irish-medium schools.
answer_text: There is some crossover between this recommendation in the Independent Review of Education and the work already underway as part of my department’s end to end review of special educational needs (SEN).   My officials have engaged with Comhairle na Gaelscolaíochta (CnaG) and the Irish-medium education sector throughout the end to end review of SEN and are alive to the particular issues and gaps in services.  Stage 1 provision for children with special educational needs relates to support provided in-house by the child’s school.  One of the aims is to ensure we have a competent and confident workforce.  Workforce development is one of the workstreams which is exploring initial teacher education and teacher professional learning in relation to SEN.   This will examine training needs among the workforce in relation to SEN, including the specific training needs for staff in Irish-medium education settings.  The training requirements of classroom assistants is also being considered.In relation to Stage 2 provision for children with SEN, this is provided externally including through the Education Authority’s Pupil Support Services, Middletown Centre for Autism and outreach support from special schools.  Another of the workstreams within the end to end review of SEN is considering the effectiveness of Pupil Support Services, which has included a close look at the support services available to the Irish-medium education sector.  Proposals will be brought forward to address the gaps in these services to ensure that the needs of children with SEN in the Irish-medium sector can be met.My Department is also liaising with colleagues in the Education Authority to ensure that the needs of the sector are fully considered in the development and implementation of the new Local Integrated Teams model for the provision of pupil support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the discussions he has had with his counterpart in the Irish government in relation to collaborating on a new waiting list initiative to replace the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive.
answer_text: I am open to collaboration with the Minister for Health in the Irish Government on areas such as waiting list initiatives amongst many others. However, under the 2024/25 Budget recently approved by the Executive, precisely zero additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists.Following the success of the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme (RoIRS) which was based on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive and introduced in June 2021, a bid for funding for a replacement scheme was submitted to the Department of Finance during this year’s budget setting process. It was intended that a new reimbursement scheme could have treated over 4,000 patients this financial year. Unfortunately, instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, the Department’s budget has been cut by over 2%.  This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the Speaker's Ruling on Assembly Questions on 12 March 2024, and pursuant to AQW 9137/22-27, what steps NI Water is taking to prevent the occurrence of blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NI Water; however, I have sought the requested information and have been advised that NI Water has been actively involved in the review of the blue green algae that manifested itself during Spring/Summer 2023, that the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is leading on.In doing so, NI Water has advised that it has established an internal focus group to identify and progress plans that can be put in place by this summer, and also longer-term actions which could be delivered over the following years, subject to the availability of funding. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com  .Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the Speaker's Ruling on Assembly Questions on 12 March 2024, and pursuant to AQW 9138/22-27, for an update on the upgrade to the (i) Ballyronan; (ii) Bellaghy; (iii) Derrychrin; (iv) Killygonlan; (v) Pomeroy; and (vi) Stewartstown Wastewater Treatment Works.
answer_text: The upgrade of wastewater treatment works (WwTWs) is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:the upgrade of Ballyronan WwTW is currently underway, with an expected completion date later this year;the upgrade of Derrychrin WwTW has been completed and NI Water is awaiting full hand over from the contractor;the upgrade of Pomeroy WwTW has been completed and NI Water is awaiting full hand over from the contractor; and(ii), (iv) & (vi)The upgrades of Bellaghy, Killygonlan and Stewartstown WwTWs are included in NI Water’s  Price Control 21 investment programme (2021/22 - 2026/27), agreed with the Utility Regulator. NI Water is working closely with my Department regarding the programming and funding for the delivery of these schemes.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the Speaker's Ruling on Assembly Questions on 12 March 2024, and pursuant to AQW 10670/22-27, to detail NI Water's plans to prevent frequent raw sewage spills from the Wastewater Treatment Works at Clonoe, County Tyrone.
answer_text: The management of the wastewater treatment works at Clonoe, known as Coalisland wastewater treatment works (WwTW), is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:During the last year, NI Water has undertaken a number of operational interventions to improve the day to day performance of the works. In conjunction to the work already completed, NI Water has plans to install additional equipment to improve the reliability of the works. It is hoped that this work will be completed during the next financial year, however, that is dependent on the availability of funding. These interventions will have a beneficial impact on the operation of Coalisland WwTW at Clonoe.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children still to be placed in Education Authority funded preschool places in the Mid Ulster constituency for the 2024-25 academic year; and (ii) the measures being put in place to resolve outstanding issues.
answer_text: I would refer you to my answer to AQW 11339/22-27 which provided information on pre-school provision in the Mid Ulster constituency area.Stage two of the admissions process is ongoing and covers a range of applications; for children who were not offered a place in a preferred setting at stage one, for children who did receive an offer at stage one but whose parents have chosen to submit a new application at stage two, and for children whose parents did not apply at stage one.It is not possible, nor would it be meaningful to provide information on numbers of children still to be placed until Stage Two of the admissions process concludes on 11 June and parents are advised of the outcome.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what measures NI Water have in place for investigating allegations of fraud; and (ii) how many employees that deal with allegations of fraud are employed full-time.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NI Water.  However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows: (i) NI Water’s Fraud and Bribery Response Plan sets out the measures to be followed when a fraud is detected or suspected.  The plan provides guidance on the following areas:Action following detection and  preparation of an Initial Confidential Enquiry Report. Reporting to the NI Water Director of Finance, Regulation and Commercial, the NI Water Board and the Department for Infrastructure. Role of Internal Audit. Manager’s duty of care. Protection of evidence. Appointment of Case Manager. PSNI involvement. Sanction, redress and write-off.Learning from experience.(ii) Eight employees who deal with allegations of fraud are employed full-time.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.
