answer_text: My Department is responsible for the substantive law on wills, and has responsibility for the 1994 Order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of mid-contract price rises, during a fixed cost contract period, that have been awarded to contractors by NI Water since January 2022; and (ii) to whom they have been awarded to.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:(i) NI Water has awarded 11 mid contract price increases to fixed cost operational contracts since January 2022. These contracts had been tendered prior to January 2022, in line with the principles of Procurement Advisory Note 01/21 and could demonstrate an unforeseeable cost impact beyond normal inflationary conditions.(ii) NI Water is unable to release the names of the 11 contracts subject to a price increase as this information is commercial in confidence. NI Water’s ‘Construction’ and ‘Professional Services’ Contracts are awarded on a call-off basis from existing Frameworks involving multiple suppliers. The companies NI Water has a contractual engagement with can be review at the following links to NI Water’s website:www.niwater.com/contracts-awardedwww.niwater.com/construction-contracts/For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the level of (i) advice; and (ii) financial assistance he intends to make available for rural communities through the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture.
answer_text: I am committed to setting up a Just Transition Fund for Agriculture and will be considering in conjunction with Executive colleagues the best approach to take to this and to the other requirements placed upon us by the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022, learning where appropriate from the experience of others.		I will also be working closely with the Finance Minister to ensure that the future funding allocated for agriculture here fully meets Northern Ireland’s needs. Funding for the Just Transition Fund for Agriculture will need to be considered in the context of budgetary pressures and priorities across the NI Executive.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures he plans to put in place to ensure that current and future graduates from the Ulster University Masters in Physician Associate Studies course can find roles in Health and Social Care Trusts and GP practices.
answer_text: My Department is very supportive of the MSc in Physician Associates programme at Ulster University. My officials have been working proactively with the HSC Trusts through the regional PA Forum and other engagement, including between the Department’s Permanent Secretary and Trust Chief Executives, to try to secure posts for the 2024 cohort of PA graduates. The current very challenging financial situation is impacting on Trusts’ ability to provide posts.  However, my officials will continue to work with stakeholders to try to find solutions to this issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for her Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: (i) The adoption and use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) within the Department of Finance offers significant opportunities to examine how processes and procedures could be improved to drive efficiencies and effectiveness across the Department. To investigate this new technology, while being cognisant of the risks, my Department is running a few small scale Proof of Value (PoV) projects to examine the use of AI in a controlled and measured way to understand how this technology can deliver value in the public sector.One of these PoVs is to investigate how a chatbot can reduce complexity, save time, and make interactions with a government service simpler and faster. The technology responds to user questions using existing published information. Another PoV involves using data analysis techniques such as statistical models to identify patterns in data, and to apply this to building occupancy to inform decisions on energy consumption, building usage etc.(ii) However, these opportunities must be considered in the light of the challenges such as the availability and suitability of data, data protection, data privacy and cyber security alongside the wider issues with AI such as usage monitoring, bias, incorrect information, and incorrect predictions.All civil servants and those working in government organisations should follow the Generative AI Framework provided by Cabinet Office, which contains detailed guidance on using generative AI safely and securely.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for his Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: (i) 	The adoption and use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) within my Department offers opportunities to examine how processes and procedures could be improved to drive efficiencies and effectiveness.   My Department relies upon the Department for Work and Pensions in Great Britain and the Department of Finance in Northern Ireland for the vast majority of its digital infrastructure and systems.  My Department will be working closely with both on shared opportunities that may arise.(ii)	The challenges faced within my Department include having appropriate governance in place, ensuring cyber and data security, changing business processes, securing the resources required to invest in the development of solutions and the risks of unreliable or inaccurate outcome from artificial intelligence.All civil servants and those working in government organisations should follow the Generative AI Framework provided by Cabinet Office, which contains detailed guidance on using generative AI safely and securely.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for their Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: (i) The development of Artificial Intelligence (AI) based technologies has introduced a number of potential opportunities to improve the efficiency and quality of services across all government and public sector organisations, and will be a key enabler in future activities undertaken by our department. These opportunities include the streamlining of administrative and workflow processes, the introduction of new data analysis methodologies, enhancements to the accessibility of our citizen facing services, the development of new tools to protect our critical assets, and optimised decision making through improved data insights. (ii)  The opportunities provided by Artificial Intelligence must be weighed against the challenges and risks that their introduction present, and in particular the protection of citizen data.  We must put in place mechanisms that enable emerging technologies to be used in an ethical way, ensuring that they do not introduce bias into our decision making.  There must always be accountability and transparency for all outputs and decisions that are derived from citizen data.  A “Generative AI” Policy has already been established within the Northern Ireland Civil Service, and our department will follow advice from technology specialists, The Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) and The National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC), as we explore the use of this technology in the future.We are committed to identifying and seizing opportunities that arise from Artificial Intelligence, while ensuring fit-for-purpose risk management and governance structures are in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for her Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: (i) As the understanding of AI technology develops, the Department of Justice welcomes the wide range of opportunities available to consider how it might be used. One example area is the processing and analysis of large amounts of diverse data to look for new trends, a task common to many of the Criminal Justice Organisations. The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism and Organised Crime (EPPOC) have recently completed a proof-of-concept pilot to develop a management dashboard with underlying AI capability. Through this type of work the Department gains the knowledge to make the best use of the new technology in the future. Another example is the use of AI to enhance the capabilities in Forensic Analysis and early work is underway to consider the potential opportunities in this area. My officials also look to our suppliers for opportunities to use AI, and in all new tenders for the development of business solutions, we welcome input from the bidders on potential uses for AI to provide a better end product and help us meet our business requirements.(ii)  The Department of Justice recognises that new technology brings new challenges and it is important we take the time to fully understand these and to make sure that appropriate safeguards are in place. Already the Department has adopted a NICS policy on the use of AI which follows an internal approval process. This ensures a full assessment of the risk is carried out before any business areas proceeds with a consideration to use AI for a task in the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for his Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Generative AI technologies offer opportunities to free-up capacity and improve productivity and quality of care services through the ability to analyse large quantities of complex information, support effective decision making, automate administrative processes and draft summary information from a range of inputs.Other UK administrations are already exploring and proving the benefits of AI in Health and Care, with opportunities including:Using AI to improve back-office efficiencyData-driven demand management, for automated referrals and intelligent triageNatural language processing for efficient reading and coding of clinical recordsUsing AI to support clinical and operational service deliveryAI driven patient engagement platforms for maximising hospital resources by reducing DNAs, reducing cancellation rates and reducing face-to-face appointments.AI to prioritise and manage patient flow and staff tasks, like for logistics teams like porters and cleaners, and in the future for clinical support teamsThe potential capabilities of this technology must be set against the potential risks – for instance in the areas of clinical efficacy and information governance. In these areas the Department of Health and the Health and Social Care (HSC) system needs to ensure that the tools provided are trustworthy and do not breach the privacy of patients or service users.Additionally, the wide adoption of advanced technologies like AI/GenAI require a number of underlying enablers – including good quality, codified data that describes a service users needs and historic episodes and treatments (for training purposes), the clinical safety and ethical governance structures that are required to oversee the safe introduction of these AI tools, and skills in cloud services engineering and security (recognising that the compute power required to run AI algorithms is often best provided from cloud technologies). The Department of Health and its partners in the HSC are establishing structures to develop these enablers and explore the capabilities of providers and products, with a particular focus in the first instance on medical image analysis for diagnostic purposes, an area with growing evidence to support clinical effectiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for her Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is one of several innovative technologies that has widespread application across the economy, some of which we do not yet fully understand. In both its development and its industrial application, it will create opportunities, benefits, risks and challenges to be managed across the board. As with other technological developments in recent decades, I expect the adoption of AI will be productivity-enhancing overall but will also create some disruption across labour markets as the nature of jobs change, and the workforce becomes more productive – and is more concentrated in higher productivity activities. The key role for my department is to encourage the adoption of productivity-enhancing technologies such as AI, data analytics, innovative software and robotics across the economy in a way that creates opportunity for all. That is why, as we build innovative and AI-focussed industrial capability through our City and Growth Deals investments, and the recently launched AI Collaboration Centre, we are also supporting individuals to position themselves with the mix of skills and qualifications – academic, vocational and technical – to benefit from the technologies that are shaping economic opportunity in the coming decade and beyond. The development of a sub-regional economic action plan seeks to provide strategic coherence and a robust framework to place the north in the best position to adopt (the benefits), and effectively manage (the disruptions) associated with AI and other technologies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for his Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is one of the key themes of DAERA’s Innovation Strategy.  The maturing of Large Language Models and Generative AI has opened up opportunities for a wide range of applications across food, farming, environment, fisheries, forestry and rural development sectors.  My Department is currently exploring how it can be used to classify land cover, monitor habitat condition, assess plant health and in change detection, for example in coastal erosion. We are also assessing how AI can be used to drive efficiencies and improve service delivery.There are challenges and risks with AI related to data protection, unethical use, upskilling staff, and hype. To ensure responsible use with appropriate governance, my Department has taken expert advice from industry leaders and is developing an AI policy and applying guidance from the GOV.uk Generative AI Framework.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for his Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence has the potential to present exciting opportunities across my Department from assisting staff to do their administrative roles  and improve how we provide sustainable services to citizens. We are assessing how we can use it to for example, monitor and predict maintenance of our infrastructure assets, and provide 24/7 customer services to answer queries and access services. The challenges Artificial Intelligence brings will need carefully managed, from ethical considerations, data privacy and security, validation of accuracy of algorithm outputs and the upskilling of staff to use Artificial Intelligence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for his assessment of the (i) opportunities; and (ii) challenges for his Department in relation to the development of Artificial Intelligence.
answer_text: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is in use, to a greater or lesser extent, in almost every activity.  My Department as an organisation has been provided with NICS Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) | NICS Intranet (nigov.net)The development of AI in the workplace, including schools, could be utilised to assist with tasks and allow Principals and Teachers to focus on the education of our young people. AI has the potential to transform the delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum. It can support curriculum planning, create interactive learning environments, and provide real-time assessments and feedback, helping students understand their progress and areas for improvement. AI-powered language learning tools can assist in teaching new languages, reading, and writing skills, adapting to the learner's pace and proficiency. However, it is important to note the risks associated with the use of AI to support the delivery of the Northern Ireland Curriculum, namely that content produced by AI can be inaccurate, inappropriate or biased.The application of AI in the awarding of qualifications is being considered by policy makers, awarding bodies and regulators.  The Joint Council for Qualifications (JCQ), of which CCEA is a constituent member, provides guidance to all schools in Northern Ireland regarding monitoring the use of AI and protecting the integrity of examinations.  This guidance advises schools on a range of AI-related issues including how to prevent the misuse of AI in assessments.The EA is actively exploring AI and new technology.  A review is currently being conducted on a draft AI standard that will provide clarity and guidance for responsible and effective AI adoption across the organisation.  The potential of this emerging technology is recognised, and EA is committed to deploying it safely through a defined pilot scheme in collaboration with a specialised AI partner. AI  has the potential to present challenges in relation to child protection.  From a safeguarding perspective, schools are provided with the most up-to-date resources in relation to online safety in Safeguarding and Child Protection in Safeguarding and Child Protection in Schools - A Guide for Schools.  DE also funds iNEQE Safeguarding Group to provide the Safer Schools Northern Ireland App which includes information on AI for pupils, parents/carers and school staff, registered to use the App.  DE continues to work with partners on the cross-departmental Online Safety Strategy led by DoHWe are also likely to face challenges regarding investment in AI and cybersecurity.The Department of Education and its arm’s-length bodies will continue to review policies and practice in relation to the use of AI and it’s use in education, and update guidance as this area develops.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 11339/22-27, and in reference to two closures of providers of pre-school education in Cookstown, what contingency measures are in place to (i) address the resultant problems; and (ii) meet parental demand for suitable, accessible pre-school nursery places.
answer_text: As previously stated in my response to AQW 11339/22-27, the Education Authority Pre-school Education Group (EA PEG) is tasked with securing sufficient pre-school provision in local areas.The EA has advised that Beacon Pre-school, Cookstown decided to close their setting with effect from 1 July 2024 as a result of insufficient demand for the provision offered.  The EA further advised that only one other funded pre-school setting has closed in the area in the last five years; namely Bosco Playgroup which decided to close in August 2022.The EA advises that there is sufficient pre-school provision in the Cookstown area to accommodate all target aged children whose parents want a funded pre-school place for them.Stage One of the pre-school admissions process concluded on 25 April with 98.33% of children offered a funded pre-school place; 89.2% at their parents’ first preference setting. Stage Two of the admissions process is currently underway and parents will be advised of the outcome on 11 June. The EA has also confirmed that sufficient funded pre-school places remain in all areas, with 24 and 76 funded places still available in Stage Two in the Cookstown area and the wider Mid Ulster Parliamentary Constituency area respectively. The EA PEG continue to monitor provision in all areas of Northern Ireland, including Mid Ulster.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of people that have (i) been charged; (ii) been cautioned; (iii) received fixed penalties; and (iv) received other convictions in relation to damage to bilingual road signs since 1 January 2023.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on persons charged with an offence by police or for persons who receive a fixed penalty as a result of an offence.  The recording of information in relation to persons charged with offences or for the issuing of fixed penalty notices is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in part (i) and (iii) of the question to the PSNI.  In relation to parts (ii) and (iv) of the question, there is no specific offence for damage to a bilingual road sign.  Such offences may be prosecuted under more generic ‘Interfered with traffic sign’ offence, prosecuted under Article 33 of the Road Traffic Regulation (Northern Ireland) Order 1997. However, in the period 1 January 2023 – end March 2024, the latest point for which information is available, figures from management information systems indicate there were no convictions at courts or out of court diversionary disposals handed down for this offence.  Note:Information provided in relation to convictions relate to initial disposals at courts.  Appeals are not included.Figure in relation to convictions and out of court disposals are provisional.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Permanent Secretary to the Executive Office.
answer_text: The Head of the Civil Service launched an external recruitment exercise to fill vacancies in two Permanent Secretary roles in December 2023.  The competition produced two successful candidates. One of the successful candidates, Mr Ian Snowden, was appointed to the DfE post on 28 March 2024.  However, the TEO post remains vacant, as the other successful candidate withdrew from the competition.  In terms of immediate next steps, the TEO Permanent Secretary role will now be filled on an interim basis through an internal temporary promotion opportunity. An NICS-wide competition to that effect has now been launched by the Head of the Civil service. 									This temporary promotion arrangement will be in place for six to twelve months, during which time a review of the most recent Permanent Secretary recruitment competition will be undertaken, and a new recruitment competition to fill the TEO vacancy on a substantive basis progressed.We were notified of these arrangements by the Head of the Civil Service on 23 April 2024, and the Committee for the Executive Office has also been informed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement she has had with the Minister of Education in relation to growing the workforce through an increased participation in adult learning.
answer_text: I have met the Education Minister and discussed the important area of careers. We were clear on the need to maximise our collective assets and work together to enhance careers services for people of all ages, including adults.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the measures being taken by the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to ensure a consultant for the Regional Acquired Brain Injury Unit (RABIU) is available at the Northern Health and Social Care Trust without delay in order to assess patients waiting for acceptance into the RABIU.
answer_text: My officials have liaised with Belfast Health and Social Care Trust (BHSCT) regarding this matter.  My officials have been advised that a Consultant in Rehabilitation Medicine visited the Northern Health and Social Care Trust (NHSCT) on Thursday 16th May 2024 and carried out assessments for several patients.  The outcomes of those assessments have been forwarded to the Consultants at RABIU for further action, where appropriate.The BHSCT have confirmed that a Consultant who was on unplanned leave resumed work on Friday 17th May 2024 and have advised the Consultant will be reviewing his patients, including those within the Northern Trust at planned liaison sessions.  The next scheduled Northern Trust Liaison session is scheduled for Friday 24th May 2024 and BHSCT expect this session to go ahead.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what contingency plans are in place in the event of an extreme solar storm or electromagnetic pulse of a similar scale.
answer_text: The management and delivery of extreme space weather preparedness is a reserved matter.  The strategy to deliver this workstream is the UK Severe Space Weather Preparedness Strategy: https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/614dea50e90e077a368ffc48/uk-severe-space-weather-preparedness-strategy.pdf and it is led by the department of Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy (BEIS) in collaboration with the Ministry of Defence and the UK Space Agency.  BEIS has delegated the ownership of the severe space weather risk to the MET Office and they are responsible for monitoring the risk and supporting national mitigation strategies. Extreme Space Weather sits on the UK National Risk Register, and it is assessed as a global risk.The NI response to an extreme space weather event would be led by the most appropriate Lead Government Department (LGD) supported by the whole of system multi-agency contingency response structures, as laid out in the Northern Ireland Civil Contingencies Framework: https://www.executiveoffice-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-civil-contingencies-framework

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what discussions have taken place between his Department and NI Water in relation to reducing wastewater spilling into public waterways.
answer_text: Cuthbert Street is a narrow cul-de-sac in the Waterside area of the city and there are five gullies located along the street. The last time the Department’s contractor was able to gain access and clean these gullies was 25 November 2021. During subsequent visits in April 2022, Oct 2022 and July 2023 the contractor was unable to gain access due to parked cars. Unfortunately, this is a common problem at many locations throughout the city no matter what time the contractor visits sites.Giving the on-going difficulties with access, my officials will liaise with the local community to see if an arrangement could be put in place with residents to move their cars or have spaces coned off to allow the contractor to carry out the necessary works.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQO 237/22-27 and AQW 10984/22-27, to detail (i) whether her Department is responsible for the law relating to wills; and (ii) what progress her Department has made in bringing forward the necessary statutory provision for wills to be validly made in Irish, as committed under New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: Meeting the New Decade New Approach commitment that wills may be validly made in Irish will require cross-government work between DoJ, DoF and the NIO, to ensure that the necessary legislation is in force and that the court system is appropriately prepared. I wrote to the Justice Minister in April 2024 on this matter and we both remain committed to engaging with Ministerial colleagues to ensure this NDNA commitment is progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a progress update on the Draft Plan Strategy for Mid-Ulster.
answer_text: I am considering the way forward in relation to the issues raised by the Mid Ulster District Council draft Plan Strategy and the Department will update the Council as soon as is possible in relation to it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 10756/22-27, to detail (i) the rationale behind the decision to not provide funding for the Minister for Health’s bid to replace the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive; and (ii) why no additional funding has been provided to address waiting lists in the Budget 2024-25.
answer_text: The Budget provides each Department with an overall funding allocation. It is for the Health Minister to decide how to allocate funding provided according to his department’s priorities.The Resource DEL outcome for the Department of Health included a general allocation of £472 million and reflected a 6.3% increase on their opening Budget 2023-24 position, excluding earmarked funding. The Department of Health also received a further £34 million of earmarked funding which was provided in financial package to tackle waiting lists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact that the budget allocation will have on waiting lists.
answer_text: The £34m which has been earmarked for waiting lists – which is coming directly from the UK Government and which had already long been announced - does not even cover half of what is required to maintain red flag (cancer) and time critical patients. My officials are currently working with Trusts to examine if it is possible to displace other hospital services to the fund the amount of the remaining investment needed (£41-46m). That in itself will cause significant damage and disruption. If we are unable to fully do so, it will lead to longer waiting times and reduce the amount of care available to red-flag and time critical patients and that would be a deplorable situation to find ourselves in.This is also a missed opportunity to improve waiting lists where significant progress could have been made. There has been no additional funding at all provided for tackling our waiting lists. Had I been allocated the funding I had bid for, there would have been an initial focus on those people waiting more than three years. Over 28,000 patients waiting more than 3 years for treatment would have been treated and over 57,000 patients waiting more than 3 years for a first outpatient appointment would have had an appointment.Instead, our waiting lists will continue to grow, which stands in stark contrast to other supposed commitments that tackling them was going to be a priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will expedite plans to introduce hate crime legislation in light of an increase in racist incidents and crimes over the last twelve months.
answer_text: I am currently finalising proposals to the legislative programme to ensure that foundational legislation to deal with hate crimes, which can lead to real improvements for victims, will be progressed within the current mandate.There are a number of options available to achieve legislative change in this regard, including through the proposed Sentencing Bill and proposed Victims Bill. I am considering the available options and liaising with relevant organisations to make sure the legislation works for victims in Northern Ireland.I remain committed to ensuring new hate crime legislation is in place by the end of the mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what urgent measures are being taken to ensure that accessible pre-school nursery places are available to meet demand in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is tasked with securing sufficient provision in local areas.Stage One of the pre-school admissions process concluded on 25 April with 98.33% of children offered a funded pre-school place; 89.2% at their parents’ first preference setting. Stage Two of the admissions process is currently underway and parents will be advised of the outcome on 11 June. The EA has confirmed that sufficient funded pre-school places remain in all areas, with 76 funded places still available in the Mid Ulster Parliamentary Constituency area and only 12 children who submitted further preferences at Stage Two of the admissions process yet to be placed.  The EA PEG continue to monitor provision in all areas of Northern Ireland including Mid Ulster.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of a number of recent closures of childcare facilities in the area, what steps have been taken to ensure that pre-school childcare provision in Cookstown is maintained at a level that is able to meet parental demand.
answer_text: Statutory responsibility for the provision and regulation of childcare as set out in The Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 sits with the Department of Health (DoH). DoH has advised that it is currently mapping childcare provision and supply across Northern Ireland. They have indicated that assessment of parental demand is extremely difficult to gauge as this requires all registered providers to give regular updates of vacancy and demand information, which is not currently provided. This will be addressed within the context of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 10569/22-27, and in light of the comments by the Minister of Finance in the Assembly Chamber on 9 April 2024 in relation to the legislative authority for wills, whether her Department is responsible for the law relating to wills.
answer_text: As I advised in my answer to AQW 10569/22-27, my Department is not responsible for the substantive law relating to wills and their validity. This is provided for in the Wills and Administration Proceedings (Northern Ireland) Order 1994, which is the responsibility of the Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the publication of the Budget 2024-25, what measures he has taken to secure funding for the pay and grading review for Education Authority Support Staff.
answer_text: The Budget allocations provided for 2024-25 do not include a specific allocation for the Education Authority Pay and Grading Review for non-teaching staff.  However I, together with Executive colleagues, am committed to working collectively to find a resolution for these important workers.The Executive has agreed to seek approval from the Treasury to reprofile some of the repurposed funds provided in the Executive restoration financial package for this purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 9580/22-27, to detail the number of times when the use of neonicotinoid pesticides on agricultural land was authorised by his Department, to include (i) the active substance in the pesticide; (ii) the crop; (iii) the area of land treated; and (iv) the location by constituency, in each of the last ten years.
answer_text: Pesticides are authorised at a national level for defined uses on a specific crop or situation. They are not authorised at an individual user level. All users must apply pesticides in accordance with the conditions of use specified on the product labelling. Further, all professional users, by law, must undertake training in the safe use of pesticides and undergo assessment on the spray equipment they use to obtain an approved certificate of competence.In relation to data on the use of pesticides, at individual farm level all professional users must keep records of pesticide usage and produce those records to the Department on request.  The Department does not compile data from individual farm records. Data on overall usage at Northern Ireland level is compiled by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI), on behalf of the Department. AFBI conducts a programme of surveys to examine pesticide usage in all sectors of the agricultural and horticultural industries. These surveys are published on the AFBI website at https://www.afbini.gov.uk/articles/pesticide-usage-monitoring-reports. Over the last 10 years the Northern Ireland Pesticide Usage Survey has recorded limited use of neonicotinoids, largely as seed treatments for some arable and fodder crops and greenhouse grown brassica and lettuce. There has also been some minor use of neonicotinoids as a sprayed insecticide on strawberry crops and outdoor grown leafy brassica, carrot and parsnip. The five neonicotinoids authorised and used in Northern Ireland during the last 10 years were clothianidin, imidacloprid, thiamethoxam, thiacloprid and acetamiprid.All neonicotinoids recorded have been used within their authorisation period and on the crops allowed by the relevant conditions of use.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the full funding package was already secured when the Casement Park procurement process was brought forward by the then Department of Culture, Arts and Leisure on behalf of Ulster GAA.
answer_text: The Casement Park Project was brought forward as part of the Regional Stadia Programme and Sub-regional Stadia Programme for Soccer - The Executive endorsed plans for three regional stadia (Association Football, Gaelic Games and Rugby) in March 2011.  The Regional and Sub-regional stadia programmes were designated as Flagship Projects by the Executive and as such had the assurance of funding approval. This approval was in place in advance of the procurement process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her discussions with the UK Government on a new fiscal framework.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether the full funding package was already secured when the Casement Park procurement process was brought forward by the then Department of Culture, Arts and Leisure on behalf of Ulster GAA.
answer_text: In March 2011, the Executive endorsed an overarching funding package for the three stadia within the Regional Stadia Programme. Each Governing Body had to bid for project funding out of this funding package through the Full Business Case Process. Therefore, an overarching funding package had been secured when the Casement Park procurement process was brought forward by the then Department of Culture, Arts and Leisure in 2013.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Cross-Border Healthcare Directive.
answer_text: Following the success of the scheme I introduced in June 2021, I had hoped to introduce a new reimbursement scheme this year. I included a bid for funding for a new replacement, which could have treated over 4,000 patients during this year’s budget setting process. Unfortunately, under the 2024/25 Budget recently approved by the Executive, precisely zero additional funding has been provided for tackling our waiting lists.Unfortunately, instead of now being able to invest in such waiting list initiatives, my Department’s budget has been cut by over 2%. This is a deeply regrettable and concerning position.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether it is normal practice to delay the issue of tender documents in the public procurement process whilst funding or business case matters are being resolved.
answer_text: The Department of Finance, Construction and Procurement Delivery (CPD) includes two Centres of Procurement Expertise (CoPEs), Construction Division and Supplies and Services Division, which award contracts on behalf of Departments and their Arms-Length Bodies. CPD will proceed to issue tender documents following approval by the funding Department. Where a Department has approval to issue tender documents pending the necessary business case or funding approvals, this information will be included in the published documentation to notify prospective tenders of the potential funding uncertainty.I cannot confirm the practice in other CoPEs (i.e. NI Water, Education Authority, Health Estates, HSC Business Service Organisation Procurement and Logistics, Department for Infrastructure Transport and Road Asset Management, NI Housing Executive, Translink).

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the Speaker's Ruling on Assembly Questions on 12 March 2024, and pursuant to AQW 9584/22-27, to detail (i) how many Event Duration Monitors have been installed at Combined Sewer Overflows across NI Water’s wastewater network; and (ii) the percentage of the network covered by Event Duration Monitors, broken down by (a) River Basin Districts; and (b) Local Management Areas.
answer_text: NI Water is responsible for the operational delivery of its day to day activities which are independently regulated by the Utility Regulator.  NI Water has a dedicated resource for queries from elected representatives.  Therefore, as the issues you previously raised were operational matters for NI Water, the company is best placed to answer your questions. You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail NI Water's plans to prevent frequent raw sewage spills from the Wastewater Treatment Works at Clonoe, County Tyrone.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether it is normal practice to delay the issue of tender documents in the public procurement process whilst funding or business case matters are being resolved.
answer_text: My Department has put in place robust procedures which ensure that all procurement carried out is compliant with Northern Ireland Public Procurement Policy and the requirements of Managing Public Money NI.The issuing of tender documentation is subject to the relevant programme or project securing appropriate approval. Contracts are only awarded when the business case has been updated to reflect the outcome of the tender process and funding has been secured. Therefore, the approach to procurement is considered on a case-by-case basis.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what representations have been made by their Department to (i) the NIO; and (ii) the Secretary of State in relation to initiating the commencement order for Part 1 Section 4 of the Language and Identity (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 which repeals the ban of Irish in courts.
answer_text: The Executive Office is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act) for which it is responsible.The Department of Justice (DoJ) has responsibility for the administration of justice, including the Courts and Tribunals Service.  It is therefore a matter for DoJ to make representation to the Secretary of State regarding commencement of Part 1 Section 4 of the Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what progress her Department has made in bringing forward the necessary statutory provision for wills to be validly made in Irish, as committed under New Decade, New Approach.
answer_text: The relevant commitments under ‘New Decade, New Approach’ were to be sponsored and overseen by the First Minister and deputy First Minister. The Department of Finance is responsible for the law relating to wills.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether all Departments have appointed a departmental representative to the Cross-Departmental Working Group as part of the co-design process for the Irish Language Strategy; and (ii) to detail the grade of each official appointed.
answer_text: All departments have appointed a representative to the Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture and Irish Language Cross-Departmental Working Group with the exception of the Department of Health.Departments have provided representation on the Cross-Departmental Working Group at senior civil servant level, Grade 5.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what support is available for homeowners to help with the installation of solar panels.
answer_text: The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set out a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources in the north of Ireland by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity.Departmental officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects ensuring consumers pay a fair price for electricity produced locally.Options for microgeneration support, including domestic renewable generation such as solar panels, will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions.The Windsor framework enables the extension to the North of the VAT relief for energy-saving materials that was previously available only in Britain. That means that people here will also be able to apply zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials, such as solar panels.Independent advice on solar panels, heat pumps and other energy efficiency matters is available from organisations such as the Energy Saving Trust. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what support he will be making available to farmers who have been adversely affected by the recent persistent heavy rainfall due to its impact on crops and livestock.
answer_text: I am acutely aware that this year’s very wet and late Spring is presenting challenges for farmers operating in almost every sector within the industry. Although there is no adverse weather financial support currently available for farmers, I have written to the UK Government about the need for central government intervention and I intend to raise this for discussion at the next meeting of the Inter-Ministerial Group for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs. Meanwhile, my officials in the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) continue to monitor the situation very closely, maintaining contact with stakeholders and farmers across the range of sectors within the industry. CAFRE technical specialists are also providing advice to local farmers on the management of their farming operations in expectation of improving weather conditions in the coming days.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what assessment has been made of the level of drug and alcohol addiction, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Statistics from the Substance Misuse Database are published annually on the Department of Health’s website at: https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/drugs-statistics. This annual publication summarises information on people presenting to services with problem drug and/or alcohol use by recording clients first presenting to treatment services, as opposed to the number currently in treatment.The table below shows the last 4 years this information was available: 2019/202020/212021/222022/23Total drug and alcohol clients recorded on Substance Misuse Database3,7182,9203,0922,960Over the last number of years, Northern Ireland has been seeing increasing risk in relation to substance use related harm. In general, alcohol and drug related deaths have increased over the past decade. This has been exacerbated by rising polydrug misuse and an increase in the complexity of cases presenting for treatment, which has put additional strain on already stretched services.That is why work is continuing to implement Northern Ireland’s substance use strategy, Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery.  Given the nature of the issues, and the overlap with wider societal factors such as poverty and deprivation; homelessness; employment and economic development; mental health and trauma; paramilitarism, community relations and justice; and the legacy of the past, addressing this issue also requires a joined-up approach across the whole Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how many Event Duration Monitors have been installed at Combined Sewer Overflows across NI Water’s wastewater network; and (ii) the percentage of the network covered by Event Duration Monitors, broken down by (a) River Basin Districts; and (b) Local Management Areas.
answer_text: The issues you raised  are operational matters for NI Water.  You should therefore direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) when the schedule of compliance assessment sampling at wastewater treatment works will change from being pre-announced to being unannounced sampling; and (ii) to detail the reason for the delay.
answer_text: NIEA instructed NI Water to change the operator self-monitoring programme from an “announced” to “unannounced” programme in 2016. NIEA has asked for the “unannounced” sampling regime to be in place for the commencement of the next Price Control Period in 2027. NIEA recognises that significant investment will be required facilitate this change.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how much of the determined level of capital investment of £2.1 billion, required to maintain and enhance the existing water and wastewater treatment infrastructure for the 2021-2027 Price Control period, has been delivered to date.
answer_text: When compared to the funding profile for the first three years, NI Water has been fully funded, and has received an additional £90m over the funding level as recommended by the Utility Regulator in May 2021.                PC21 RequirementBudget allocationYear£m£m2021/22178.62222022/23251.2290.12023/24327.3337.4Total757.1849.5

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the circumstances when his Department would authorise the use of neonicotinoid pesticides on agricultural land.
answer_text: Pesticides, including neonicotinoid pesticides, are only granted authorisation following a rigorous assessment of safety, including risks to human health and the environment, including biodiversity. All approvals are also subject to periodic review.Acetamiprid is the only neonicotinoid approved as an active substance in the EU which can be included in pesticides authorised for the use in NI.  The usage of pesticide products containing acetamiprid and other, now banned neonicotinoids, by NI growers has been low. My Department’s policy on the authorisation of neonicotinoids for use on agricultural land will continue to be guided by science and a rigorous assessment of safety and any risks.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the determined level of capital investment of £2.1 billion, required to maintain and enhance the existing water and wastewater treatment infrastructure for the 2021-2027 Price Control period, is expected to be delivered in full by the end of the Price Control period.
answer_text: NI Water has been fully funded to PC21 levels for the first three years of the price control period.My Department is currently reviewing NI Water’s Operating Plan and Budget for 2024-2025.  Budgets for future years have not yet been agreed and therefore I have not yet taken decisions on future allocations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients are awaiting Speech and Language Therapy after a referral by a consultant, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The information requested is not held centrally by the Department; the data was provided by each Health and Social Care Trust.  Please find the Trust responses below, except Western HSCT who were unable to provide a response in the timeframe provided:TrustTotal patients waitingBelfast176Northern271South Eastern113Southern115

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what actions he is taking to increase the provision of Speech and Language Therapy in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: I am conscious of the vitally important role that Speech and Language Therapists play across health and social care in the management of complex speech, language, communication and swallowing problems.With demand for speech and language therapy services far exceeding capacity, increasing the provision of the service requires significant investment in the professional workforce. I remain committed to implementing the recommendations of the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029, which envisages increasing the number of undergraduate training places commissioned each year above the current funded levels by 12 to reach the recommendation of 40.A budget for Northern Ireland Departments has not yet been set for 2024/25 and accordingly I am unable to make any commitment to expand commissioned places at this time.  I will continue to make the case for this essential investment in our future workforce; however I expect that the 2024/25 budget will be extremely challenging.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following comments by the Speaker at the conclusion of Question Time on 12 March 2024, (i) how many people are on the housing waiting list; (ii) how many of these people are living in housing stress; and (iii) to detail the average waiting time for housing allocations, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as per Annex A which has been laid in the library due to the volume of information. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, following comments by the Speaker at the conclusion of Question Time on 12 March 2024, to detail any prospective areas and sites being considered for social housing in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“In areas of housing need where there is a consistent shortage of new build activity, the Housing Executive’s Regional Place Shaping teams undertake site identification studies which are shared with nominated housing associations. In 2022/23, site identification studies were prepared for Coalisland and Donaghmore and to date, one of the sites identified in Coalisland is on-site and two sites have been included in the 3-year Social Housing Development Programme (See Table 1 below).Further studies have been programmed for both Cookstown and Magherafelt for the next 3 years (2024/25 – 2026/27). Please note that programmed schemes can be lost or slip to future programme years for a variety of reasons e.g. relating to delays in acquiring sites and/or failure to secure Planning Approval. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps he will take to establish pay parity between further education lecturers and school teachers.
answer_text: Further Education lecturers’ pay, as well as the terms and conditions of employment, are matters for the Further Education colleges as employers. I am however committed to improving pay for Further Education lecturers. 		I am giving full attention to the concerns raised by Further Education lecturers following the announcement of the school teachers’ pay agreement. My officials continue to work closely with Further Education colleges, as, employers, to enable further options regarding a pay offer to be explored within the resources available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he will introduce a formal link with pay for further education lecturers and school teachers.
answer_text: Further Education lecturers’ pay, as well as the terms and conditions of employment, are matters for the Further Education colleges as employers. However, I am committed to ensuring that the pay provided to FE lecturers fully recognises the range of teaching and learning which they deliver to our students and their hard work and dedication in doing so. 		I regard the issue of closing the pay gap with teachers as a priority issue. I have therefore asked for my Officials to explore ways to ensure this pay gap is addressed as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to restore the financial support available to local councils through the Rate Support Grant to the level in place before the collapse of the Executive in February 2022.
answer_text: I will consider the funding for councils, including Rates Support Grant, in the context of my Department's overall budget priorities, if any further funding becomes available for the current financial year.My Department has no budget settlement beyond 2023/24.  Funding requirements for 2024/25 will be considered against competing priorities and within my overall Departmental budget position once the Executive sets a 2024/25 Budget. I am therefore unable to confirm any funding at this stage for councils for the next financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the financial support provided by his Department to students in further education, as a result of the cost of living crisis.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any support Invest NI has provided to companies involved in the manufacture of armaments for use by the Israeli Defence Forces in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI has not provided any support to companies involved in the manufacture of armaments for use by the Israeli Defence Forces within the last 5 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, following the judgement in the case on the death of Sean Brown, for her assessment of the coroner's call for a public inquiry.
answer_text: The Coroner, Mr Justice Kinney, has decided that the inquest cannot proceed due to the Public Interest Immunity (PII) issues involved. He has stated his intention to write to the Northern Ireland Secretary of State Chris Heaton-Harris, asking the Government to establish a public inquiry into Sean Brown’s death. Mr Justice Kinney has stated that he expects a reply to his request within four weeks.I deeply regret the further pain and anguish caused to Sean’s family, in waiting for justice for such a long time, and having embarked on the inquest process, only to discover that it cannot be completed. While it is for the UK Government to decide how to respond to the call for a public enquiry, I fully support the Brown family in their desire to find out the truth about what happened to Sean.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what support is available for businesses to help with the installation of solar panels.
answer_text: My department is working in partnership with Invest NI to deliver a new phase of its Energy & Resource Efficiency (ERE) Programme for Business. The Programme will include a new Energy Efficiency Capital Grant Scheme (EECG) which will be open to the wider business base. The EECG will include grant support for energy efficiency measures along with low carbon and renewable technologies, all aimed at reducing emissions and helping businesses take control of their energy use by lowering their energy demand. The wider ERE Programme includes support in the form of Technical Consultancy, Resource Matching Through Industrial Symbiosis and Resource Efficiency Finance which are already in operation and the EECG will launch in the coming weeks.

answer_text: Seven schools in Mid Ulster have been announced to advance in planning under the Major Capital Works Programme and two schools have been announced under the Fresh Start Agreement.  Details of the schools and current project stage are detailed below. Major Capital ProgrammeProject StageEdendork Primary School, DungannonTechnical design and pre-tender estimate (RIBA Stage 4).Gaelscoil Uí Néill, CoalislandPre-construction, Integrated Supply Team has been appointed.Holy Trinity College, CookstownIn construction, completion is anticipated in Spring 2026. St Conor’s College, ColeraineConcept design (RIBA Stage 2).St Patrick’s College, MagheraA business case to identify the preferred option is being finalised. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to address the backlog of resurfacing the roads in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, including those in Mid Ulster, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective.I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many roads require (i) resurfacing; and (ii) partial resurfacing in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effectiveI have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network. The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Mid Ulster District Council area was presented to Council members and can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/mid-ulster-district-council-report-2023The resurfacing programme along with other programmes for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Council in due course.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how many of his Department's employees have Jubilee House, Ballykelly, as their permanent place of work.
answer_text: There are 406 staff headquartered in Jubilee House, Ballykelly as at 31 January 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6022/22-27, whether his Department will work with local stakeholders to identify sources of funding to repair the surface of the runway at the Ardboe Aerodrome.
answer_text: The aerodrome road is privately owned and not under the Department’s responsibility.However, my Department will work with local stakeholders to help them explore alternative sources of funding. I am aware that officials have agreed to meet with residents and interested parties to discuss the status of the aerodrome and answer any questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what funding he has secured to finance a pay and grading review for Education Authority support staff.
answer_text: Following the agreement of the financial package from HM Treasury the Finance Minister allocated additional Resource DEL funding of £296.8m to the Department of Education.  This did not include any funding in respect of the Pay & Grading review for education sector support staff.  However, the Finance Minister has advised that this issue can be revisited as part of the Budget 2024-25 process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when he plans to commence the further roll-out of 20mph speed limit schemes at schools in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with DfI’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070The program to date has been rolled out in tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total length, in miles, of (i) rural roads in Mid Ulster; and (ii) roads that will be resurfaced in Mid Ulster in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Following the reform of local government in 2014, the Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the new council areas. All data relating to road lengths and works carried out are recorded by the Department in kilometres. Figure 2 of the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics 2022-23 details the length of adopted road by road type for all local government districts. A link to the relevant web page is included below.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2022-23The programme of resurfacing work planned for 2024/25 across Mid Ulster will be published in the DfI Roads Annual Report to Mid Ulster Council which will be produced once budgets for the coming year are finalised.
