question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the implementation of the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 (the Act) was brought through Westminster by the Northern Ireland Office.TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of this Act for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies namely: The Office of Identity and Cultural Expression;The Irish Language Commissioner; andThe Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition.We have made our initial decisions regarding the recruitment process and further advice is being developed to enable the competitions to be launched. We will keep Members updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the upgrade to the (i) Ballyronan; (ii) Bellaghy; (iii) Derrychrin; (iv) Killygonlan; (v) Pomeroy; and (vi) Stewartstown Wastewater Treatment Works.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should, therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of work orders issued under the current Interim Term Contracts for Asphalt Resurfacing contracts, between now and the end of the financial year, in each DfI Roads division.
answer_text: Details of the number of work orders to be issued by my Department under the current Interim Term Contracts for Asphalt Resurfacing contracts, between now and the end of the financial year are not currently available. Work is currently ongoing on the planning and delivery of schemes associated with the additional funding which I have recently announced.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on upgrading street lights in the Mid Ulster constituency to new LED street lighting.
answer_text: My Department’s programme to replace its sodium street light with energy efficient LED units is very well advanced in the Mid Ulster constituency.  Subject to the provision of the necessary funding to my Department in 2024/2025, all remaining sodium street lighting luminaire will be replaced with LED units by September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his plans to address issues with the water infrastructure.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) what steps have been taken in relation to the repeal of the Administration of Justice (Language) Act (Ireland) 1737, which bans Irish in courts; and (ii) what preparatory work has been undertaken by the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service in anticipation of the repeal, in line with the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: (i)	The commencement of provisions within the Identity and Language Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 is a matter for the Northern Ireland Office. The Department of Justice is unable to comment on any plans by the NIO to commence the relevant provisions.(ii)	The Department has contracts in place for the provision of face to face and telephone interpreting services, and for the translation of written materials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the Department’s timescale to adopt The Mills, Coalisland.
answer_text: My Department have contacted the Surety for the bonds, Ulster Bank Ltd. and informed them that we intend drawing down the bond to have the development completed to an adoptable standard.  My Department plans to commence works to bring the road infrastructure up to standard when a contractor resource becomes available in the new financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the progress made in the co-design process for the Irish language Strategy; and (ii) a timeline for completion of the remainder of that process, up to and including the publication of a new draft-strategy, to ensure Section 28(d) of the Northern Ireland Act is fulfilled in full.
answer_text: To date, the co-design process has involved extensive engagement with sectoral organisations, community focus groups, delivery bodies, and the public sector, with work progressing firstly through an Expert Advisory Panel and then a Co-Design Group. Both the Panel and the Group were composed of community, voluntary and academic Irish language stakeholders and experts to ensure a broad range of experience. Owing to the cross-cutting nature of the strategy, a Cross-Departmental Working Group is considering the Co-Design Group’s proposed action plan.  It will then be for Executive colleagues to agree next steps. A draft strategy will issue for public consultation following Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for a breakdown of the individual amounts paid to his Department by way of compensatory measures for pollution incidents, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: In line with DAERA’s enforcement policy and in relation to water pollution incidents involving a fish kill, my Department will seek to recover appropriate compensatory costs. The individual amounts received by my Department from orders of compensatory awards relating to fish kill events over the last five calendar years are as follows:Year20192020202120222023IndividualAmountNoneNoneNone£2,836.00£2,140.72£4,281.44£6,291.90£661.62£1,733.94£56.52£3,525.34Total£9,258.16£12,269.32Where a magistrate orders the payment of compensation in the case of a fish kill, the awards are used by DAERA to fund projects to improve fish stocks where possible in the river impacted by the pollution.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps have been taken to fast track the public appointment process for the Irish language Commissioner and other Commissioners, as set out in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 amends the Northern Ireland Act 1998 and provides the statutory basis for the establishment of 3 new bodies, namely: the Office of Identity and Cultural Expression; the Irish Language Commissioner; and the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition. TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of this Act for which it is responsible, including the establishment of the new bodies. This will be in-line with our decisions. Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are being taken to improve the road surface of Main Street, Toomebridge.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads un a reasonable condition. While there are many roads within every council area, including Antrim & Newtownabbey, that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.  Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the Antrim area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Antrim & Newtownabbey which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/annual-report-antrim-and-newtownabbey-borough-council-2023Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.  Plans for future years will be developed based on budget availability, delivery capacity and the condition of the network as a whole.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether Invest NI is providing any support to companies involved in the manufacture of armaments for use by the Israeli Defence Forces.
answer_text: Invest NI is not providing any support to companies involved in the manufacture of armaments for use by the Israeli Defence Forces.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps have been taken by his Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: My Department has a well-established provision for inclusion of national and cultural principles in relation to its functions and in the schools’ curriculum.The Department’s language policy sets out the administrative services which people can expect from the Department in Irish, Ulster Scots or any other language and informs those who wish to use these languages how they may communicate with us.  This policy can be found on the Department’s website at DE language policy | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk).  The Department is also represented on the Languages Strategy Cross Departmental Group led by the Department of Communities.In addition, my Department already has a statutory duty to deliver Irish Medium education with Irish being an established language subject in the curriculum.The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 at Part 1(5) amended the Education (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 to insert a duty on the Department to encourage and facilitate the use and understanding of Ulster Scots in the education system.  My Department will facilitate this, and will also work with the Irish Language Commissioner and the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition when appointed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps have been taken by his Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The provisions relating to national and cultural identity and language contained within the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 have yet to be commenced.  My department is therefore not in a position to give effect to these provisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps have been taken by his Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 does not place any specific additional responsibility on the Department of Health at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what steps have been taken by her Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: Commencement of provisions within The Identity and Language Act (NI) 2022 is a matter for the Northern Ireland Office.  The Department of Justice has taken a number of steps to support individuals who wish to engage with the Department in Irish or Ulster Scots.  For example, arrangements are in place to deal with correspondence received in any language and documents can be made available in alternative languages on request.  My Department has also adopted a tri-lingual logo which is used on official documents, Departmental headed paper, advertisements and digital platforms.  A senior official from my Department is also a member of a Cross-Departmental Working Group that is chaired by an official within the Department for Communities and which was established to support the development of an Irish Language Strategy and an Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps have been taken by his Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The provisions relating to national and cultural identity, the Irish language and the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British Tradition contained within the Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022 are not yet in force and require regulations to be made by the Secretary of State, by way of Statutory Instrument. My department is therefore not in a position to give effect to these provisions.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps have been taken by his Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: In line with the commitment in NDNA, an official from my Department sits on the Department for Communities’ cross departmental working group set up to develop an Irish Language Strategy and an Ulster-Scots Language, Heritage and Culture Strategy.The DfE logo on headed paper, digital platforms and internal and external websites is bilingual.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how he intends to assure parents, in the absence of inspections by the Education and Training Inspectorate, that specialist units in mainstream schools meet the needs of children with Special Educational Needs.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has overall responsibility for special education provision in Northern Ireland and its Special Education Strategic Area Plan (2022-2027) (SESAP) aims to ensure that pupils with a statement of Special Educational Needs (SEN) have access to a placement that best meets their needs, through a continuum of education provision which includes mainstream schools, specialist provision in mainstream schools and special schools.My department’s policy has a presumption of mainstream and just over 84% of children with SEND are educated in mainstream classes, 5% are educated in specialist provision in mainstream and just over 10% are education in special schools.Specialist provision classes in mainstream schools form an important part of this continuum of provision, providing smaller classes that offer targeted support within an inclusive mainstream school environment.The number of specialist provisions in mainstream schools has increased significantly in recent years to meet the increasing demand for specialist education placements.Children’s needs are assessed and recommended for specialist education provision by the EA’s Educational Psychology Service.  Evaluation of that placement takes place through an annual review process, or on an ad hoc basis if required, to ensure it continues to meet the child’s needs.    The EA’s ‘Framework for Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools’ sets out a range of criteria and indicators which schools must meet in establishing a Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools (SPiMS) class, to ensure children’s needs will be met and they have a quality educational experience in an inclusive environment.  Schools also have designated EA SEN Link Officers and specialist provision classes are supported by its Specialist Setting Support Team, which offers a wide range of training, advice, consultation and strategies to schools.  This service engages with parents to seek their views on their child’s progress, consistency of approach between home and school and understanding of how to support their child’s needs. In terms of inspection, for some time now, the ongoing industrial action short of strike has made it difficult for the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) to provide necessary assurances of the quality of education in all of our schools, not just specialist provisions.Adapting to challenges, the ETI has moved to monitoring visits by district inspectors (DIs) and thematic evaluations which provided a sample of evidence from schools, allowing compilation of evidence-based first-hand practice.Specialist provisions have been an important feature of our mainstream schools for decades and, in 2019, the ETI published a report on SPiMS containing 30 case studies of effective practice and a report in 2020 on the curricular challenges and approaches adopted by specialist provisions following the pandemic.  The ETI will also publish its findings of a specific evaluation into specialist provision

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister what steps have been taken by their Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: TEO is taking forward the necessary arrangements to implement the provisions of this Act for which it is responsible.This will be in-line with our decisions.Members will be kept informed on progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps have been taken by his Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: My Department’s provision for the Act includes longstanding policy guides for both the Irish Language and Ulster Scots. Correspondence received in any language is replied to in that language. On request, forms and documents are translated into any language, including Irish and Ulster Scots.  The Department’s corporate identity is trilingual. This is reflected, for example, on departmental official documents, publications, letter headed paper, the digital platforms; internal and external, signs, vehicles, plant and advertising.The Department is represented by a senior official on the Languages Strategies Cross Departmental Working.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what steps have been taken by her Department to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: The duties and powers in the Identity and Language (NI) Act 2022 relevant to the Department of Finance have yet to be commenced by Parliament. My Department will prepare for the commencement of relevant provisions as part of our commitment to equality, diversity and inclusion.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what steps have been taken by the Commission to give effect to the provisions in The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.
answer_text: In respect of Part 7A (National and Cultural Identity) of the Northern Ireland Act 1998 (the 1998 Act), the Assembly Commission’s Equality Scheme already contains detailed provision under which individuals may choose, affirm, maintain and develop their national and cultural identity; and express and celebrate that identity in a manner that takes account of the sensitivities of those with different national and cultural identities. The Assembly Commission is currently considering any additional steps which may be necessary to comply with the duties in Part 7A of the 1998 Act.In respect of Part 7B of the 1998 Act (The Irish Language), the Assembly Commission is awaiting the publication of standards of best practice relating to the use of the Irish language by public authorities.  It will be for the Irish Language Commissioner to publish these standards, having consulted with such persons as the Commissioner considers appropriate.  These standards must be approved by the First Minister and deputy First Minister acting jointly. The Assembly Commission will take any additional steps necessary to comply with these standards when they are published.In respect of Part 7C of the 1998 Act (The Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition), the Assembly Commission is awaiting the publication of guidance on developing and encouraging the relevant language, arts and literature (including facilitating the use of Ulster Scots in the provision of services to the public or a section of the public in Northern Ireland).  It will be for the Commissioner for the Ulster Scots and the Ulster British tradition to publish such guidance, having consulted with such persons as the Commissioner considers appropriate.  The Assembly Commission will take any additional steps necessary to comply with this guidance when it is published.Finally, you will wish to note that, for the purposes of The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022, the Assembly Commission is a “public authority”. The institution of the Assembly does not have a corporate identity and is not a “public authority” for the purposes of The Identity and Language (Northern Ireland) Act 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures he plans to put in place to improve the provision of mental health services across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: In June 2021 I published the Mental Health Strategy 2021 – 2031 which set out the vision and future direction for mental health services in Northern Ireland over the next decade. Action 31 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to the development of a Regional Mental Health Service (RMHS), operating across the five Trusts. It will provide regional professional leadership that will be responsible for consistency and improvement in service delivery and development. Drawing on the actions in the Mental Health Strategy, three core attributes of a high quality Regional Mental Health Service were identified: A regionally consistent service in terms of models, service delivery and service structures;  The establishment of care networks, building capacity and ensuring regionally agreed evidence-based approaches with appropriate agreed care pathways; andLocally based service delivery working across primary and secondary mental health care with the full integration of the community and voluntary sector.I announced the launch of the RMHS in October 2022.  Since then, considerable work has been progressed, in discussion with key stakeholders, to develop and agree a 5-year implementation plan for the Regional Service and to recruit key posts. The Head of the RMHS Collaborative Board has recently been recruited and is expected to take up post on 1 March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has for a review of mental health services across all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: In June 2021 I launched the Mental Health Strategy 2021 – 2031 which set out the vision and future direction for mental health services in Northern Ireland over the next decade. Action 31 of the Mental Health Strategy commits to the development of a Regional Mental Health Service, operating across the five Trusts. It will provide regional professional leadership that will be responsible for consistency and improvement in service delivery and development. Drawing on the actions in the Mental Health Strategy, three core attributes of a high quality Regional Mental Health Service (RMHS) were identified: A regionally consistent service in terms of models, service delivery and service structures;  The establishment of care networks, building capacity and ensuring regionally agreed evidence-based approaches with appropriate agreed care pathways; andLocally based service delivery working across primary and secondary mental health care with the full integration of the community and voluntary sector.I announced the launch of the RMHS in October 2022.  Since then, considerable work has been progressed, in discussion with key stakeholders, to develop and agree a 5-year implementation plan for the Regional Service and to recruit key posts. The Head of the RMHS Collaborative Board has recently been recruited and is expected to take up post on 1 March 2024.  Once established, the Collaborative Board will have responsibility for the oversight of mental health services development and delivery, including what further reviews may be required across Trusts.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures are being taken to improve access to consultant-led neurology services in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Investment has been allocated to Northern Trust to increase the consultant workforce by two consultant neurologists. This would have increased overall capacity to 2.5 whole-time equivalent (WTE) Consultant Neurologists. Despite two recent recruitment attempts, these posts have not yet been filled.  Current services are available via outreach consultant-led outpatient clinics, provided by Belfast Trust consultants. In addition, elements of the service are delivered by specialist nurses. The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has been engaging with Northern Trust and other HSC Trusts to explore options to secure additional support for the Northern Trust. My department is leading on the Regional Review of Neurology Services which is tasked with identifying the optimum configuration of neurology services for the next 10-15 years. Several workstreams have been established in priority areas including workforce, long-term and complex conditions, and care coordination with the aim to improve access.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of a new mental health facility at the Antrim Area Hospital site.
answer_text: The new mental health facility at Antrim Area Hospital is currently in design stage. The new purpose-built Birch Hill Centre for Mental Health will be the single inpatient mental health facility for the Northern Health & Social Care Trust. The Trust anticipates the new facility will be open for patients by winter 2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of funding difficulties facing community pharmacy owners, whether he will confirm whether the emergency measures provided in November and December will be extended from now and into 2024/25.
answer_text: I very much appreciate the extremely important work that community pharmacies deliver for our population.I have received representation from pharmacy contractors and from their representative body, Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI). An urgent meeting was held recently with representatives of the community pharmacy sector, CPNI to consider these matters further.  I have asked my Department to address some of the pressures that pharmacies are facing within what is available and affordable. I encouraged CPNI to continue to work with Department officials to seek ways to ensure the pharmacy service can develop in line with our shared strategic objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures will be taken to repair the condition of the runway at Ardboe Aerodrome to improve access for local residents and businesses.
answer_text: The first 288 metres of the old aerodrome road network, from the Kilmascally Road to the Ardboe Business Park, is adopted by the Department. This section of road has had significant strengthening and resurfacing works carried out by my Department within the last five years. The remainder of the aerodrome road is privately owned and not under the Department’s responsibility.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many applicants have been on the housing waiting list for (i) 1-2 years; (ii) 2-3 years; (iii) 3-4 years; (iv) 4-5 years; and (v) more than 5 years, broken down by constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures will be taken to improve the condition of Battery Road, from Drumconvis Road to the junction with Drumenny Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many people are on the housing waiting list; (ii) how many of these people are living in housing stress; and (iii) what is the average waiting time for housing allocations, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: The number of people on the social housing waiting list, those in housing stress and the average waiting time for allocations, broken down by constituency are operational matters for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the social housing waiting list in Mid Ulster, broken down for each of the last four years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what consideration he has given to the reintroduction of the Happy Healthy Minds programme.
answer_text: The Healthy Happy Minds Pilot concluded on 31 March 2023.  An evaluation of the pilot has been carried out and I will assess its findings as I seek to better support the emotional health and wellbeing of learners.  The Independent Counselling Service for Schools (ICSS) remains available to post-primary age pupils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people awaiting the implementation of all, or part, of their domiciliary care package in each Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As at January 2024 the number of clients assessed and waiting for a FULL package of Domiciliary care were;HSCT NameTOTAL Clients waiting for full packageBelfast                                    323 Northern                                 1,157 South Eastern                                    194 Southern                                    623 Western                                    260 REGION                                 2,557 Source: Monthly returns supplied by HSC Trusts to PSSI, SPPGAs at January 2024 the number of clients assessed and waiting for a PARTIAL package of Domiciliary care were;HSCT NameTOTAL Clients waiting for partial packageBelfast414Northern553South Eastern106Southern71Western398REGION1,542Source: Monthly returns supplied by HSC Trusts to PSSI, SPPGDomiciliary care services continue to experience significant pressures across the region, and there are a range of factors currently impacting upon delivery of this service; this includes significant retention issues in regard to domiciliary care staff.Where delivery of care packages has been negatively impacted due to challenges regarding capacity, Trusts have in place mitigation measures to try to ensure services continue to be provided.My Department remains committed to the provision of a high quality domiciliary care service, and will continue to work alongside Trusts and independent sector providers to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what plans there are for an all-island animal cruelty register.
answer_text: Policy responsibility for the welfare of animals sits with the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA).  Responsibility for measures to combat animal cruelty offences is, therefore, a matter for that Department. Any decision to develop and implement a register of those convicted of animal welfare offences on an all-island basis is also a policy matter for that Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are being taken by his Department to repair and reinstate the overgrown footpath along Grove Road, Swatragh.
answer_text: Due to the budgetary constraints faced by my Department it has been necessary to operate a limited road maintenance service for multiple years.  This means that only the highest priority defects are repaired.  This is not a position that I want to be in and I hope that critical services and infrastructure such as our roads are provided with the investment we need. As such, I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.I would encourage the Member to continue to report any road defects through the NI Direct online portal at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/report-pothole-or-other-surface-defect which will send it directly into the workflow for the relevant officials and ensure that they can be addressed effectively. I have been advised that the work to the footpath at Grove Road is anticipated to be undertaken in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans there are for an all-island animal cruelty register.
answer_text: An all-Ireland register of animal welfare offenders can only be created if such a register was considered feasible in both jurisdictions. No register exists in the Republic of Ireland nor are there any plans to create one there.My Department has considered this matter extensively in the past and concluded that the creation of a register is untenable. The situation remains that a clear evidential need has not been identified, the costs of establishing and maintaining a register would be significant and the data protection implications involved are prohibitive. However, if Assembly colleagues or stakeholders advocating for a register can provide additional evidence to support the establishment and operation of a register, which could include where any such register has worked effectively elsewhere, my officials will consider any such detail received. 		I am conscious that an objective of any such register would be to ensure, as much as possible, that those banned from keeping animals do not obtain animals again. 		My Department remains available to discuss with stakeholders other ways of working together to minimise the possibility of these instances occurring.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps the NI Environment Agency is taking to prevent the occurence of blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: I have no doubt that the member is aware that there are no quick fixes to the occurrence and probable reccurrence of blue green algal blooms in Lough Neagh. This is a problem that has been decades in the making and it could take decades to restore.	A DAERA-led cross departmental Water Quality Steering Group has been tasked with producing a report of evidence-informed recommendations on the actions required to secure longer term improvements in water quality. Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) officials have submitted recommendations which will form part of the that report.An Interagency Monitoring group has also been established and NIEA is currently developing protocols for monitoring and response should there be a repeat of the blooms in subsequent years. NIEA officials have also contributed to the Department’s CAFRE’s recent farm training events on slurry management.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps NI Water is taking to prevent the occurrence of blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his department is taking to prevent the occurrence of blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) is leading on a review of the blue green algae blooms that occurred in Lough Neagh during Spring/Summer 2023.  My Department, alongside representation from NI Water and other key stakeholders are supporting DAERA as part of this review process, which is considering the range of actions required to address and prevent future occurrence of the algae.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the development of an Executive Childcare Strategy.
answer_text: Since my appointment as Education Minister, I have made the development and implementation of the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy a priority and while I have stated that our ambition should not be limited, it is important that we take time to carefully consider our options and to secure the necessary funding. To that end I plan to meet parents, providers, and those with an interest in the strategy, to hear firsthand how best we can meet the developmental needs of young children whilst helping to reduce the cost of childcare.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what steps his department is taking to prevent the occurence of blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh.
answer_text: There are no quick fixes to the occurrence and probable re-occurrence of blue green algal (BGA) blooms at Lough Neagh. This is a problem that has been decades in the making and it could take decades to resolve.However, I am pleased to confirm that action on the ground has already commenced. For example, my Department, through its College for Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE), is delivering programmes for farmers / land managers at various levels targeted towards Lough Neagh catchment areas. Work on a Small Business Research Initiative scheme to identify viable technical solutions to deal with the immediate effects of BGA within the Lough will also commence shortly. Looking to longer term solutions, my Department has established a cross- departmental Water Quality Steering Group (WQSG) which is currently finalising a draft report with a set of evidenced-based recommendations to tackle BGA and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland. Although I expect to bring these recommendations to the Executive for discussion and consideration shortly, I would stress that Lough Neagh cannot be seen in isolation from the natural environment more generally and we cannot tackle its problems in isolation either. I look forward to working with the Executive, the Assembly and indeed all stakeholders in finding solutions to address this complex issue.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, pursuant to AQW 5405/22-27, whether the formal inspections that are taking place include a health and safety audit.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has been monitoring problems with damp ingress related to the roof project and all affected areas are kept under regular review. Following a worsening of the problems in some areas earlier this year, monthly inspections now take place and a report is maintained with a formal record of any deterioration that has taken place since the last assessment, details of any further problems that arise and actions that have been required as a result.These monthly inspections include an assessment of any health and safety risks arising from the damp ingress problems and where necessary, mitigating measures are introduced.  As noted in AQW 5405/22-27, the Assembly Commission has issued proceedings against two parties for negligence, breach of statutory duty and breach of contract.  Expert witnesses employed by the Assembly Commission have assessed all of the affected areas and proposals have been submitted for further investigatory works to take place to help determine the source of the damp ingress problem(s) so that remedial repairs can be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether a health and safety audit has been conducted as a result of water ingress at Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission has been monitoring problems with damp ingress related to the roof project and all affected areas are kept under regular review. Following a worsening of the problems in some areas earlier this year, monthly inspections now take place and a report is maintained with a formal record of any deterioration that has taken place since the last assessment, details of any further problems that arise and actions that have been required as a result.These monthly inspections include an assessment of any health and safety risks arising from the damp ingress problems and where necessary, mitigating measures are introduced.  As noted in AQW 5405/22-27, the Assembly Commission has issued proceedings against two parties for negligence, breach of statutory duty and breach of contract.  Expert witnesses employed by the Assembly Commission have assessed all of the affected areas and proposals have been submitted for further investigatory works to take place to help determine the source of the damp ingress problem(s) so that remedial repairs can be undertaken.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what measures are being taken to address the damp ingress at Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is aware of problems with damp ingress related to the roof project and all affected areas are being kept under constant review including formal inspections at least twice per month.  Proposals have been submitted for further investigatory works to take place to locate the damp ingress problem(s) and enable repairs to be undertaken.In respect of issues related to the roof project, the Assembly Commission has issued proceedings against two parties for negligence, breach of statutory duty and breach of contract.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what health and safety audit has been conducted as a result of damp ingress at Parliament Buildings.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission is aware of problems with damp ingress related to the roof project and all affected areas are being kept under constant review including formal inspections at least twice per month.  When necessary, ceiling and wall-mounted electrical services will be temporarily disconnected for health and safety reasons.   In respect of issues related to the roof project, the Assembly Commission has issued proceedings against two parties for negligence, breach of statutory duty and breach of contract.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission, in relation to departmental and agency events held in Parliament Buildings, (i) what coordination mechanism is in place in preparation for events; (ii) what post-event method of evaluation is used for these events; and (iii) what evaluations have been carried out on events to date this year.
answer_text: For external events held in Parliament Buildings, it is the role of the event organiser, whether a department, public agency or other organisation, to determine the format and content of their event, as well as undertaking activities such as issuing invites.  The Assembly Commission’s Events Office leads in ensuring that the necessary practical arrangements identified by the organiser in the booking process are provided.Following any organiser’s event, the Events Office will invite feedback on the support provided in Parliament Buildings and review this to ensure continued excellence in service provision. However, as the Assembly Commission has no role in determining the purpose or content of an organiser’s event, the Events Office, or other Assembly Commission officials, has no role in evaluating such events.On occasion, events organised by internal Assembly stakeholders, such as the Speaker or committees, may involve contributions or support from departments or public agencies.  The nature of the planning, coordination and evaluation processes will vary depending on the nature of the event and the support provided.  However, these are matters for the relevant Assembly stakeholder organising the event and the Assembly Commission has no role in determining the arrangements for these events.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures are being taken by the Department to immediately address the funding shortfall in Community Pharmacy.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many times utility providers have been instructed by his Department to repair the road surface in Mid Ulster, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Utility companies are responsible for the permanent reinstatement of roads to an agreed specification. When utility companies do not reinstate roads in a satisfactory manner, the Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  The table below details failed inspections by year in each of the last 5 years ;YEARNumber of failed inspections2017/181272018/19922019/201352020/211252021/22130The reinstatement defects were notified to the utility company responsible for the necessary remedial works. Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to list the resurfacing works that will be completed in Mid Ulster in this financial year.
answer_text: The Western Divisional Roads Manager, Daniel Healy, met with Mid Ulster Council on 29 September 2022 to present the 2021-2022 Annual Report for DfI Roads Western Division.  The report is available in the Assembly library and can also be accessed via the following link: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/annual-report-mid-ulster-district-council-2022 Section 2.6, pages 21 to 23, of the report lists the proposed resurfacing works for 2022-2023.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Western Division at dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many people in Mid Ulster are currently not in training, education or employment.
answer_text: The Labour Force Survey provides estimates of the number of people not in education, employment or training (NEET).The estimated NEET number (people aged 16 to 64) for Mid Ulster for April to June 2022 was 12,000, which is also available on the NISRA website in Table 1 at the following link: https://www.nisra.gov.uk/system/files/statistics/TblLFS2091.XLSX.Estimates from the Labour Force Survey are subject to an associated sampling error that decreases as the sample size increases.  It is the nature of sampling variability that the smaller the group whose size is being estimated, the less precise that estimate will be. Estimates for Assembly Area will therefore have a higher sampling variability than estimates for the region as a whole.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what his Department is doing to address the lack of NHS places in dental practices in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: Access to routine dental care continues to be significantly affected by the COVID-19 pandemic, which has caused reduced activity and therefore reduced capacity in dental practices. In order to increase activity in General Dental Services, I introduced the Rebuilding Support Scheme (RSS), which rewards dentists based on treatments being provided.  The RSS has seen an increase in activity levels of around 16.5% over the first 5 months of 2022/23, and support will continue until the end of the financial year.Any General Dental Practitioner signed up to the RSS is required to treat patients based on clinical need, regardless of their registration status. This is to ensure that those patients most in need are able to obtain a dental appointment.In addition, any patient who has an urgent or emergency dental need, is able to make use of an Emergency Dental Clinic during weekends or Public Holidays. There the patient will be triaged remotely, and appropriate advice given or treatment arranged.To further help those in most need, on 3 October 2022, my Department commenced a scheme throughout Northern Ireland to provide access to unregistered patients who are experiencing an emergency or urgent dental condition.Support being provided to dental practices will remain under ongoing review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how long the Basic Payment Scheme to farmers is guaranteed for.
answer_text: As confirmed in the Future Agricultural Policy Decisions for Northern Ireland which I announced on 24 March 2022, a Farm Sustainability Payment (FSP) will be introduced.  FSP will initially be the main replacement for the Basic Payment Scheme (BPS).   My officials are continuing to develop the framework of FSP and the delivery processes required. Over time, other agricultural support schemes will be introduced to better meet local needs. These schemes will be designed to help farmers develop their businesses, no matter where they farm to become more productive, profitable and resilient, and to maximise the sustainable returns from the assets at their disposal.  At present, I have not finalised the timetable for these changes.The budget for BPS/FSP and other agricultural support schemes has been guaranteed by HM Treasury for the next three financial years up to 2024-2025 and is ring fenced on an annual basis.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what urgent measures are being taken to establish funding to replace the loss of the European Social Fund Programme.
answer_text: The British Governments centralised approach to the replacement of EU Funds has crystallised a significant loss of spending power for the Executive.  The SPF (Shared Prosperity Fund) has been introduced by the British Government as a replacement for the European Social Fund (ESF). However, this fund raises significant concerns; it has not been subjected to screening under our equalities legislation, it will operate directly from Whitehall bypassing Executive Departments, it will deliver too little money, it risks duplication of provision and it lacks alignment to our local priorities.I remain committed to securing as much funding for here as possible, and will not stand in the way of any funds flowing to the North. I will continue to press this issue with the British Government and my officials will continue to engage with their Whitehall counterparts with the aim of finding a solution which affords our Executive Departments an appropriate role.Unfortunately the absence of the Executive has limited my ability to take action in relation to this unacceptable and underfunded approach by the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what urgent measures are being taken to resolve issues with the NI Supply Teacher Register to ensure full and prompt payment to teachers.
answer_text: The majority of the issues encountered in respect of the introduction of the new NI Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) have now been resolved.  Following the delay in go-live from 22 to 29 August and performance issues in the first few days of operation, the Education Authority (EA) put in place significant additional staffing resources to manually input thousands of retrospective bookings for days worked in August whilst the system was not fully operational and to provide assistance to those experiencing difficulties in using the new solution.  The Department also extended the payroll deadline to provide additional time for the bookings to be inputted, accepted by teachers and approved by schools.The EA have been working with the system supplier to resolve bugs that have been identified at various stages of the process or by certain groups of users.  Additional functionality and changes to system logic have also been implemented based on user feedback.The NISTR is now working effectively to enable schools to independently source and book qualified, pre-employment checked teachers to provide vital cover.  Once the teacher has attended and the engagement is complete, the school can sign-off the bookings on the system the next day unless the booking extends into the following calendar month in which case the booking can be partially signed-off for the days in the payroll period ended within a three day window between the 1st and 3rd working days of the new month.All approved bookings are transferred in a file from the NISTR to the Department’s payroll team after the 3rd working day.   The Department and the EA are working together intensively to ensure that the file contains data that aligns with the needs of the payroll system and that salary payment for all approved bookings for September days (and any outstanding days from August) will be paid through the relevant payroll this month. Additionally, the NISTR Helpline will reopen from 11am on 7 October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when an Energy Bills Support Scheme will be available for businesses.
answer_text: A UK-wide Energy Bill Relief Scheme for businesses has recently been announced by the UK Government.  It will apply from 1st October for an initial period of 6 months before being reviewed. BEIS is working with the energy suppliers here on delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why the decision was made to withdraw funding for the new leisure, health and well-being hub at Gortgonis Centre, Coalisland; and (ii) who was consulted regarding this decision.
answer_text: A decision has not been made to withdraw funding for the Gortgonis Centre in Coalisland as funding for the project was never agreed.  The Gortgonis project has been subject to ongoing discussions between Departmental and Council officials from 2018 however to date the Council have not provided the Department for Communities with a final business case. The potential to secure funding is being urgently explored and work will continue with the council to progress this much needed project for the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Waste Management Strategy.
answer_text: Work has begun to develop a new Waste Management Strategy to deliver DAERA’s commitment in the draft Environment Strategy for Northern Ireland to publish a new Waste Management Strategy by 2023. This includes implementation of new recycling and landfill reduction targets and incorporating new policy developments since 2013, such as a deposit return scheme for cans and bottles and extended producer responsibly schemes for priority waste streams like electrical goods and textiles. Most recently, the department organised a well-attended stakeholder workshop on 29 September 2022 at CAFRE’s Greenmount campus, with the presentations delivered available on the DAERA website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

answer_text: Information provided by the Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts on the number of domiciliary care workers currently directly employed by them, and details of agency staff use, is provided in the table below.  The figures provided relate only to domiciliary care workers and do not include other staff groups required to run the domiciliary care service, for example administrative staff, social workers, or occupational therapists. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many additional staff would be required to be employed by each Health and Social Care Trust to fully clear the current waiting lists for domiciliary care packages.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on how many additional staff would be required to clear the current waiting list for domiciliary care packages. Care is provided by a mix of Trusts and independent providers and whilst Trusts endeavour to provide care packages where they are required, ultimately there are a range of factors which can impact upon this delivery.  Staffing is only one element of this. Work has been ongoing to deliver additional funding to the independent sector, both in-year and on a recurrent basis, to try to alleviate the ongoing pressures on the system. The £23m funding package which I approved in November 2021, was intended to stabilise and enhance capacity within the independent domiciliary care and wider social care sector. This additional funding secured an increase in the hours of domiciliary care delivered by the independent sector and helped to stabilise the care sector during a very difficult period. Despite a very challenging financial context I have ensured that funding was secured to ensure this uplift was carried through to the current year and has been made recurrent. The number of people waiting for a domiciliary care package fluctuates on an ongoing basis and although the overall demand for care packages continues to increase, I am confident that the funding to date has assisted in stabilising the independent sector, and has increased capacity of providers to deliver care packages.Officials from my Department continue to engage with Trust colleagues and independent sector providers on an ongoing basis, to maintain and support service provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the schools in Mid Ulster that have a 20mph speed limit in place; and (ii) his Department's plans to put such schemes in place at other school locations in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: I am pleased to confirm that my Department has implemented part-time 20mph speed limit measures which will benefit the following 18 schools within the Mid Ulster area:Orritor PS;Phoenix PS;Lissan PS;St Malachy’s PS;Ballylifford PS;Woods PS;St Trea’s PS;Spires PS;St Pius X;St Eoghan’s, Moneyneany;St Brigid’s, Gulladuff;Ampertain PS;Crossroads PS;Augher Central;Aughnacloy PS;St Joseph’s, Galbally;Edendork PS; andAughnamullen PS.My Department has invested £2million over the past two financial years which will see the introduction of part-time 20mph speed limits that will benefit 215 schools across the North.  When all of the tranche 2 sites are fully operational and have had sufficient time to bed in, my Department intends to carry out a review of this work to determine if a further tranche is justified. Until this review is carried out I am not in a position to commit to the installation of part-time 20mph speed limits at any more schools.Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Western Division at: dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients currently waiting for the delivery of all, or part, of a domiciliary care package, broken down by (i) length of wait; and (ii) Trust area.
To ask the Minister for the Economy what financial support does his Department provide to domestic properties to assist the adoption of renewable energy sources such as solar and wind.
answer_text: There is currently no financial support available from my Department for homeowners who wish to install solar panels or wind turbines.The Energy Strategy for Northern Ireland, The Path to Net Zero Energy (December 2021), sets the policy direction for increasing renewable energy generation to achieve a target of at least 80% of electricity consumed in Northern Ireland coming from a diverse range of renewable sources by 2030 (target established by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022). Essential to meeting this target will be establishing a support scheme(s) for renewable electricity and, under the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2022, officials are currently considering appropriate options with a view to public consultation by the end of 2022.   In the interim, homeowners may wish to seek independent advice on domestic renewables and other energy matters from organisations such as the Energy Saving Trust, the Centre for Sustainable Energy or Citizen’s Advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the Ammonia Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has developed a comprehensive Ammonia Strategy which I am currently finalising for publication. The draft Ammonia Strategy will propose a series of farm measures to reduce ammonia, conservation actions to improve the condition of habitats, and a revised operational protocol for the assessment of air pollution effects. The ammonia reduction element of the Ammonia Strategy will build on the significant support provided for low emissions slurry spreading equipment in Tier 1 of the Farm Business Improvement Scheme (FBIS)-Capital.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the development of the Sexual Orientation Strategy.
answer_text: My Department continues to lead a significant programme of work in the co-design of the Executive’s four social inclusion strategies including the Sexual Orientation (LGBTQI+) Strategy. 		The drafting of the Strategy is ongoing in readiness for presentation to an incoming Executive. I am committed to the delivery of a meaningful strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Biodiversity Strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Biodiversity Strategy for Northern Ireland is being developed through a co-design process of engagement with a wide range of key stakeholders. The department has engaged with government departments, councils and other public bodies. Organisations representing marine and land users as well as environmental non-governmental organisations have been engaged over the past few months. The initial feedback from these discussions will inform two larger stakeholder events planned for October, where we will work with stakeholders to create and refine Northern Ireland specific targets that will align with the outcomes required by the Convention of Biological Diversity.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how much has the decision to appeal the industrial tribunal finding in the case of Dr Tamara Bronckaers cost taxpayers to date; and (ii) how much is it expected to cost once completed.
answer_text: The latest estimate of legal costs on this case between my Department and Dr Tamara Bronckaers is £309k.  This covers both the costs of the Industrial Tribunal and those of the Court of Appeal as Departmental Solicitor’s Office billed these under one case file as both elements were so closely linked.  This is in addition to the Industrial Tribunal Settlement to Dr Bronckaers of £1,250k.Legal work continues on this case as it has not been discharged and final costs will only be determined at that time.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what measures he is considering introducing to address the gender bias in the provision of apprenticeships.
answer_text: There is no gender bias in the provision of apprenticeships, which are open to anyone who meets the eligibility and entry requirements set down by the employer and training provider.  The level of female participation in apprenticeship provision is complex, linking into the progression pathways that are chosen and also reflects traditional gender balance in various occupational areas. This is outside the control of my Department’s apprenticeship programmes.  Nevertheless, it is disappointing that the most recently published statistics show that only 26% of new starts to the Apprenticeships NI programme at October 2021 were female, although this increases to 31% in the most recent statistics for new starts to the Higher Level Apprenticeships programme.   I remain committed to removing the age eligibility criteria in the ApprenticeshipsNI programme to make more apprenticeships available to those aged 25 and above.  This will provide more options for women looking to re-enter the workforce.The annual Northern Ireland Apprenticeship Week in February also offers the opportunity for apprenticeships to be showcased to everyone.  Events for 2023 are still being finalised however this provides an opportunity to highlight apprenticeships opportunities to all genders as does the Department’s twice yearly apprenticeship advertising campaign.  I also note that the 2022 Apprentice of the Year was a female undertaking an engineering apprenticeship at level 3.More widely, my Department established a Women in STEM Steering Group in 2020 to examine the significant and persistent underrepresentation of women at every stage of the core STEM skills pipeline and establish how women can best be supported to fully participate in the 10X Economic Vision.  The group has published research on the barriers to Northern Ireland achieving a diverse and inclusive workforce and has worked with industry, academia and the public sector to develop a shared vision and action plan for Women in STEM, published in March 2022.This action plan and supplementary policy insights paper present a pathway and recommendations to addressing the underrepresentation of women in the STEM skills pipeline, including apprenticeships.  With continued commitment from stakeholders across industry, academia and government, the activities underpinning the action plan will create an environment that encourages and supports more young people, and young women in particular to reach their full STEM potential through whatever academic pathway they choose. My Department is currently delivering two pilot projects funded through the Economic Recovery Action Plan.  Delivered by the Royal Academy of Engineering and STEM Learning in partnership with colleges and industry, both pilots aim to inspire young people from participating schools and inform them of all the academic pathways and local employment opportunities available to them.  Informed by the Women in STEM action plan and endorsed by the Women in STEM steer

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the current timeline for publication of the Childcare Strategy consultation.
answer_text: I made a Ministerial Statement to Assembly members on 7 September 2022 providing an update on the work to develop an Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  In it, I confirmed my intention to bring costed options for Executive consideration in March 2023.Ultimately, the publication of a draft Strategy for consultation will be subject to Executive consideration and approval, including a commitment to additional and sustained funding for childcare.You can view the full Ministerial Statement at the following link https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/written-ministerial-statement-update-executive-childcare-strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps she is taking to resolve the pay dispute between the Northern Ireland Housing Executive and the Northern Ireland Public Service Alliance and Unite.
answer_text: I have no input into the negotiations or agreement of the pay awards for Housing Executive Workers.I will be required to approve the pay remits in line with DoF guidance when they have been submitted by the Housing Executive on the basis of an agreed settlement.I would urge those involved in negotiations to work urgently to find an agreed resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what provision can be made within Farm Sustainability Payments for holdings of less than five hectares where established intense farm activity takes place.
answer_text: Earlier this year, following a comprehensive consultation exercise, I announced policy decisions which will provide a more targeted approach to help farmers to develop their businesses, no matter where they farm, to become more productive, profitable and resilient, and to maximise the sustainable returns from the assets at their disposal.  A key mechanism in achieving this will be the creation of a Farm Sustainability Payment (FSP) to replace the Basic Payment Scheme.I also made the decision to restrict eligibility for FSP to those businesses claiming 5 or more hectares of eligible land.  No provision can be made within FSP for those businesses with less than 5 ha of eligible land irrespective of the intensity of farming activity being undertaken.  However, such businesses may retain eligibility for other support measures that will be launched in the future including the Farming with Nature Package.  All land managers with 3 ha or more of eligible agricultural land who meet the scheme requirements will be eligible to participate in this measure.  This includes land under conacre and common land.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline the immediate measures her Department is considering to mitigate the impact of the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.	My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether she plans to re-open the Emergency Fuel Payment Scheme; and (ii) for a timefrome when it will re-open.
answer_text: The cost of living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.I am committed that my Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what percentage of PSNI vehicles are powered by electricity; and (ii) what plans has she to introduce an all-electric fleet.
answer_text: The details requested are an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB).  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NIPB.You will, therefore, wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she intends to introduce mandatory installation of solar panels to all newly constructed social dwellings.
answer_text: Increasing the energy efficiency of homes is a vital way of mitigating the effects of climate change, reducing fuel poverty and improving health. Therefore the Department is supporting sustainable and energy efficient design beyond the statutory minimum by allowing Housing Associations to claim a supplementary ‘Energy Efficiency Multiplier’ for new dwellings which exceed the minimum standards currently required under the Building Regulations 2012.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many emergency MOT appointments were made in each month between January 2022 and August 2022, broken down by MOT test centre.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) continues to assist all customers who request an urgent appointment to enable them to tax their vehicle, however, it does not hold the information you have requested.The DVA publishes headline statistics on the number of vehicle, driver and theory tests conducted on a monthly basis, which are available on the Department’s website at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-and-vehicle-agency-monthly-tests-conducted-statistics.For the latest information on MOTs I would ask you to encourage your constituents to visit NI Direct at Book, view, change or cancel an MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect. This information is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to DVA customers.  The advice confirms if a customer gets to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what provisions are currently in place in Mid Ulster for young children with special needs who require a nursery place.
answer_text: All schools are required to make provision for children and young people with a range of special educational needs (SEN).However, for those children whose Statement of SEN requires either a special school or specialist provision class in mainstream school, the following provisions are currently in place in Mid Ulster: St Patrick’s PS, Annaghmore – Specialist Provision (Learning – SLD)Kilronan School, MagherafeltSperrinview School, Dungannon

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what steps his Department is taking to promote Mid Ulster as part of Northern Ireland’s tourism product.
answer_text: Tourism NI continues to promote Northern Ireland (NI) as a leading tourism and short break destination since the lifting of travel restrictions, in May 2021 in Northern Ireland, and June 2021 in the Republic of Ireland (ROI). An integral part of Tourism NI’s Recovery Strategy, Tourism NI’s positive consumer focused campaigns highlight the many ways people can embrace Northern Ireland’s giant spirit, including Mid Ulster. The campaigns promote Northern Ireland as a destination, highlighting specific attractions and experiences, which resonate with key target visitors. Tourism Northern Ireland promoted Mid Ulster area, in the Northern Ireland and Republic of Ireland markets in their Summer 2022 campaign, and it will be featured again in their Autumn 2022 Campaign.Tourism Northern Ireland’s 2022 campaign activity, in both Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland, featured the Mid-Ulster and surrounding area, including experiences such as Lough Neagh Tours, Seamus Heaney HomePlace and Woodlab Distillery.Tourism businesses in Mid Ulster are also invited to apply for funding support from the Tourism Northern Ireland Co-operative Marketing fund, to support their own promotional activity in the Northern Ireland and Republic of Ireland markets.Tourism NI also works closely with all 11 local authorities. Since Spring 2021, Tourism NI has provided a further £71,750 in funding support for marketing activity undertaken by Mid Ulster District Council.Working in partnership with Tourism Ireland, so far in 2022, Tourism NI has hosted 11 international media and film crews visits within the Mid Ulster area, from the Great Britain, American and Austrian markets. Their itineraries included experiences such as Ardtara Country House, Heaney Homeplace, Lough Neagh Stories and St Patrick’s Chair.Tourism Northern Ireland continues to work closely with the Visitor Information Centre (VIC) network, who collaborate with their tourism industry to upload information on the local area to Tourism Northern Ireland’s consumer website, discovernorthernireland.com.As part of a Market Led Product Development Programme in 2021-22, Tourism NI provided £265k to Mid Ulster Council to undertake a number of destination enhancement activities including delivery of a Living History Programme and delivery of 2 new light installations at OM Dark Sky Visitor Centre, Davagh.  In addition, as part of an Experience Development funding programme in 2020-21, Tourism NI provided a combined total of approx £450k of capital funding to a number of tourism businesses in the Mid Ulster area.  Furthermore, Tourism NI are working closely with Council partners, including Mid Ulster council, to progress all of the potential tourism projects within the £252m Mid South West Growth Deal.  Tourism NI has previously assisted with tour operator testing of project concepts in the Mid Ulster area and is in the process of arranging extensive testing with consumers in key international markets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 1972/22-27, what consideration her Department has given to providing funding for the removal of asbestos in properties previously owned by the Housing Executive; and how many properties previously owned by the Housing Executive are on the asbestos register, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: The absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget is having a negative impact across many areas of Departmental planning. The asbestos register is for current Housing Executive properties only.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many people in Mid Ulster are currently experiencing homelessness.
answer_text: The latest published figures on the number of people in Mid-Ulster classified as homeless can be found in the Homelessness Bulletin at: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-july-december-2021

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what steps she plans to take to increase the level of provision of special educational needs nursery places in Mid Ulster.
answer_text: The Department aims to provide a funded pre-school place for every child in their immediate pre-school year whose parents want it. The Education Authority's (EA) Pre-School Education Group (PEG) is responsible for securing sufficient provision in local areas to achieve this aim.   Where a child has a statement of special educational needs (SEN) their admission to an education setting is dealt with via a separate process. The placement of children for whom the EA maintains a statement of (SEN) is arranged through the Statutory Assessment and Review Service (SARS). The SEN placements process is managed to ensure each child receives a suitable placement that will appropriately support and cater to their individual needs. This is managed on a case by case basis. Securing appropriate provision for children with statements of SEN remains a key priority for me and is being taken forward by the EA supported by the Department and key partners, to ensure children are placed appropriately and in line with their needs.
